plan.executiveSummary: This is a thoughtfully-architected homelab SOC (Wazuh + TheHive/Cortex + OpenCTI + Grafana on single-node k3s, three MCP bridges, a SvelteKit dashboard) that is currently undeployable as written and would mislead anyone who trusts its docs. The single most important truth: the documented one-command path (`deploy-stack.sh all`) cannot stand up the stack. The Wazuh Manager and Dashboard are never applied; OpenSearch's `opensearch-certs` / `wazuh-dashboard-certs` secrets, the `soc-auth-secret` basic-auth secret, the `soc-tls-cert` ingress cert, and the declarative `secrets.yaml` are all referenced by manifests but created by nothing. Functionality: the flagship Wazuh MCP tools (`list-alerts`, `get-alert`, `get-vulnerabilities`) query Server-API endpoints that do not exist in Wazuh 4.x/4.8+ — they 404 on every call — and the OpenCTI `tactic` filter is silently a no-op. Security: TLS verification is disabled in several places (dashboard `NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0` process-wide, Grafana datasource `tlsSkipVerify`, Wazuh Dashboard/TheHive/Cortex OpenSearch connections), and the /api/* SOC proxy has no auth, exposing full agent inventory to any LAN client. Cleanliness: a large maintenance trap exists where every service has BOTH a dead raw `deployment.yaml` AND the Helm path that deploy actually uses, so the manifests an auditor reads are not what runs, and they have diverged (versions, TLS posture). Design: there are zero tests, zero CI, no pinned chart versions, no Pod Security Standards, and pervasive documentation drift (NetworkPolicy counts, OpenCTI 6.6.6 vs 6.9.5, unsubstantiated "audited against ISO 27001" claims). The good news: the bones are sound, the codebase is small, the fixes are mostly mechanical, and the project already documents the right patterns (CA-cert mounts, default-deny NetworkPolicies) it just failed to wire everywhere. Most criticals are an afternoon of cert/secret scripting plus four missing `kubectl apply` lines.
plan.overallGrade: C- — strong architecture and security intent, but undeployable via its own documented path and undermined by missing tests/CI, dead duplicate manifests, and disabled TLS; nearly all blockers are mechanical, low-effort fixes.

criticalAndHigh.title: deploy-stack.sh never deploys the Wazuh Manager StatefulSet or Dashboard — the core SIEM is silently absent
criticalAndHigh.why: deploy_wazuh() applies only ossec/rules/decoders ConfigMaps + the agent DaemonSet, prints 'Wazuh Helm chart may need manual setup', and never runs kubectl apply for k8s/wazuh/manager/deployment.yaml or dashboard/deployment.yaml. Agents deploy with no manager to enroll to and no dashboard. test-flow.sh:71-72 asserts these pods are Running, so the documented smoke test cannot pass. The product's central component does not exist after a clean deploy.
criticalAndHigh.fix: Add `kubectl apply -f` for wazuh/manager/deployment.yaml, wazuh/dashboard/deployment.yaml, and wazuh/dashboard/configmap-dashboard.yaml to deploy_wazuh(); remove the misleading Helm note; add a `kubectl wait` on wazuh-manager before the agents; fix teardown.sh to also delete wazuh/dashboard/.

criticalAndHigh.title: opensearch-certs / wazuh-dashboard-certs secrets and opensearch-ca-cert ConfigMap are referenced by core pods but never created — OpenSearch/Wazuh never start
criticalAndHigh.why: OpenSearch (helm install --wait), the Wazuh Manager, and the Wazuh Dashboard mount the secrets opensearch-certs and wazuh-dashboard-certs (esnode/kirk/dashboard/root-ca PEMs). No script, manifest, or cert-manager Certificate creates them (verified: NO CREATORS IN SCRIPTS). The opensearch-ca-cert ConfigMap ships a literal REPLACE_WITH placeholder and is never applied. A non-existent secret volume leaves pods unschedulable, so OpenSearch (and everything depending on it) never comes up.
criticalAndHigh.fix: Add a scripts/generate-certs.sh (openssl or cert-manager Certificates) that produces root-ca.pem + esnode/kirk/dashboard PEMs with the SANs/DNs in opensearch.yaml (CN=opensearch-node and CN=admin, O=MatchBox, C=US), then kubectl create the opensearch-certs (shared+wazuh ns) and wazuh-dashboard-certs secrets and populate the opensearch-ca-cert ConfigMap. Wire it into deploy_shared()/deploy_wazuh() before the helm/apply steps.

criticalAndHigh.title: secrets.yaml is never applied; only soc-opencti-secrets is created at deploy time — every Helm chart/StatefulSet waits on missing secrets
criticalAndHigh.why: validate_secrets() only greps secrets.yaml for the CHANGE_ME marker and exits; nothing runs kubectl apply -f secrets.yaml (verified). OpenSearch/Redis/RabbitMQ/MinIO reference existingSecret soc-shared-secrets; Wazuh/TheHive/Grafana reference soc-wazuh/thehive/grafana-secrets — none created. Additionally soc-opencti-secrets has two competing sources of truth (declarative block in secrets.yaml AND the imperative kubectl create, which substitutes the literal 'placeholder' for missing TI keys).
criticalAndHigh.fix: Add a deploy_secrets step (kubectl apply -f shared/secrets.yaml, which carries per-resource namespaces) before deploy_shared; pick ONE owner for soc-opencti-secrets and remove the duplicate; make missing TI keys fail-fast or omit the env rather than seeding 'placeholder'.

criticalAndHigh.title: Traefik soc-auth-secret (the only org-wide auth layer) is never created — every dashboard route fails closed
criticalAndHigh.why: All five IngressRoutes attach the soc-basic-auth Middleware referencing secret soc-auth-secret, created by nothing but an inline comment (verified NO CREATORS IN SCRIPTS). Traefik basicAuth with a missing secret fails the route. This is also the sole thing gating Grafana's anonymous Viewer access, so a misconfig here removes the only authentication in front of all SOC dashboards.
criticalAndHigh.fix: In deploy_ingress(), before applying routes, generate htpasswd creds from an env var and `kubectl create secret generic soc-auth-secret -n kube-system --from-literal=users=...` (idempotent get-or-create); fail fast if the password env is unset; document the env in a runbook.

criticalAndHigh.title: Ingress TLS is non-functional: cert-manager/self-signed-issuer never installed, soc-tls-cert never created
criticalAndHigh.why: deploy_ingress() installs Traefik and applies traefik-routes.yaml (TLSStore + every route reference secretName soc-tls-cert) but never installs cert-manager, never applies self-signed-issuer.yaml, and never creates soc-tls-cert. The referenced scripts/generate-tls-cert.sh does not exist. CLAUDE.md falsely claims the ClusterIssuer auto-generates the cert. Result: HTTPS dashboards have no certificate after the documented deploy.
criticalAndHigh.fix: Pick one path in deploy_ingress(): either install cert-manager + apply self-signed-issuer.yaml and wait for the Certificate Ready, or openssl + `kubectl create secret tls soc-tls-cert -n kube-system`. Gate route apply on the secret existing. Fix CLAUDE.md and remove/realize the dangling generate-tls-cert.sh reference.

criticalAndHigh.title: Wazuh MCP flagship tools query non-existent Server-API endpoints (/alerts, /alerts/{id}, /vulnerability/{agent}) — 404 on every call
criticalAndHigh.why: list-alerts/get-alert hit /alerts on the port-55000 Server API, which has no such resource — alerts live only in the indexer (wazuh-alerts-* on 9200 via _search). get-vulnerabilities hits the removed legacy /vulnerability/{agent}; in 4.8+ (deployment is 4.14.3) CVE inventory moved to the wazuh-states-vulnerabilities-* index. These three are the primary tools an analyst would use and they cannot work against the configured endpoint.
criticalAndHigh.fix: Re-point alert + vulnerability retrieval at the Wazuh Indexer/OpenSearch API (separate base URL + auth, POST _search), or remove the tools until indexer access is wired. Add at least one contract/integration test against a real instance so a non-existent endpoint is caught pre-ship.

criticalAndHigh.title: Dashboard disables TLS verification process-wide (NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0) for a credential-bearing channel
criticalAndHigh.why: Set at module load and in the runtime .env (adapter-node prod server reads it), this turns off cert/hostname validation for EVERY outbound fetch in the process. The Wazuh proxy sends Basic-auth admin creds over https://localhost:55000 with no scoped CA, so the credentialed channel is MITM-exposed. CHANGELOG claims this was already fixed via CA certs; it was not.
criticalAndHigh.fix: Remove the global flag from vite.config.ts and both env files. Trust the SOC CA specifically: set NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS to the Wazuh CA, or pass a scoped undici Agent with the pinned CA only on the Wazuh fetches. Never disable verification globally.

criticalAndHigh.title: No authentication on /api/* SOC proxy routes — full agent inventory exposed to any LAN client
criticalAndHigh.why: No hooks.server.ts, no locals.user, no origin/CSRF check anywhere. Any client reaching the adapter-node port can GET /api/wazuh and receive every agent's id/name/status/OS/version/IP/registration plus alert/case/indicator/connector counts — reconnaissance-grade SOC data with no login.
criticalAndHigh.fix: Add src/hooks.server.ts enforcing auth on /api/* (session cookie, Traefik forward-auth, or at minimum a shared bearer token), and/or bind adapter-node to localhost behind the authenticated Traefik ingress. Add same-origin checks before any future state-changing route.

criticalAndHigh.title: Agent→Manager traffic blocked: NetworkPolicy allows only pod sources but agents use hostNetwork
criticalAndHigh.why: allow-wazuh-manager ingress on 1514/1515/55000 is from podSelector:{}, but the agent DaemonSet runs hostNetwork:true so connections originate from the node host IP, which a podSelector does not match. With default-deny-ingress active, agent enrollment (1515) and log shipping (1514) are dropped under k3s's enforced NetworkPolicy controller — so even once the manager is deployed, no telemetry flows.
criticalAndHigh.fix: Add an ingress rule admitting the node source (ipBlock for node/pod CIDR) on 1514/1515, or drop hostNetwork if hostPath mounts suffice. Validate from an agent pod to the manager on the live cluster.

criticalAndHigh.title: Agent registration keys (client.keys) stored on emptyDir — wiped on every manager restart
criticalAndHigh.why: /var/ossec/etc is an emptyDir; only /var/ossec/data is PVC-backed. On any pod restart the initContainer re-seeds etc from image defaults, losing client.keys, and <purge>yes</purge> drops historical agent identities — every agent must re-enroll and event history is disrupted.
criticalAndHigh.fix: Persist /var/ossec/etc (or at least client.keys) on a PVC, with an initContainer that seeds image defaults only when empty and then overlays the ConfigMap ossec.conf.

criticalAndHigh.title: Wazuh agent DaemonSet runs privileged + hostNetwork + hostPID with full host-root mount
criticalAndHigh.why: privileged:true + hostPID + hostPath / mounted is effectively root on the node — the maximum-privilege posture and the largest blast radius in the cluster; a compromise of the agent image equals host root. Partly inherent to FIM/rootcheck, but the breadth (all of / vs specific dirs) and privileged (vs scoped caps) are reducible.
criticalAndHigh.fix: Drop privileged in favor of capabilities.drop:[ALL] + only the caps FIM/rootcheck need (e.g. DAC_READ_SEARCH); add seccompProfile RuntimeDefault; narrow the hostPath from / to the inspected dirs; re-evaluate hostPID. Test FIM/rootcheck still function before rollout.

criticalAndHigh.title: INDEXER/API/DASHBOARD password secretKeyRefs marked optional:true — pods start fail-open with blank credentials
criticalAndHigh.why: All four credential secretKeyRefs use optional:true, so a missing secret/key leaves the var unset and the container boots with an empty password against a security-enabled OpenSearch/Wazuh API instead of failing fast — masking misconfiguration (especially given the secret-creation gaps above) and risking blank-cred auth.
criticalAndHigh.fix: Remove optional:true from all four refs so kubelet hits CreateContainerConfigError when creds are absent (fail-closed). Add a deploy check that soc-shared/soc-wazuh secrets exist before deploying wazuh workloads.

criticalAndHigh.title: OpenCTI get-attack-patterns silently ignores the tactic filter (never implemented)
criticalAndHigh.why: Tool is advertised as 'filtered by tactic' but the only use of tactic is `search: search || tactic || null` — coalesced into free-text search and dropped entirely whenever search is also set. No killChainPhases/x_mitre FilterGroup is applied, so callers get silently wrong results for a documented capability.
criticalAndHigh.fix: Add a FilterGroup on killChainPhases.phase_name passed via $filters (the pattern already used in search-indicators) and pass search/tactic as independent args; or correct the tool description to match actual behavior.

criticalAndHigh.title: Helm-deployed OpenCTI/TheHive/Cortex connect to TLS-only OpenSearch over http:// and omit credentials — connectivity broken on the real deploy path
criticalAndHigh.why: deploy-stack.sh installs all three via Helm using these values, which point at http://opensearch...:9200, but opensearch.yaml sets plugins.security.ssl.http.enabled:true (HTTPS-only) and the values omit ES credentials. So storage init fails. The correct https+CA+creds config lives only in the dead raw deployment.yaml files that are never applied. (Cortex's raw manifest is ALSO http+no-auth, contrary to one finding's claim.)
criticalAndHigh.fix: On the canonical Helm path, set https://...:9200, mount the OpenSearch CA (opensearch-ca-cert ConfigMap in each namespace), and supply ES username/password from soc-shared-secrets. Then delete the dead raw manifests so one config governs.

criticalAndHigh.title: OpenCTI Helm values set ADMIN_TOKEN to empty and never inject it from the secret — worker/connectors cannot authenticate
criticalAndHigh.why: OPENCTI_ADMIN_TOKEN is "" and extraEnvs injects everything EXCEPT admin-token, while the worker/connectors use the fixed admin-token from soc-opencti-secrets. Under the Helm path the platform comes up with an empty/auto-generated token mismatched from the workers/connectors, breaking ingestion.
criticalAndHigh.fix: Add an extraEnvs entry sourcing the admin token from soc-opencti-secrets/admin-token (verify the correct chart key, likely APP__ADMIN__TOKEN), and ensure platform+worker+connectors share one token; or clearly mark helm-values.yaml non-functional if raw is canonical.

criticalAndHigh.title: Grafana OpenSearch datasource credentials (opensearch-grafana-auth / OPENSEARCH_PASSWORD) never created — Wazuh dashboards fail auth
criticalAndHigh.why: envFromSecrets references opensearch-grafana-auth and the datasource uses $__env{OPENSEARCH_PASSWORD}, but no script/manifest creates that secret or sets the var (it appears only in this file). The OpenSearch datasource authenticates with an empty password and fails — the core Wazuh alert visualization is dead.
criticalAndHigh.fix: Create opensearch-grafana-auth with key OPENSEARCH_PASSWORD in deploy_monitoring() (derive from soc-shared-secrets/opensearch-password), or point envFromSecrets at soc-shared-secrets and map the existing key.

criticalAndHigh.title: Stale on-disk Wazuh MCP build + shared package has no dist and isn't installed — fresh build/runtime of any MCP server is broken
criticalAndHigh.why: settings.json runs dist/index.js, which is stale (inlines fetchWithRetry/formatResponse, predating the @matchbox/mcp-shared refactor in src). shared/ declares main:dist/index.js but has no dist/ and is not installed in any consumer; `tsc --noEmit` in wazuh-mcp fails TS2307 Cannot find module '@matchbox/mcp-shared'. No workspace/build-order mechanism exists, so a clean npm install + build of any server fails.
criticalAndHigh.fix: Adopt npm workspaces (root package.json) or tsc -b project references so shared builds first; build+install shared so node_modules/@matchbox/mcp-shared resolves; rebuild all consumers' dist from current src; add a CI build gate so dist never diverges from src.

criticalAndHigh.title: Two conflicting RabbitMQ definitions; dead raw StatefulSet references a non-existent ConfigMap
criticalAndHigh.why: rabbitmq-deployment.yaml ('replaces Bitnami chart', official image) is never applied; deploy uses bitnami/rabbitmq via rabbitmq.yaml. The two disagree (Erlang cookie + peer-discovery plugin vs neither), and the dead StatefulSet mounts configMap rabbitmq-config that is defined nowhere — it would stick in ContainerCreating if ever applied. Two sources of truth for one service is a correctness landmine.
criticalAndHigh.fix: Delete rabbitmq-deployment.yaml (Bitnami chart is the wired path), which also removes the dangling rabbitmq-config reference; record the decision in CHANGELOG.

criticalAndHigh.title: Dead raw deployment.yaml manifests for TheHive/Cortex/OpenCTI contradict the Helm deploy path and have diverged
criticalAndHigh.why: deploy-stack.sh deploys all three exclusively via Helm; the raw deployment.yaml files (and rabbitmq-deployment.yaml) are referenced nowhere and diverge from the Helm values in security-relevant ways (raw TheHive https+SSL_VERIFY=false vs Helm plain http; raw OpenCTI pins 6.6.6 vs Helm pins none; TheHive raw comment falsely claims 'no public Helm chart' while the script uses strangebee/thehive). An auditor reads manifests that do not run.
criticalAndHigh.fix: Make Helm canonical and delete the four dead raw manifests (thehive, cortex, opencti, rabbitmq deployment.yaml), or wire them in and drop Helm — pick one source of truth per component. Remove the stale 'no public Helm chart' comment.

criticalAndHigh.title: Dual/triple deployment implementations across components — no single source of truth (architectural)
criticalAndHigh.why: OpenCTI, TheHive, Cortex, and RabbitMQ each ship BOTH a raw manifest AND a Helm values file, with deploy using Helm and ignoring the raw set; the raw set has drifted in image versions and TLS posture. (Redis is NOT duplicated — only a Helm values file exists.) This is the root structural cause behind multiple of the cleanliness/security divergence findings.
criticalAndHigh.fix: Standardize on one strategy. 2026 best practice: a single Kustomize base/overlays tree or a Helm umbrella chart, deployed via Argo CD/Flux; minimally, delete the parallel raw set and keep Helm.

criticalAndHigh.title: OpenCTI core pinned to 6.6.6 while connectors and docs say 6.9.5 (version skew breaks connector registration)
criticalAndHigh.why: Platform/worker are 6.6.6 (raw, dead manifest) while all four connectors are 6.9.5 and README/CHANGELOG advertise 6.9.5; the active Helm values pin no platform tag at all so the deployed version floats with chart appVersion. OpenCTI requires connector pycti major.minor to match the platform, so a 6.9 connector against a 6.6 platform typically fails GraphQL schema validation on registration.
criticalAndHigh.fix: Pin platform AND worker image.tag in helm-values.yaml to one minor version, align all four connectors and README/CHANGELOG to it, and add a comment tying connector version to platform version so they upgrade together.

criticalAndHigh.title: No CI/CD at all — .github has only templates, no workflows
criticalAndHigh.why: Nothing lints bash, validates k8s manifests, scans for secrets, or builds the 4 TypeScript packages. The build-breaking shared-package refactor and the wrong-endpoint MCP bugs are exactly the class of defect a typecheck/contract test or manifest validation would catch — and the absence of CI is what let them ship. The PR template asks reviewers to manually confirm builds pass, but nothing enforces it.
criticalAndHigh.fix: Add .github/workflows/ci.yml on pull_request/push: shellcheck scripts/*.sh; kubeconform over k8s/**; gitleaks secret scan; matrix `npm ci && npm run build` per MCP package (build shared first) plus svelte-check on the dashboard. Pin actions to full commit SHAs; make them required status checks.

remediationGroups.theme: Wire TLS/CA trust everywhere instead of disabling verification (security)
remediationGroups.rationale: The project already adopted the correct CA-mount pattern for some components but left verification OFF in several others; these are all the same fix (trust the SOC self-signed CA, never disable). Batch them so the CA material generated for the cert criticals is consumed consistently. Includes: Grafana datasource tlsSkipVerify:true; Wazuh Dashboard verificationMode:none; TheHive TH_DB_ES_SSL_VERIFY=false; Cortex http://OpenSearch; OpenCTI/TheHive/Cortex missing CA on Helm path; the three MCP servers' bare fetch with no CA (Wazuh/TheHive/OpenCTI); Wazuh MCP startup probe relying on the global bypass.
findingTitles: Grafana OpenSearch datasource skips TLS verification (tlsSkipVerify: true)
Dashboard→OpenSearch TLS verification disabled (verificationMode: none) [merge with Wazuh Dashboard OpenSearch verificationMode none duplicate]
TheHive disables TLS certificate verification to OpenSearch (TH_DB_ES_SSL_VERIFY=false)
Cortex talks to OpenSearch over plaintext HTTP
No TLS verification strategy for self-signed Wazuh/TheHive/OpenCTI cert in the three MCP servers (NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS / scoped undici Agent)
Wazuh MCP startup connectivity probe relies on global TLS bypass

remediationGroups.theme: Centralize and harden the upstream-fetch + error layer in the MCP shared package
remediationGroups.rationale: fetchWithRetry/formatResponse and the per-server boilerplate are the shared chokepoint; fixing them once propagates to all three servers and removes copy-paste drift. Includes: retry idempotency + overall deadline; valid-JSON truncation (cap records pre-serialize); library logging hook instead of hardcoded console.error; generic tool-facing errors (stop leaking status/URL/raw upstream bodies to the LLM); extract startServer()+single safeId factory and reconcile the three divergent safeId regexes; drop the unreachable 'failed after retries' throw clutter (keep+comment for TS).
findingTitles: fetchWithRetry retries non-idempotent POST auth and double-counts timeout
formatResponse truncates raw JSON mid-token, producing invalid JSON
Shared timeout/retry use console.error from a library; no caller hook
Error messages/connectivity logs leak internal URL/status to LLM/stderr (wazuh)
Raw upstream API error body forwarded to the LLM (thehive)
GraphQL error objects stringified into thrown message (opencti)
Boilerplate duplicated across the three MCP servers; reconcile divergent safeId regexes
fetchWithRetry loop can fall through to a misleading 'failed after retries' error

remediationGroups.rationale: Remaining per-tool defects and conventions that aren't the indexer-endpoint criticals but ship wrong/awkward behavior. Includes: TheHive merge-alerts wrong endpoint/payload; OpenCTI search-indicators/get-indicator query observables not indicators (rename/redescribe); JWT TTL guess + no 401 re-auth (derive from exp, clear-and-retry on 401); falsy-zero/!==undefined guards; readOnlyHint annotations on all read-only tools; per-tool error wrapping returning {isError,content}; response typing for thehive/opencti; expose tlp/pap/ioc and constrain type/relationship_type enums; remove no-op encodeURIComponent.
findingTitles: merge-alerts uses an endpoint/payload that does not match TheHive 5 API
search-indicators / get-indicator query observables, not indicators
JWT cached with hardcoded TTL guess, no expiry-from-server, no invalidation on 401 (wazuh + dashboard)
level_min/level falsy-zero / range filtering use truthiness checks
All tools read-only yet no readOnlyHint/annotations declared
No per-tool error wrapping (thehive)
thehiveApi/graphql return type is unknown with no response typing
Hardcoded TLP/PAP=2 and ioc=true with no override
type/relationship_type filter values unvalidated (no enum)
Redundant encodeURIComponent on regex-validated safeId; inconsistent agent_id validation

remediationGroups.theme: Make the deploy script robust and reproducible
remediationGroups.rationale: deploy-stack.sh is the documented entry point; these batch into one pass over the script to make it idempotent, fail-fast, and pinned. Includes: dedupe + align the divergent Helm repo URLs into one shared definition with --force-update (setup-lima URLs are canonical); pin --version on all 9 helm installs; ensure namespaces exist in every standalone branch; treat OpenSearch readiness-wait failure as fatal and exit non-zero on UNHEALTHY>0; strengthen validate_secrets (reject default usernames/duplicate TI keys, validate env-supplied OpenCTI creds); fix the misplaced 2>/dev/null||true redirects in teardown/setup-ism/test-flow.
findingTitles: Helm repo URLs diverge between setup-lima.sh and deploy-stack.sh (dedupe all three duplicate findings)
No Helm chart versions pinned — non-reproducible deploys
opencti standalone deploy creates a Secret before its namespace exists
wait-for-ready failures masked with || true; post-deploy health check non-blocking
validate_secrets does not catch insecure default usernames or env-supplied OpenCTI creds
Misplaced redirect: 2>/dev/null || true applies to assignment not command substitution

remediationGroups.theme: Cluster security hardening: Pod Security Standards, securityContext baseline, egress tightening, RBAC
remediationGroups.rationale: Defense-in-depth controls that should be applied as one coherent hardening pass once the stack actually deploys. Includes: namespace PSS enforce labels (restricted/baseline for shared/opencti/thehive/monitoring; privileged for wazuh); standardize seccompProfile RuntimeDefault + drop-ALL + runAsNonRoot across manager/dashboard/cortex/connectors; NFS-server scoped caps + dedicated namespace; replace 'to: []' on 443 egress with internet-only ipBlock excepting cluster/link-local CIDRs; scope kube-system dashboard ingress to the Traefik podSelector; tighten open agent enrollment (auth password) and consider digest-pinned images.
findingTitles: No Pod Security Standards enforced at namespace level
No runAsNonRoot/readOnlyRootFilesystem/cap-drop/seccomp on manager or dashboard; inconsistent hardening across workloads
NFS server runs fully privileged with no capability scoping
Egress HTTPS rule to:[] lets opencti/thehive pods reach ANY destination on 443
kube-system dashboard ingress uses broad namespaceSelector (not Traefik-only)
Open agent enrollment with no password and use_source_ip disabled
Container images not digest-pinned (tag-only)
Cortex job-runner SA can create arbitrary Jobs/PVCs with no PSS floor on spawned pods

remediationGroups.theme: Lima/k3s host hardening and provisioning de-duplication
remediationGroups.rationale: All the Lima findings touch two near-duplicate provision scripts; fix once after extracting the shared block. Includes: drop --write-kubeconfig-mode=644 (default 600); revert kubeconfig server to 127.0.0.1 + pin portForward hostIP:127.0.0.1; pin INSTALL_K3S_VERSION and the helm installer to a tagged release with checksum; extract the duplicated provision script; remove the no-op sed and dangling /etc/hosts DNS comment.
findingTitles: k3s API server / kubeconfig 0.0.0.0 + world-readable kubeconfig (dedupe the 3 Lima findings)
Supply-chain: unpinned curl|sh for k3s and helm-from-main
Provision script duplicated across two Lima defs with no shared source
No-op sed in kubeconfig fallback (127.0.0.1 -> 127.0.0.1)
Dangling DNS comment with no corresponding provisioning

remediationGroups.theme: Resolve dead/duplicate manifests and orphaned config (cleanliness, single source of truth)
remediationGroups.rationale: After choosing Helm-canonical, sweep the dead/placeholder/orphaned artifacts in one cleanup so future audits read what runs. Includes: delete dead raw deployment.yamls + rabbitmq-deployment.yaml; remove the wazuh-namespace opensearch-ca-cert placeholder block (or wire it); drop dead indexer-password key; remove empty k8s/wazuh/indexer/ dir (or README it); delete aspirational helm-values.yaml or commit to a chart; standardize wazuh ConfigMap labels; wire-or-document the orphaned setup-ism.sh and w2thive.py integration (and fix the w2thive full_log==null crash).
findingTitles: opensearch-ca-cert ConfigMap (wazuh ns) unused and ships placeholder
indexer-password key in soc-wazuh-secrets is dead
Empty k8s/wazuh/indexer/ directory implies a missing component
helm-values.yaml is aspirational and diverges from applied raw manifests
Service selectors use app.kubernetes.io/name while ConfigMaps use legacy app: label
ISM setup is orphaned — never called by deploy and swallows all failures
w2thive.py integration is never deployed (orphaned) and crashes on full_log==null
Filebeat CA path env points to ca.crt while certs are root-ca.pem

remediationGroups.theme: Dashboard UI: accessibility, off-localhost URLs, and component de-duplication
remediationGroups.rationale: Front-end batch covering the single-pane-of-glass breakage off-host plus a11y and DRY cleanup. Includes: source all external/iframe URLs from PUBLIC_* env via config (fix the hardcoded localhost:3000/9000/9001/4000/5601 in GrafanaEmbed, config.ts, and per-service pages); make ServiceCard keyboard-operable + add aria-labels/StatusDot text; add prefers-reduced-motion; add +error.svelte; extract a ServiceDetailLayout + shared iconMap/spinner/styles; centralize theme application in the layout; fix version-drift literals (Sidebar v1.5.0, thehive-mcp 1.0.0 vs 1.4.0) by reading package.json.
findingTitles: Grafana iframe/service links hardcode http://localhost (breaks off-localhost)
Clickable card not keyboard-operable, a11y warnings suppressed
Icon-only buttons/links lack accessible names; status by color only
Three perpetual CSS animations ignore prefers-reduced-motion
No +error.svelte route boundary
Five near-identical service pages + duplicated iconMap/spin/hardcoded URLs
Theme applied via JS on mount across three places (brittle)
Version drift: sidebar v1.5.0 vs package.json 1.0.0; thehive-mcp 1.0.0 vs 1.4.0
Dashboard-API: upstream error leakage, unvalidated JSON casts, duplicated proxy boilerplate, brittle health check, default fallback creds

remediationGroups.theme: Reconcile documentation drift and overclaims to a single source of truth
remediationGroups.rationale: Docs actively mislead (and one tool that doesn't exist is documented). Batch into one doc pass with a VERSIONS/counts source-of-truth. Includes: remove run-sca-scan/PUT /sca from mcp-servers.md; fix README dashboard env path (dashboard/.env) and root .env.example; fix OpenCTI version everywhere; fix NetworkPolicy counts (actual 31); fix Wazuh Dashboard port 5601 in network-diagram.md; bump SECURITY.md supported version + real contact; soften 'audited against ISO 27001/NIST/OWASP' to 'designed with reference to' (or add a real control-mapping doc); fix adding-agents 4.9.2→4.14.3; reconcile RAM figures; fix stale CLAUDE.md Dashboard Integration section; fix integrations.md mislabeled Cortex/OpenCTI block; fix architecture.md disk figure.
findingTitles: mcp-servers.md documents a run-sca-scan tool / /sca endpoint that do not exist
README dashboard env setup points to wrong directory; root .env.example incomplete
OpenCTI version inconsistent across docs/manifests/helm
NetworkPolicy counts wrong (docs 18/23, actual 31)
network-diagram.md states Wazuh Dashboard on 443; actual 5601
SECURITY.md Supported Versions stale; vague disclosure contact
Compliance 'audited against' claims unsubstantiated

CLAUDE.md Dashboard Integration section entirely stale
integrations.md OpenCTI<->TheHive Configuration block shows Cortex config

remediationGroups.theme: Operational robustness, observability, and lower-impact hardening
remediationGroups.rationale: Reliability and observability nice-to-haves once the stack runs. Includes: exec/API health probes on the manager (not just tcpSocket 1514); PDB; Prometheus retentionSize vs PVC headroom; add Alertmanager receiver + SOC-specific PrometheusRules (OpenSearch yellow/red, manager down, connector CronJob failures); structured JSON logging + audit log for MCP WRITE tools; secrets hygiene (echo|base64 history, enable k3s --secrets-encryption, rotate the on-disk dashboard/.env tokens, fix identical TI placeholder keys); MITRE connector pinned release tag; dashboard polling visibilitychange/backoff and duplicate-poll removal; VirusTotal replicas:0+CONNECTOR_AUTO trap; in-flight token-refresh lock.
findingTitles: TCP-socket-only probes can't detect manager process health
No PodDisruptionBudget; single replica with no anti-restart safeguards
Prometheus retentionSize (3GB) exceeds 5Gi PVC headroom
No alerting rules / Alertmanager receiver shipped
MCP servers emit only ad-hoc console.error; no structured/audit logging
secrets.yaml.example echo|base64 leaks to shell history; K8s secrets unencrypted at rest
Threat-intel API keys identical placeholder values; rotate on-disk dashboard/.env tokens
MITRE connector imports from mutable master branch (supply chain)
Polling does not pause on hidden tab / no backoff; duplicated overview polling
VirusTotal connector replicas:0 with CONNECTOR_AUTO=true (silent no-op)
Module-scoped JWT cache: no in-flight refresh lock / concurrency guard
Monitoring egress/shared ingress: Redis/MinIO/node-exporter metrics blocked; add allow-prometheus-scrape + port 9121

remediationGroups.rationale: Low-risk papercuts and DX foundation, batched to clear the long tail. Includes: read version from package.json (stop hardcoding McpServer version); add TOKEN_TTL_MS/REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS/MAX_RESPONSE_CHARS to .env.example; unify the WAZUH_API_URL default across src/settings/.env; drop declaration:true from app tsconfig; log the swallowed connectivity error; remove dead/no-op code; add Makefile/Taskfile + .nvmrc + engines + shared eslint/prettier(or Biome); reference external env for MCP secrets rather than inlining into settings.json.
findingTitles: Reported server version hardcoded (1.0.0) vs package.json (1.4.0)
TOKEN_TTL_MS/REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS/MAX_RESPONSE_CHARS undocumented env vars
Default WAZUH_API_URL inconsistent across src/settings/.env
tsconfig declaration:true for an executable app
Connectivity-test catch swallows the underlying error (thehive)
safeId comment claims path-traversal protection but IDs are GraphQL variables
OPENCTI_URL / connectivity-probe duplication / type narrowing (opencti cleanliness set)
No Makefile/Taskfile, devcontainer, lint/format, or Node engines pin
MCP servers rely on plaintext credentials in .claude/settings.json env block
Connectivity probe / startup boilerplate duplication (thehive/opencti bypass fetchWithRetry)
Unescaped JSON payload interpolated into single-quoted curl -d in setup-ism.sh
NFS PV RWM vs local-path RWO backing single-node assumption

stateOfTheArtUpgrades: Add GitHub Actions CI (build/typecheck all 4 TS packages, svelte-check, kubeconform on k8s/**, shellcheck, gitleaks, helm template|kubeconform) with actions pinned to commit SHAs and required status checks — this is the single control that would have caught most of the criticals before they shipped.

Adopt a real test suite (Vitest): unit-test the pure shared functions (fetchWithRetry retry/timeout, formatResponse truncation boundary, the safeId regexes) and contract-test each MCP tool's request path/params — directly prevents the wrong-endpoint and stale-build class of bug.

Pick ONE deployment mechanism per component (Helm-canonical) and express the stack as a Kustomize base/overlays tree or a Helm umbrella chart, ideally reconciled by Argo CD/Flux — eliminates the dead-duplicate-manifest landmine and the raw-vs-Helm config drift in one move.

Manage secrets with SOPS+age (lightest for a homelab) or Sealed Secrets / External Secrets Operator so secret manifests can live in Git, be versioned, and be rotated — replacing the hand-edited gitignored secrets.yaml + imperative kubectl create dual-source pattern, and enable k3s --secrets-encryption at rest.

Automate TLS/PKI: a cert-manager selfsigned ClusterIssuer issuing all internal certs (opensearch-certs, wazuh-dashboard-certs, soc-tls-cert) so trust is consistent and rotating, and remove every NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED/verify:false/tlsSkipVerify in favor of NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS or scoped CA mounts.

Enforce Pod Security Standards at the namespace level (restricted/baseline + a scoped privileged exemption only for the Wazuh agent and NFS) plus a baseline securityContext (seccomp RuntimeDefault, drop-ALL, runAsNonRoot) so privilege regressions fail closed rather than relying on advisory per-pod settings.

Add authentication and observability the SOC currently lacks: SvelteKit hooks.server.ts (or Traefik forward-auth/SSO) on /api/*, an Alertmanager receiver + SOC-specific PrometheusRules, and structured JSON logging with an audit trail on MCP WRITE tools (create-case, add-observable, run-analyzer, enrich-observable).

Pin everything for reproducibility: helm --version on all 9 chart installs, INSTALL_K3S_VERSION + checksummed helm installer, and container images by @sha256 digest (prioritizing third-party erichough/nfs-server and the soon-to-be-legacy Bitnami redis/rabbitmq, which should be migrated to maintained/official charts).

Establish a single documentation source of truth (a VERSIONS.md / generated key table) for versions, ports, and NetworkPolicy counts, and soften the 'audited against ISO 27001/NIST 800-53/OWASP LLM Top 10' claim to 'designed with reference to' unless a real control-mapping doc with evidence is added.

Add developer-experience scaffolding: a Makefile/Taskfile wrapping deploy/teardown/test, .nvmrc + engines pins, npm workspaces (root package.json) so the shared MCP package builds first, and a shared Biome (or ESLint+Prettier) config — turning ad-hoc shell + manual PR-checkbox verification into reproducible, enforced workflows.
quickWins: Add the 4 missing kubectl apply lines to deploy_wazuh() (manager/deployment.yaml, dashboard/deployment.yaml, dashboard/configmap-dashboard.yaml) and remove the misleading 'Helm chart may need manual setup' note — restores the core SIEM.
Add kubectl apply -f shared/secrets.yaml to a deploy_secrets step before deploy_shared — unblocks every Helm chart/StatefulSet waiting on missing secrets.
Remove NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 from dashboard/vite.config.ts, dashboard/.env, and dashboard/.env.example; set NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS to the SOC CA instead — closes the credential-MITM hole.
Remove optional:true from the four Wazuh INDEXER/API/DASHBOARD password secretKeyRefs so pods fail closed on missing creds (one-line each).

Flip Grafana datasource tlsSkipVerify:true→false, Wazuh Dashboard opensearch.ssl.verificationMode none→full, and TheHive TH_DB_ES_SSL_VERIFY false→true (all with the mounted root-ca.pem) — trivial edits once CA material exists.
Set FILEBEAT_SSL_CERTIFICATE_AUTHORITIES from /etc/ssl/opensearch/ca.crt to root-ca.pem to match the actual secret key.

Delete the four dead raw manifests (thehive/cortex/opencti deployment.yaml + shared/rabbitmq-deployment.yaml) and the dead indexer-password key, empty k8s/wazuh/indexer/ dir, and opensearch-ca-cert wazuh placeholder block.
Remove run-sca-scan + PUT /sca from docs/mcp-servers.md and fix README's dashboard env step to `cp dashboard/.env.example dashboard/.env`.
Fix the w2thive.py full_log crash: (alert.get('full_log') or 'N/A')[:500].
Fix the OpenCTI get-attack-patterns tactic filter (add the killChainPhases FilterGroup) or correct its description so it stops returning silently-wrong results.
Dedupe and align the divergent Helm repo URLs into one definition using the canonical setup-lima.sh URLs (the deploy-stack.sh ones are stale).

confirmed.title: list-alerts / get-alert query a non-existent Wazuh Server API endpoint
confirmed.evidence: Both tools call wazuhApi("/alerts", ...) and wazuhApi(`/alerts/${alert_id}`) against WAZUH_API_URL (port 55000, the Server API authenticated via /security/user/authenticate at line 43). The Wazuh 4.x Server API has no /alerts or /alerts/{id} endpoint. Alerts are stored only in the Wazuh indexer (wazuh-alerts-* index, port 9200) and must be retrieved via POST .../_search. These two flagship tools will return HTTP 404 (surfaced as 'Wazuh API error: 404' at line 67) for every call.
confirmed.recommendation: Re-point alert retrieval at the Wazuh Indexer/OpenSearch API (port 9200, separate base URL + auth) and implement a POST _search against wazuh-alerts-4.x-*, or remove these two tools until indexer access is wired. Add at least one integration/contract test that exercises a real Wazuh instance so a non-existent endpoint is caught before shipping.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading the file. WAZUH_URL defaults to https://localhost:55000 (line 23) — the Wazuh Server API. getToken authenticates via /security/user/authenticate (line 43), confirming this is the port-55000 Server API, not the indexer. list-alerts calls wazuhApi('/alerts', params) at line 96 and get-alert calls wazuhApi(`/alerts/${encodeURIComponent(alert_id)}`) at line 107. The Wazuh 4.x Server API has no /alerts or /alerts/{id} resource; alerts live only in the indexer (wazuh-alerts-* on port 9200, queried via _search). Both calls hit wazuhApi which throws 'Wazuh API error: 404 ...' at line 67 on a 404. These are the two flagship tools and they cannot work against the configured endpoint. Severity critical is accurate.

confirmed.title: get-vulnerabilities hits removed/incorrect /vulnerability/{agent} endpoint
confirmed.evidence: wazuhApi(`/vulnerability/${agent_id}`, params) targets the legacy Vulnerability Detector Server-API path. In Wazuh 4.8+ the vulnerability detection module was rebuilt and CVE inventory is exposed through the indexer (wazuh-states-vulnerabilities-* index), not via GET /vulnerability/{agent} on port 55000. CLAUDE.md states the deployment is Wazuh 4.14.3, so this endpoint does not exist and the tool will 404.
confirmed.recommendation: Query the indexer wazuh-states-vulnerabilities-* index (port 9200) for 4.8+ to retrieve agent CVEs, or remove the tool. Confirm against the exact 4.14.3 API surface. Separately, drop the no-op encodeURIComponent on safeId-validated agent_id (tracked in the cleanliness finding).
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed: line 157 is wazuhApi(`/vulnerability/${encodeURIComponent(agent_id)}`, params) against the port-55000 Server API. The legacy GET /vulnerability/{agent_id} Server-API path belonged to the old Vulnerability Detector. In Wazuh 4.8+ the vulnerability module was rebuilt and CVE inventory moved to the indexer (wazuh-states-vulnerabilities-*); the Server-API agent vulnerability endpoint was removed. CLAUDE.md states the deployment is Wazuh 4.14.3 (>= 4.8), so this endpoint does not exist and the tool 404s via the same line-67 throw. Critical is justified (a flagship tool that always fails). The finding's secondary note about redundant encodeURIComponent on safeId is also correct and overlaps the cleanliness finding.

confirmed.title: Runtime uses a stale build; settings.json runs dist/index.js that predates the shared-package refactor
confirmed.evidence: The committed dist/index.js inlines fetchWithRetry, RETRYABLE_CODES, formatResponse and MAX_RESPONSE_CHARS locally (lines 36, 97) and lacks the main() connectivity test present in src/index.ts:201-215. .claude/settings.json:5 launches exactly this file ('mcp-servers/wazuh-mcp/dist/index.js'). The source has been refactored to import { fetchWithRetry, formatResponse } from '@matchbox/mcp-shared' (src line 21) but was never rebuilt, so the deployed behavior diverges from source.
confirmed.recommendation: Add a prestart/CI build gate (npm run build before start, build the shared package first) so dist is always regenerated from current src. Since dist/ is already gitignored, the issue is build hygiene, not a commit problem: ensure deploy/start always rebuilds rather than relying on a stale on-disk dist. Correct the audit note — dist already contains main() and is not committed.
confirmed.verifyNote: Partially confirmed with two evidence errors. CONFIRMED: dist/index.js inlines fetchWithRetry (line 36), RETRYABLE_CODES (line 34), formatResponse (line 97) and MAX_RESPONSE_CHARS (line 95) locally and does NOT import @matchbox/mcp-shared (grep found no such import), whereas src line 21 imports { fetchWithRetry, formatResponse } from '@matchbox/mcp-shared'. settings.json line 5 launches 'mcp-servers/wazuh-mcp/dist/index.js'. So the on-disk runtime artifact is stale vs source — the real, high-impact issue. TWO EVIDENCE ERRORS: (1) The finding says dist 'lacks the main() connectivity test present in src:201-215' — FALSE; dist DOES contain main() with the identical connectivity test at dist lines 196-210. (2) The finding calls it 'The committed dist/index.js'; in fact dist/ is gitignored (.gitignore:11) and dist/index.js is NOT tracked by git (git ls-files/check-unmatch confirm). So 'committed' is wrong, which also undercuts the recommendation to 'commit dist out of source control entirely' — it already is out of source control. The core defect (stale on-disk build that settings.json runs) holds, so isReal=true; severity high is appropriate because the divergence is only the import source (behavior is currently equivalent), not a functional regression on its own.

confirmed.title: Shared package has no dist/ built, so @matchbox/mcp-shared import will fail to resolve at runtime
confirmed.evidence: package.json declares "main": "dist/index.js" and "types": "dist/index.d.ts", but the shared/ directory contains only src/, package.json, tsconfig.json — there is no dist/ (confirmed via ls). wazuh-mcp depends on it via "file:../shared" and src/index.ts:21 imports from it. A fresh `npm run build` of wazuh-mcp (moduleResolution: bundler resolves to main=dist/index.js) and any `node dist/index.js` that actually imports the shared package will fail with module-not-found because shared/dist/index.js doesn't exist. There is also no build script ordering to guarantee shared builds first.
confirmed.recommendation: Make the shared package actually consumable before consumers build: either adopt npm workspaces (root package.json with workspaces + a build script that compiles shared first, e.g. tsc -b project references), or run shared's build and `npm install` so node_modules/@matchbox/mcp-shared exists and points at shared/dist. Also rebuild all three consumers' dist/ so the committed artifacts actually import the shared package instead of stale inlined copies (current dist diverges from src). Add a CI build to prevent this from recurring.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed and actually stronger than stated. shared/package.json:6-7 declares "main":"dist/index.js" and "types":"dist/index.d.ts", but shared/ contains only src/, package.json, tsconfig.json — no dist/ (verified via ls). All three consumers import it: wazuh-mcp/src/index.ts:21, thehive-mcp/src/index.ts:22, opencti-mcp/src/index.ts:20, each via package.json "@matchbox/mcp-shared":"file:../shared". Beyond the missing dist, the dependency is not installed at all: node_modules/@matchbox does not exist in any consumer. I ran `tsc --noEmit` in wazuh-mcp and it failed with `src/index.ts(21,48): error TS2307: Cannot find module '@matchbox/mcp-shared'`. The committed wazuh-mcp/dist/index.js is stale — it has fetchWithRetry/formatResponse INLINED (no import of the shared package), i.e. built from the pre-refactor source, so it runs standalone but does not exercise the shared package and masks the breakage. No build-order/workspace mechanism exists (no root package.json). A fresh `npm install` + `npm run build` of any consumer is broken. High severity is correct.

confirmed.title: No TLS verification strategy for self-signed Wazuh cert; bare fetch() with no CA config
confirmed.evidence: WAZUH_API_URL defaults to https and the homelab uses a self-signed cert (CLAUDE.md: soc-tls-cert). All requests use Node's global fetch with no custom https.Agent, no NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS guidance, and no ca/rejectUnauthorized option (lines 43, 63, 206). Against a self-signed endpoint this either fails entirely, or operators silently set NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 process-wide (disabling cert validation for the whole process, MITM-exposing the admin credentials sent at line 42-45). Neither .env.example nor settings.json documents a CA path.
confirmed.recommendation: Document and require NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS pointing at the SOC CA in .env.example and settings.json, or pass a scoped undici Agent with the pinned CA via the fetch dispatcher option in mcp-shared so all three call sites inherit it. Never instruct operators to set NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0. Pin the CA so the admin credential is not MITM-exposed.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. WAZUH_URL defaults to https (line 23) and CLAUDE.md states a self-signed cert (soc-tls-cert). All HTTPS calls go through global fetch with no custom dispatcher/Agent and no ca/rejectUnauthorized: getToken's fetchWithRetry at line 43 (sends Basic-auth admin credentials, lines 42-45), wazuhApi's fetchWithRetry at line 63, and the bare fetch in main() at line 206. Neither .env.example (only WAZUH_API_URL/USER/PASSWORD) nor settings.json documents NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS or any CA path. Against a self-signed endpoint, Node's fetch rejects the cert unless the operator globally sets NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0, which disables verification process-wide and exposes the Basic-auth credential to MITM. High is appropriate (credential-exposure risk + undocumented insecure workaround). Note line 63 routes through the shared fetchWithRetry, which equally has no CA config.

confirmed.title: level_min and level falsy-zero / range filtering bugs from truthiness checks
confirmed.evidence: Filters are applied with `if (level_min)` (line 92) and `if (level)` (line 175). These are guarded by zod min(1) so 0 cannot pass today, but the pattern is fragile: any future relaxation to min(0) silently drops the filter. More importantly, list-alerts builds params['search'] = `rule.level>=${level_min}` (line 92) — the Wazuh Server API has no /alerts endpoint to apply this to, and even on a valid endpoint a free-form 'search' param is not the documented level filter (q= or specific level params are). The query semantics are likely wrong even if the endpoint existed.
confirmed.recommendation: Use explicit `!== undefined` guards for optional numeric params to future-proof against a min(0) relaxation. When the /alerts retrieval is re-pointed at the indexer, use the documented level filter (a proper range query / field, not a free-form 'search' string). Add boundary tests once the endpoint works.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed as written but over-stated at medium. Line 92 is `if (level_min) params['search'] = `rule.level>=${level_min}``; line 175 is `if (level) params['level'] = String(level)`. Both are guarded by zod .min(1) (lines 83 and 168), so level 0 cannot reach these checks today — the falsy-zero bug is latent, not active. The finding itself concedes 'so 0 cannot pass today.' The secondary point — that list-alerts overloads params['search'] with `rule.level>=N` against a /alerts endpoint that does not exist, and that 'search' is not the documented level filter — is valid but is fully subsumed by the critical /alerts finding (the endpoint doesn't exist, so the param semantics are moot). Net: a real-but-latent fragility plus a wrong-query-semantics note; downgrade to low since nothing currently misbehaves given the zod floors.

confirmed.title: Search/name/group string params are not constrained against Wazuh query-injection
confirmed.evidence: search (rules/decoders), group, and name accept arbitrary text up to 256 chars (z.string().max(256)) and are passed straight into Wazuh query params (e.g. params['search']=search, params['group']=group). The Wazuh API 'q'/'search' grammar uses operators (;,()=<>~), so unsanitized input can alter query logic (parameter/query injection within the agent's own privilege scope). While URL.searchParams.set encodes for transport, it does not neutralize Wazuh's query operators.
confirmed.recommendation: Restrict free-text filters to a conservative charset or whitelist the subset of Wazuh query grammar you intend to expose, and prefer field-specific params over generic search/q. Document that LLM-provided tool arguments are untrusted input. Treat as low/hardening given the read-only, single-privilege blast radius.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed as code fact, but severity over-stated. search (line 167, used at 174), group (line 169, used at 176), and name (line 117, used at 124) are z.string().max(256) with no charset restriction and are passed directly into Wazuh query params. URL.searchParams.set (wazuhApi line 60) encodes for transport but does not neutralize Wazuh's q/search grammar operators (;,()=<>~). So an LLM-supplied value could alter query logic. However: (a) only get-rules/get-decoders/search-agents reach valid endpoints (/rules,/decoders,/agents), and any injection stays within the authenticated agent's own read-only privilege scope — no privilege escalation, no writes; (b) the cited line 124 is search-agents name (correct), not 'search'. This is a defense-in-depth hardening item against query-logic tampering, not an exploitable security hole, so low is more accurate than medium. isReal=true for the unconstrained-input fact.

confirmed.title: fetchWithRetry retries non-idempotent POST auth and double-counts timeout
confirmed.evidence: fetchWithRetry retries any request including POST /security/user/authenticate (wazuh-mcp src:43). A 429/503 or timeout on auth will be retried, which is acceptable for auth but the function applies the same retry policy to all callers regardless of method/idempotency. Also, on a 429/503 retry it sleeps 1000ms (line 30) while the original AbortController timer is still pending until clearTimeout in finally — fine — but the retry's own attempt starts a brand-new timeoutMs, so total wall time can be ~2*timeoutMs+1s with no overall deadline. Truncation/timeout are per-attempt, not per-call.
confirmed.recommendation: Add a method/idempotency-aware retry decision (e.g. only auto-retry GET, or take an explicit `retryable` flag), enforce an overall deadline across attempts rather than per-attempt timeouts, and make max attempts + backoff configurable instead of the hardcoded 2-iteration loop.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. fetchWithRetry (shared/src/index.ts:18-46) takes only (url, opts, timeoutMs) with no method/idempotency awareness and retries every caller. wazuh-mcp/src/index.ts:43 calls it for the POST /security/user/authenticate auth flow, so a 429/503 (line 28) or AbortError timeout (line 35) on that POST is retried. Each attempt creates its own AbortController + setTimeout(timeoutMs) (lines 24-25), cleared per-attempt in finally (line 42); there is no overall deadline, so worst case is ~2*timeoutMs plus the 1000ms backoff sleep (lines 30/37). Truncation/timeout are per-attempt, exactly as the finding states. Note the retry policy here is single-retry (loop bound 0..2) and hardcoded, not configurable. Medium is appropriate — correctness/robustness concern, not a security hole; auth retry is mostly tolerable, the lack of an overall deadline is the more material issue.

confirmed.title: formatResponse truncates raw JSON mid-token, producing invalid JSON for the LLM
confirmed.evidence: When JSON exceeds maxChars it does json.slice(0, maxChars) + a note. This cuts in the middle of a string/object, so the LLM receives syntactically broken JSON it cannot reliably parse, and the truncation message is appended outside any JSON structure. There is no attempt to truncate the underlying array (e.g. number of records) before serialization, which would be the cleaner cap.
confirmed.recommendation: Cap records before serialization (slice the data array) or emit a structured wrapper like {truncated:true, shown:N, total:M, data:[...]} that stays valid JSON. At minimum make the truncation note unambiguous that the payload is not parseable JSON.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. formatResponse (shared/src/index.ts:49-53) does JSON.stringify then, when json.length > maxChars (line 51), returns json.slice(0, maxChars) + a plaintext note (line 52). slice cuts at an arbitrary character boundary, so the output is syntactically broken JSON and the truncation note is appended outside any JSON structure. No attempt is made to cap the underlying record count before serialization. Real correctness/usability issue for LLM consumers. Medium is fair, though impact is bounded (default MAX_RESPONSE_CHARS=50000 means it only triggers on large payloads, and the result is still human/LLM-readable text, just not parseable JSON).

confirmed.title: No tests anywhere; no CI; correctness bugs (wrong endpoints, stale build) would have been caught
confirmed.evidence: package.json has only build/dev/start scripts — no test script, no test files in the repo for the MCP servers. The endpoint-mismatch (/alerts, /vulnerability) and the stale-dist divergence are exactly the class of defect an integration or contract test would surface. The shared package likewise has no tests.
confirmed.recommendation: Add vitest with unit tests for the shared fetchWithRetry/formatResponse (mock fetch; assert retry-on-429/503, timeout/abort, truncation at MAX_RESPONSE_CHARS) and contract tests asserting each tool's request path/params, plus a CI build gate that fails if dist is out of date. Tests for the shared package too.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. package.json scripts (lines 7-11) contain only build/dev/start — no test script. A repo-wide find across mcp-servers (excluding node_modules) for *.test.*, *.spec.*, vitest*, jest* returned zero files. The shared package's package.json (lines 8-11) likewise has only build/dev, no test. No CI config was surfaced. The two critical endpoint bugs and the stale-dist divergence are exactly the defect class a contract/integration test or a build gate would catch. Medium is appropriate (process gap that enabled the criticals).

confirmed.title: JWT token cached in module scope with hardcoded TTL guess, no expiry-from-server, no invalidation on 401
confirmed.evidence: tokenExpiry is set to now + TOKEN_TTL_MS (default 850000ms ≈ 14.2min) rather than the token's actual exp claim. Wazuh's default token TTL is 900s but is configurable; if an admin lowers it, the cached token will be used past expiry and every call returns 401 (surfaced as 'Wazuh API error: 401' at line 67) with no re-auth retry. There is no path that clears jwtToken on a 401 to force re-authentication.
confirmed.recommendation: Derive tokenExpiry from the JWT exp claim (decode the token) or read expiry from the auth response, with a safety margin. On a 401 from wazuhApi, clear jwtToken and retry getToken() once before surfacing the error. Re-categorize as a robustness/reliability item.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed as code behavior; security severity over-stated. TOKEN_TTL_MS defaults to 850000ms (~14.2min) at line 32; getToken returns the cached token while Date.now()<tokenExpiry (line 40); tokenExpiry is set to now+TOKEN_TTL_MS (line 51), i.e. a fixed guess, not the JWT exp claim or the auth response. There is no path that clears jwtToken on a 401 — wazuhApi just throws 'Wazuh API error: 401' (line 67) with no re-auth retry. Wazuh's default token TTL is 900s, so 850000ms is a conservative-but-blind guess; if an admin lowers the TTL below ~14min, cached tokens are used past expiry and every call 401s until the 14min window rolls. This is an availability/correctness robustness gap, not a confidentiality/integrity security defect — better classified low (and arguably functionality/design rather than security). isReal=true.

confirmed.title: Error messages and connectivity logs may leak internal URL/status to LLM/stderr surface
confirmed.evidence: Thrown errors include resp.status/statusText and the API surface ('Auth failed: ...', 'Wazuh API error: ...'). These propagate to the MCP client as tool errors visible to the model. fetchWithRetry (shared:29,36) and main() (index:207,209) also log full URLs to stderr. Low risk in a homelab but the auth-failure message combined with Basic-auth flow could aid an attacker probing the bridge.
confirmed.recommendation: Return generic tool-facing errors ('Wazuh request failed') to the model while logging full detail (status, URL) only to stderr. Avoid echoing internal URLs back through the tool result. Low priority/nit.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed factually but minimal impact. Thrown errors include status/statusText and the operation: 'Auth failed: ...' (line 48) and 'Wazuh API error: ...' (line 67); these propagate to the MCP client as tool errors visible to the model. main() logs the full WAZUH_URL to stderr at lines 207 and 209, and the shared fetchWithRetry logs URLs to stderr (shared lines 29, 36->actually 29 and the throw at 45). The leaked data is the operator's own internal SOC URL and HTTP status — no secrets (credentials are never echoed). On a localhost/homelab single-operator bridge this is negligible. Downgrade to nit. isReal=true for the leak-of-internal-detail fact.

confirmed.title: Redundant encodeURIComponent on regex-validated safeId; inconsistent agent_id validation
confirmed.evidence: safeId already restricts to [a-zA-Z0-9_.~-]+ (line 34), all of which are URL-safe, so encodeURIComponent at lines 107/139/157 is a no-op. Meanwhile list-alerts validates agent_id/rule_id with the *-suffixed regex allowing empty string (line 84-85: ^[a-zA-Z0-9_.~-]*$) while get-agent-info uses safeId (+, min 1). Two different agent_id schemas across tools is inconsistent.
confirmed.recommendation: Drop the no-op encodeURIComponent on safeId-validated values (or add a comment if kept defensively), and centralize one agent_id schema, expressing optional vs required via .optional() rather than diverging the regex. Fix the audit's line ref 137 -> 139.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed; one cited line number is off. safeId = z.string().regex(/^[a-zA-Z0-9_.~-]+$/) at line 34 — every allowed char is URL-safe, so encodeURIComponent is a no-op at lines 107 (get-alert), 139 (get-agent-info), and 157 (get-vulnerabilities). The finding cited 137 for get-agent-info; the actual call is line 139 (grep confirms encodeURIComponent at 107/139/157). Inconsistency also confirmed: list-alerts validates agent_id/rule_id with the *-suffixed regex `^[a-zA-Z0-9_.~-]*$` allowing empty string (lines 84-85), while get-agent-info/get-vulnerabilities use safeId (+, min 1). Two agent_id schemas across tools. Pure cleanliness; nit.

confirmed.title: No [WRITE] annotations needed but no readOnly/annotations declared; all tools are read-only yet unmarked
confirmed.evidence: CLAUDE.md states the convention is '[WRITE] annotations on mutations'. These tools are all read-only, which is correct, but the SDK supports tool annotations (readOnlyHint) and none are set. For a SOC bridge that may later add write tools (restart agent, etc.), establishing the readOnlyHint/destructiveHint pattern now prevents an unmarked mutation slipping in later.
confirmed.recommendation: Add annotations: { readOnlyHint: true } to each tool registration so the [WRITE]/destructiveHint pattern is established and future mutating tools must consciously opt out.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. All seven tools (lines 78-198) are registered via server.tool(name, description, schema, handler) with no annotations object; a grep for readOnlyHint/annotations/[WRITE] across mcp-servers src matched only node_modules and the other servers' SDK type imports — wazuh-mcp/src/index.ts has none. CLAUDE.md states the convention is '[WRITE] annotations on mutations'. The tools are correctly read-only, but no readOnlyHint is declared, so a future write tool could slip in unmarked. Design/convention gap; low is reasonable.

confirmed.title: TOKEN_TTL_MS and REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS/MAX_RESPONSE_CHARS are undocumented env vars
confirmed.evidence: .env.example lists only WAZUH_API_URL/USER/PASSWORD, but the code reads TOKEN_TTL_MS (index:32) and the shared package reads REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS and MAX_RESPONSE_CHARS (shared:9,12) from the environment. CLAUDE.md convention says 'Use .env.example files to document required environment variables'. Operators have no signal these tunables exist.
confirmed.recommendation: Add TOKEN_TTL_MS, REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS, and MAX_RESPONSE_CHARS to .env.example with their defaults (850000, 10000, 50000) and a one-line description each.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. .env.example (lines 1-4) documents only WAZUH_API_URL, WAZUH_API_USER, WAZUH_API_PASSWORD. The code reads TOKEN_TTL_MS at src line 32, and the shared package reads REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS (shared line 9) and MAX_RESPONSE_CHARS (shared line 12) from process.env. None of these three tunables are in .env.example, contrary to the CLAUDE.md convention to document required env vars via .env.example. Low/cleanliness.

confirmed.title: Default WAZUH_API_URL points to localhost:55000 while deployment is soc.homelab.local
confirmed.evidence: Code default is https://localhost:55000 and settings.json:7 also uses localhost:55000, but .env.example uses https://soc.homelab.local:55000 and CLAUDE.md routes Wazuh via Traefik at soc.homelab.local. Three inconsistent values for the same endpoint across the codebase increase the chance of pointing at the wrong host.
confirmed.recommendation: Pick one canonical value. Given the Traefik ingress in CLAUDE.md, soc.homelab.local:55000 is the likely correct host; make src default, settings.json env, and .env.example agree. (Verify whether the MCP process can reach Traefik or needs a port-forward to localhost first.)
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. src line 23 defaults to 'https://localhost:55000'; settings.json line 7 sets WAZUH_API_URL to 'https://localhost:55000'; but .env.example line 2 uses 'https://soc.homelab.local:55000' and CLAUDE.md routes Wazuh via Traefik at soc.homelab.local. Three sources, two different host values for the same endpoint. Inconsistency increases the chance of pointing at the wrong host. Low/cleanliness. Note: settings.json env actually overrides the code default at runtime, so localhost is the effective value today (which contradicts the documented deployment host).

confirmed.title: Shared timeout/retry use console.error from a library; no structured logging or caller hook
confirmed.evidence: The shared library writes retry/timeout notices directly to console.error. A reusable package hardcoding stderr logging gives consumers no way to silence or redirect it, and mixes library concerns with app logging. For three MCP servers sharing this, log noise is uncontrolled.
confirmed.recommendation: Accept an optional logger callback (default no-op) or emit nothing from the library and let callers log; at minimum gate the two console.error calls (lines 29, 36) behind a DEBUG flag.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed with a line correction. The library writes retry/timeout notices via console.error at shared/src/index.ts:29 and :36 (the finding cited :29,:37 — line 37 is the AbortError-branch setTimeout sleep, not a log statement; the second console.error is line 36). A reusable package hardcoding stderr logging gives the three consuming MCP servers no way to silence/redirect it. For stdio MCP servers, stderr is the conventional log channel so the noise is not catastrophic, but the lack of a caller hook is a legitimate cleanliness concern. Low/trivial is appropriate.

confirmed.title: tsconfig declares declaration:true for an executable app (no consumers of its types)
confirmed.evidence: wazuh-mcp is an entrypoint binary (#!/usr/bin/env node), not a library, yet tsconfig sets declaration:true, emitting an index.d.ts (present in dist) that nothing imports. Harmless but indicates the tsconfig was copied from the shared library config without adjustment.
confirmed.recommendation: Remove declaration:true from the wazuh-mcp app tsconfig (keep it only on the shared package, which is consumed for types).
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. tsconfig.json line 11 sets 'declaration': true, and src/index.ts is an entrypoint binary (#!/usr/bin/env node, line 1). dist/index.d.ts exists (605 bytes, confirmed via ls) and nothing imports it. Harmless type-emit for an app; nit. Reasonable inference that the config was copied from the shared library's tsconfig.

confirmed.title: merge-alerts uses an endpoint/payload that does not match TheHive 5 API
confirmed.evidence: POST "/api/v1/alert/merge" with body { alertIds, caseId }. TheHive 5 exposes bulk merge at POST /api/v1/alert/merge/_bulk (body { caseId, alertIds }) or per-alert POST /api/v1/alert/{alertId}/merge/{caseId}; there is no /api/v1/alert/merge route taking this body. The tool also advertises 'creates new case if caseId omitted', but the bulk merge endpoint requires a target caseId.
confirmed.recommendation: Switch to POST /api/v1/alert/merge/_bulk with body { caseId, alertIds } against the deployed TheHive 5 version. When case_id is omitted, first create/promote a case (e.g. POST /api/v1/alert/{alertId}/case) instead of relying on undocumented auto-create, and update the tool description accordingly. Add an integration smoke test against a running TheHive.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. Line 215 does POST to "/api/v1/alert/merge" with body { alertIds: alert_ids, caseId: case_id }. TheHive 5's documented merge routes are per-alert POST /api/v1/alert/{alertId}/merge/{caseId} and bulk POST /api/v1/alert/merge/_bulk (body { caseId, alertIds }). There is no /api/v1/alert/merge route accepting this body, so the call will 404/400 against a real TheHive 5. The tool also advertises 'creates new if omitted' (line 212 description) but bulk merge requires a target caseId, so caseId=undefined will not auto-create a case. Both halves of the evidence verified. Medium is appropriate — broken write tool, fails at runtime, but isolated to one tool.

confirmed.title: Reported server version (1.0.0) does not match package.json (1.4.0)
confirmed.evidence: new McpServer({ name: "thehive-mcp", version: "1.0.0" }) while package.json declares "version": "1.4.0". The MCP handshake will advertise a stale version. Same drift exists in wazuh-mcp and opencti-mcp.
confirmed.recommendation: Read version from package.json at build/runtime (import assertion or a generated constant) for a single source of truth, or at minimum sync the literal at index.ts:60 to 1.4.0.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. index.ts line 60 hardcodes version: "1.0.0" in the McpServer constructor, while package.json line 3 declares "version": "1.4.0". The MCP handshake will advertise the stale 1.0.0. (Did not independently verify the same drift in wazuh-mcp/opencti-mcp as they are out of scope for this unit, but the thehive-mcp claim is true.) Low/cleanliness is correct.

confirmed.title: Startup connectivity test duplicates timeout logic and bypasses shared fetchWithRetry
confirmed.evidence: main() builds its own AbortController + setTimeout + raw fetch for the /api/v1/status probe instead of using fetchWithRetry from @matchbox/mcp-shared, re-implementing the exact timeout pattern the shared package already provides. This also means the probe gets no retry on transient 503.
confirmed.recommendation: Route the connectivity probe through fetchWithRetry (or a small shared healthCheck helper) so timeout/retry behavior is consistent and centralized.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. main() lines 227-232 build a fresh AbortController + setTimeout(5_000) + raw fetch for the /api/v1/status probe, re-implementing the exact timeout pattern that fetchWithRetry in @matchbox/mcp-shared (shared/src/index.ts:18-46) already provides. The shared helper retries 429/503 and AbortError once; the inline probe gets no retry on transient 503/timeout. Evidence accurate. The first impacted line is 227 (AbortController), not 226 (the try). Low/cleanliness.

confirmed.title: Connectivity-test catch swallows the underlying error
confirmed.evidence: catch (e) { console.error(`WARNING: TheHive unreachable ...`); } — the caught error `e` is never logged, so DNS/TLS/connection-refused causes are lost during troubleshooting.
confirmed.recommendation: Include a sanitized reason, e.g. `... unreachable at ${THEHIVE_URL}: ${e instanceof Error ? e.message : e}`.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. Line 234 catch (e) logs a generic 'WARNING: TheHive unreachable at ${THEHIVE_URL} ...' (line 235) and never references e, so DNS/TLS/connection-refused root cause is lost. Nit is correct.

confirmed.title: HTTPS to self-signed SOC cert will fail with no documented TLS handling
confirmed.evidence: THEHIVE_URL defaults to https://localhost:9000 and .env.example uses https://soc.homelab.local/thehive, while CLAUDE.md states ingress uses a self-signed cert (soc-tls-cert). Node's fetch rejects self-signed certs by default, so every thehiveApi call and the startup probe will throw 'self-signed certificate' with no CA-trust or opt-out path provided.
confirmed.recommendation: Document and wire a CA bundle (NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS pointing at the SOC root CA) in .env.example and the runbook, or expose an explicit, clearly-warned insecure flag for the homelab. Do not silently set NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 in code.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. Line 24 defaults THEHIVE_URL to https://localhost:9000; .env.example line 2 uses https://soc.homelab.local/thehive; CLAUDE.md Security section states ingress uses a self-signed cert (soc-tls-cert). Node's global fetch rejects self-signed certs by default (UNABLE_TO_VERIFY_LEAF_SIGNATURE / self-signed certificate), so both the startup probe (line 229) and every thehiveApi call (via fetchWithRetry) will throw with no CA-trust path or insecure opt-out anywhere in the code or .env.example. Verified no NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS / NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED handling present. Medium is reasonable — it makes the whole server non-functional against the documented deployment until the operator manually sets a CA env var, though it is an env/config gap rather than a logic bug.

confirmed.title: Raw upstream API error body forwarded to the LLM
confirmed.evidence: On non-ok responses the first 200 chars of resp.text() are interpolated into the thrown Error and surfaced to the model. TheHive error bodies can echo request data or internal details; the 200-char slice is the only guard and no field allowlisting is done.
confirmed.recommendation: Log the full body to stderr only; return a generic 'TheHive API error: <status>' to the tool caller, or scrub/allowlist fields before forwarding.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. Lines 52-53: on non-ok responses the first 200 chars of resp.text() are interpolated into the thrown Error message (`TheHive API error: ${resp.status} ${text}`), which propagates up to the tool result and is surfaced to the model. The 200-char slice is the only guard; no field allowlisting or scrubbing. TheHive error bodies can echo request data. Low/security is appropriate — minor info-exposure to the model, not to an external party.

confirmed.title: No automated tests and no per-tool error wrapping
confirmed.evidence: scripts only contains build/dev/start; there is no test script or any test files. Tool handlers call thehiveApi directly with no try/catch, so a thrown Error propagates as an MCP protocol error rather than a structured { isError: true, content } tool result, giving the model a less actionable failure.
confirmed.recommendation: Add a thin handler wrapper that catches errors and returns { isError: true, content:[{type:'text',text:...}] }, add a 'test' script, and add at least contract tests for schema validation plus a mocked-fetch happy/error path.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed both halves. package.json scripts (lines 7-11) only define build/dev/start — no test script. No test files exist for thehive-mcp (the only *.test.* hits are inside node_modules/zod and fast-uri). All eight tool handlers call thehiveApi directly with no try/catch, so a thrown Error propagates as an MCP protocol error rather than a structured { isError: true, content } result. The original 'medium' label in the dimension field is design-level; the issue is real but I am rating it low because each sub-issue is itself low/cleanliness severity and the server otherwise functions. The cited line package.json:7 is the start of the scripts block — accurate.

confirmed.title: thehiveApi return type is unknown with no response typing
confirmed.evidence: thehiveApi returns Promise<unknown> and every caller passes the result straight to formatResponse; there are no interfaces for Case/Observable/Alert/Job responses, so callers get no type safety on TheHive payload shapes.
confirmed.recommendation: Introduce minimal response interfaces (or a generic <T>) for the entities actually consumed, even if only for documentation and future field extraction.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. thehiveApi is declared Promise<unknown> (line 40, signature spans 36-40) and every caller passes the result straight to formatResponse (which itself takes unknown). No Case/Observable/Alert/Job interfaces exist in the file. Callers get no type safety on payload shapes. Nit is correct. Note: line 36 cited in the finding is the function-declaration start; the unknown return type is on line 40 — corrected.

confirmed.title: Hardcoded TLP/PAP=2 and ioc=true with no override
confirmed.evidence: create-case forces tlp:2/pap:2 and add-observable forces tlp:2/pap:2 plus ioc:true. Marking every added observable as an IOC and pinning AMBER may be wrong for non-IOC context observables (e.g. an internal filename) and pollutes downstream Cortex/sighting workflows.
confirmed.recommendation: Expose optional tlp/pap/ioc parameters (defaulting to the current values) on create-case and add-observable so the model can classify accurately.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. create-case hardcodes tlp:2/pap:2 (lines 101-102) with no schema params for them. add-observable hardcodes tlp:2, pap:2, ioc:true (lines 149-151) with no override params in its zod schema (lines 137-142). Marking every added observable ioc:true and pinning AMBER is wrong for non-IOC context observables (e.g. an internal filename, which is an allowed data_type) and can pollute downstream Cortex/sighting workflows. Nit/design is appropriate.

confirmed.title: get-attack-patterns ignores the `tactic` parameter (filter never implemented)
confirmed.evidence: The tool is described as 'List MITRE ATT&CK techniques, optionally filtered by tactic' with a `tactic` param 'Filter by kill chain phase'. But the only use of `tactic` is `search: search || tactic || null` — it is coalesced into the free-text `search` field, and is completely ignored whenever `search` is also provided. There is no kill-chain-phase / x_mitre filter applied to the GraphQL query at all.
confirmed.recommendation: Implement a real tactic filter — add a FilterGroup on killChainPhases.phase_name (e.g. filter key `killChainPhases.phase_name`) and pass it via a $filters: FilterGroup argument, the same pattern already used in search-indicators (lines 71-99). Pass search and tactic as independent arguments rather than coalescing them. At minimum, if the filter cannot be supported, correct the tool/param descriptions to match actual behavior.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading lines 186-219. Tool described (line 189) as 'optionally filtered by tactic' with a tactic param (line 192) 'Filter by kill chain phase'. The GraphQL query (lines 197-214) accepts only $search and $first — it has no FilterGroup and no killChainPhases / x_mitre filter.
