The handler's only use of tactic is line 215: `{ search: search || tactic || null, first: limit }`, coalescing tactic into the free-text search and dropping it entirely whenever search is also provided. The advertised filter behavior is genuinely unimplemented, so callers get silently wrong results. High severity is appropriate for a documented capability that does not function.

confirmed.title: search-indicators / get-indicator query observables, not indicators (name/description mismatch)
confirmed.evidence: Both tools are named/described around 'indicators (IOCs)' but query `stixCyberObservables`/`stixCyberObservable`, which in OpenCTI are observables — a distinct entity type from Indicators (the STIX pattern objects). A user searching for an 'indicator' by name/pattern will silently get observables. The `entity_type` filter values in the description ('IPv4-Addr, Domain-Name, StixFile, Url') confirm these are observable types.
confirmed.recommendation: Either rename/redescribe the tools to reference 'observables' (matching stixCyberObservables/stixCyberObservable), or query the `indicators` root field when actual Indicator entities are intended. Clarify in the descriptions that observables (IOC values) and indicators (STIX patterns) are distinct so callers know which they are querying.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. search-indicators (line 62, described 'Search OpenCTI threat indicators (IOCs)') queries the `stixCyberObservables` root field (line 78) and selects `observable_value`. get-indicator (line 107, 'Get full details of an OpenCTI indicator') queries `stixCyberObservable` (line 114). In OpenCTI's model, StixCyberObservables (IPv4-Addr, Domain-Name, StixFile, Url — exactly the entity_type values in the line-67 description) are distinct from Indicators (STIX pattern objects, queried via the `indicators` root field). Notably get-indicator already nests an `indicators` sub-field (lines 133-143), underscoring that the top-level entity it fetches is an observable, not an indicator. The naming/description genuinely conflate the two STIX concepts, so a user searching 'indicators' silently gets observables. Medium is fair: results are returned and useful, but for a different entity type than advertised.

confirmed.title: No TLS trust handling for self-signed OpenCTI cert; default URL will fail TLS
confirmed.evidence: CLAUDE.md states OpenCTI is served behind a self-signed cert at soc.homelab.local. The code uses plain `fetch`/`fetchWithRetry` over HTTPS with no custom CA or https.Agent. Against a self-signed cert this throws (UNABLE_TO_VERIFY_LEAF_SIGNATURE) unless callers globally set NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 — which disables TLS verification process-wide and is insecure. The code default `https://localhost:4000` also mismatches .env.example (`https://soc.homelab.local/opencti`).
confirmed.recommendation: Support trusting the SOC CA explicitly — document NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS with the soc-tls-cert CA, or construct a per-request https.Agent that trusts the SOC CA — instead of relying on a process-wide NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 bypass. Align the in-code default URL with .env.example (or drop the fallback and fail fast).
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed on both counts. (1) CLAUDE.md states the stack runs behind a self-signed cert (soc-tls-cert) at soc.homelab.local. graphql() uses the shared fetchWithRetry over HTTPS (lines 35-42) and the health probe uses plain fetch (line 302); a grep found no https.Agent, NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS, rejectUnauthorized, or NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED handling anywhere in index.ts or shared/src/index.ts. Against a self-signed cert Node fetch throws UNABLE_TO_VERIFY_LEAF_SIGNATURE unless TLS verification is globally disabled, which is insecure. (2) The in-code default `https://localhost:4000` (line 22) does mismatch .env.example's `https://soc.homelab.local/opencti` (.env.example:2). Severity medium is reasonable: it is a design/operability gap (self-signed CA trust) rather than an active vuln, and OPENCTI_URL is expected to be set in normal operation.

confirmed.title: Connectivity probe duplicates AbortController/timeout logic instead of reusing fetchWithRetry
confirmed.evidence: main() hand-rolls an AbortController + setTimeout + .finally(clearTimeout) for the /health probe (lines 300-305), duplicating the timeout machinery already provided by the shared `fetchWithRetry`. It also bypasses the shared retry behavior and sends the Bearer token to an endpoint (/health) that is typically unauthenticated.
confirmed.recommendation: Reuse fetchWithRetry (or add a shared probeHealth helper in @matchbox/mcp-shared) for the connectivity check so timeout/retry logic lives in one place, and drop the Authorization header from the unauthenticated /health probe.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. main() (lines 297-309) hand-rolls `new AbortController()`, `setTimeout(... 5_000)`, and `.finally(() => clearTimeout(timer))` (lines 300-305) for the /health probe, duplicating the timeout/abort machinery already centralized in fetchWithRetry (shared/src/index.ts:18-46). It also bypasses the shared single-retry behavior and includes the Authorization Bearer header (line 303) on the /health endpoint, which is typically unauthenticated. A cleanliness nit; low severity is appropriate.

confirmed.title: `type` filter value passed through with no validation or enum constraint
confirmed.evidence: `type: z.string().optional()` is forwarded directly into the GraphQL filter `{ key: 'entity_type', values: [type] }`. This is not an injection risk (it is a bound variable), but any arbitrary string is accepted; an invalid type silently yields empty results rather than a helpful error. Same pattern for `relationship_type` in get-relationships.
confirmed.recommendation: Constrain `type` to a z.enum() of supported observable types (IPv4-Addr, Domain-Name, StixFile, Url, etc.) and `relationship_type` to a z.enum() of supported relationship types, or document and validate the accepted values so invalid input fails fast with a clear message instead of returning empty results.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. `type: z.string().optional()` (line 67) is forwarded directly into the GraphQL filter `{ key: 'entity_type', values: [type] }` (line 73) with no enum constraint. As the finding notes, this is not an injection risk because it is a bound GraphQL variable, but any arbitrary string is accepted and an invalid type yields an empty result set rather than a clear error. The same unconstrained pattern applies to relationship_type in get-relationships (line 252, used at line 288). Low/cleanliness severity is correct.

confirmed.title: safeId comment claims path-traversal protection but IDs are GraphQL variables, not paths
confirmed.evidence: Comment: 'Validate ID parameters to prevent path traversal'. The validated IDs are only ever passed as bound GraphQL `$id` variables (e.g. line 146, 240), never interpolated into a URL path or query string, so there is no path-traversal vector to defend against. The regex is reasonable defense-in-depth but the rationale is inaccurate.
confirmed.recommendation: Update the comment to describe the real intent, e.g. 'reject malformed entity IDs / defense-in-depth input validation', rather than claiming path-traversal protection.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. Line 30 comment reads 'Validate ID parameters to prevent path traversal'. Every safeId-validated value (indicator_id, observable_id, connector_id, entity_id) is only ever passed as a bound GraphQL variable — e.g. `{ id: indicator_id }` at line 146, `{ id: observable_id, ... }` at line 240, `{ id: entity_id, ... }` at line 287 — never interpolated into a URL path or query string. There is no path-traversal vector here; the regex is reasonable defense-in-depth input validation but the stated rationale is inaccurate. Nit is the right severity (comment-only accuracy issue).

confirmed.title: GraphQL response type is `unknown` and never narrowed before formatting
confirmed.evidence: `graphql()` returns `Promise<unknown>` and each tool passes the raw result straight to `formatResponse`. There is no typed model of the OpenCTI responses (edges/node shapes), so field renames or null nodes are not caught at compile time and the output passed to the LLM is untyped/unshaped.
confirmed.recommendation: Define response interfaces or zod schemas per query and have each tool extract/normalize the relevant fields before formatting, improving compile-time safety and producing more compact, predictable LLM output.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. graphql() is declared `Promise<unknown>` (line 34) and returns `result.data` (line 53) typed as unknown. Each tool passes the raw result straight into formatResponse (lines 102, 148, 182, 217, 242, 292) with no interface, zod schema, or runtime narrowing of the edges/node shapes. Field renames or null nodes would not be caught at compile time, and the LLM receives the raw, unshaped OpenCTI JSON. Design/cleanliness issue; low severity is appropriate.

confirmed.title: GraphQL error objects stringified into thrown message (up to 500 chars) may leak schema internals
confirmed.evidence: `throw new Error(\`GraphQL errors: ${JSON.stringify(result.errors).slice(0, 500)}\`)`. The full error array (which can include resolver paths, stack-like extensions, and internal type names) is surfaced verbatim to the LLM/caller. Low risk in a homelab but it exposes backend internals.
confirmed.recommendation: Log the full error array to stderr for debugging and return only a sanitized, generic message to the caller (e.g. the first error's `message` field), avoiding verbatim disclosure of resolver paths and internal schema details.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. Line 51: `throw new Error(`GraphQL errors: ${JSON.stringify(result.errors).slice(0, 500)}`)`. The entire errors array is serialized verbatim (capped at 500 chars) and surfaced to the caller/LLM; GraphQL error objects routinely include resolver paths, extensions, and internal type names. Note this is a deliberate truncation to 500 chars, so the exposure is bounded, and the same pattern bounds the HTTP-error path to 200 chars at line 45. Genuine information-exposure concern but low risk in a homelab, so low severity is right.

confirmed.title: .env.example OPENCTI_URL diverges from in-code default
confirmed.evidence: .env.example sets `OPENCTI_URL=https://soc.homelab.local/opencti` while the code fallback (index.ts:22) is `https://localhost:4000`. If the env var is ever unset, the server silently targets a different host than documented.
confirmed.recommendation: Make the in-code default match the documented value, or remove the misleading fallback and fail fast when OPENCTI_URL is unset, mirroring the existing OPENCTI_TOKEN required-check at lines 25-28.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. .env.example:2 sets `OPENCTI_URL=https://soc.homelab.local/opencti` while the in-code fallback at index.ts:22 is `https://localhost:4000`. If OPENCTI_URL is unset, the server silently targets a host different from the documented one. This is the same divergence underlying finding 3's second point; tracked here as a standalone consistency nit. Nit severity is correct.

confirmed.title: No request-level pagination cursor; large result sets silently truncated at formatResponse layer
confirmed.evidence: Queries return `pageInfo { globalCount }` but never `endCursor`/`hasNextPage`, and tools expose only `limit` (max 100) with no `after` cursor. When results exceed MAX_RESPONSE_CHARS (50000) the shared formatResponse truncates mid-JSON (shared/src/index.ts:52), producing invalid/partial JSON to the LLM with no way to fetch the next page.
confirmed.recommendation: Add an `after` cursor parameter and return endCursor/hasNextPage in pageInfo so callers can paginate, and/or change formatResponse to truncate at the array/edge level (drop whole edges) rather than slicing mid-structure, so output stays valid JSON.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. Every list query returns only `pageInfo { globalCount }` (lines 91, 177, 212, 283) — a grep found no endCursor, hasNextPage, or `after` anywhere — and tools expose only `limit` (max 100) with no cursor parameter. When serialized output exceeds MAX_RESPONSE_CHARS (default 50000, shared line 12), formatResponse does a raw `json.slice(0, maxChars)` (shared/src/index.ts:51-52), which truncates mid-JSON and yields structurally invalid output to the LLM with no way to fetch subsequent pages. The cited file:line points at the search-indicators return (line 102), which is a fair representative anchor for the missing-cursor pattern (the truncation itself lives in shared/src/index.ts:52). Design issue, low severity is reasonable.

confirmed.title: TLS certificate verification disabled process-wide via NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0
confirmed.evidence: vite.config.ts sets `process.env.NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED = '0'` unconditionally, and .env.example:26 documents `NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0`. This is a global Node flag: it disables TLS chain/hostname verification for EVERY outbound HTTPS fetch in the process (Wazuh https://localhost:55000, Wazuh dashboard https://localhost:5601, and any future upstream), not just the intended self-signed localhost services. The credential-bearing Wazuh auth call (Basic auth at wazuh/+server.ts:17) and JWT calls then run over an unauthenticated TLS channel, enabling MITM/credential interception if name resolution or routing is ever tampered with.
confirmed.recommendation: Remove the unconditional `NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED = '0'` line from vite.config.ts and drop the var from .env.example/.env. Pin trust per-request instead: set NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS to the SOC self-signed CA, or pass an undici Agent with `ca:` set on the credential-bearing fetches (wazuh getToken/wazuhFetch). This mirrors the CA-cert approach already in CHANGELOG.md:12/16. Note the prod vector is the .env var, not vite.config.ts, so fix both.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading the file: vite.config.ts:5 sets `process.env.NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED = '0'` unconditionally, and .env.example:26 documents `NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0`. This is the documented global Node flag that disables TLS chain/hostname verification for all outbound HTTPS in the process. The credential-bearing Wazuh Basic-auth call is at wazuh/+server.ts:17-19 and runs over HTTPS to localhost:55000, so verification is effectively off for it. CHANGELOG.md:12 confirms the proper per-CA approach (ConfigMap-mounted CA cert) was already adopted for OpenCTI/Wazuh server-side, so the recommendation is consistent with project direction. Nuance: vite.config.ts only executes during dev/build, not in the adapter-node production server; at production runtime the disabling comes from the .env var (.env.example:26) which the Node server reads. Either way the flag is real and global. Severity high is appropriate for a credential-bearing channel.

confirmed.title: No authentication/authorization on /api/* SOC proxy routes
confirmed.evidence: None of the GET handlers (wazuh, thehive, opencti, cortex, grafana, health) perform any auth, origin, or client-identity check (confirmed: no hooks.server.ts, no locals.user, no getClientAddress/origin checks anywhere under src). Anyone who can reach the dashboard's Node port can call /api/wazuh and receive full SOC internals: every agent's id, name, status, OS+version, IP address, and registration date (wazuh/+server.ts:90-98), plus alert/case/indicator/connector counts. This is reconnaissance-grade data for an attacker on the LAN.
confirmed.recommendation: Add src/hooks.server.ts with a `handle` that enforces auth on /api/* (session cookie, Traefik forward-auth/SSO, or at minimum a shared bearer token), and/or bind the adapter-node server to localhost behind the authenticated Traefik ingress (soc.homelab.local). Add same-origin/CSRF checks before introducing any state-changing routes (all current handlers are GET-only).
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed independently. There is no src/hooks.server.ts (find returned nothing). Grep across src found no locals.user, no getClientAddress, no origin/CSRF check; the only Authorization headers are the outbound ones to upstreams (cortex:23, thehive:12, opencti:12, wazuh:19/33). All six routes export only an unauthenticated GET (wazuh:40, thehive:27, opencti:25, grafana:7, cortex:8, health:13). The Wazuh GET returns full agent inventory (id, name, status, os, ip, version, registration) built at wazuh/+server.ts:90-98, plus alert/case/indicator/connector counts. Any client reaching the Node port gets SOC reconnaissance data. High is appropriate.

confirmed.title: Upstream error messages leaked verbatim to the browser
confirmed.evidence: All five service routes return `{ error: (err as Error).message }` to the client (wazuh:128, opencti:69, grafana:30, cortex:44, thehive:65). Raw upstream/fetch error strings can disclose internal hostnames, ports, TLS/connection details, and Wazuh/Grafana API status semantics to an unauthenticated caller, aiding fingerprinting of the internal SOC topology.
confirmed.recommendation: Log the detailed error server-side (console.error) and return a generic body to the client, e.g. `{ status: 'error', error: 'upstream unavailable' }`. Apply uniformly across all five routes; a shared helper (see the duplicated-boilerplate finding) would let this be fixed once.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. All five service routes return the raw error message to the client: wazuh/+server.ts:128 `{ error: (err as Error).message, status: 'error' }`, opencti:69, grafana:30, cortex:44, thehive:65. Error strings include upstream status codes and connection details (e.g. wazuh:23 `Wazuh auth failed: ${resp.status}`, wazuh:36 `Wazuh API ${resp.status}`, plus fetch/DNS errors), which can fingerprint internal SOC topology. Combined with the no-auth finding this is reachable by any LAN client. Medium is defensible (it is low-impact info disclosure on its own but amplified by the missing auth).

confirmed.title: TheHive count query response type is likely wrong (array vs number)
confirmed.evidence: thehiveCount() POSTs a query pipeline `[{_name:listCase},{_name:'count'}]` and returns `resp.json() as Promise<number>`. TheHive's /api/v1/query endpoint returns results as a JSON array even for aggregations; a `count` operation typically yields `[N]`, not a bare `N`. If so, openCases/alerts (thehive:59-60) would render as an array/`[object]` rather than the integer count.
confirmed.recommendation: Confirm the actual TheHive 5.x /api/v1/query count shape, then unwrap defensively regardless: `const r = await resp.json(); const n = Array.isArray(r) ? r[0] : r; return Number.isFinite(n) ? n : 0;`. Add a runtime finite-number guard before returning.
confirmed.verifyNote: Code confirmed: thehive/+server.ts:8-25 thehiveCount() POSTs `query:[{_name:listOp},{_name:'count'}]` and returns `resp.json() as Promise<number>` (line 24); the result feeds openCases/alerts at lines 59-60. The unvalidated cast is real. However, I cannot independently confirm the upstream claim that TheHive 5.x /api/v1/query count returns `[N]` rather than a bare number without hitting the live API or docs (out of scope here). The bug is plausible but its existence depends on unverified runtime behavior, so I downgrade the severity from medium to low: it is at worst a display defect (openCases renders wrong), not a security/data-integrity issue.

confirmed.title: Default fallback credentials/URLs baked into source
confirmed.evidence: WAZUH_USER defaults to 'wazuh-wui' (wazuh:6) and all routes default URLs to localhost ports. While not a secret leak, the hardcoded default username plus the documented insecure TLS default nudges insecure deployment. CORTEX_API_KEY is compared against the literal sentinel 'CHANGE_ME' (cortex:20), implying a known placeholder may ship in configs.
confirmed.recommendation: Fail fast at startup when required env vars (passwords/tokens, and ideally URLs) are unset in production rather than silently falling back to localhost/wazuh-wui defaults, preventing accidental auth/proxy against unintended targets. Keep the 'CHANGE_ME' guard but also treat an unset key as a hard error in prod.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. wazuh/+server.ts:6 `WAZUH_USER = env.WAZUH_API_USER || 'wazuh-wui'`; all routes default URLs to localhost ports (wazuh:5, thehive:5, opencti:5, grafana:5, cortex:5, health:6-10). cortex/+server.ts:20 compares CORTEX_API_KEY against the literal 'CHANGE_ME' sentinel. No actual secret values are present in source (WAZUH_PASS/THEHIVE_API_KEY/OPENCTI_TOKEN/CORTEX_API_KEY all default to ''). Username default + the documented insecure TLS default nudge insecure deployment. Low is appropriate.

confirmed.title: Unvalidated upstream JSON cast directly to typed shapes
confirmed.evidence: Every route casts `resp.json()` straight to a concrete interface via `as` (wazuh:50-77, opencti:46-54, thehive:33/55, grafana:15, cortex:15) with no runtime validation. If an upstream returns an unexpected shape (e.g. partial outage, error body with 200), accesses like `stats.data.affected_items` (wazuh:82) or `data.about.version` (opencti:57) throw an uncaught TypeError that is then swallowed into a generic 502, masking the real cause.
confirmed.recommendation: Add lightweight runtime validation (zod or manual guards) for the fields actually consumed, and guard the top-level optional chains (e.g. `stats?.data?.affected_items ?? []`, `data?.about?.version`). Centralize parsing in a shared fetch helper.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. Every route casts `resp.json()` straight to a concrete interface via `as` with no runtime validation: wazuh:50-77 (and dereferences like stats.data.affected_items at :82, info.data.affected_items at :121), opencti:46-54 (data.about.version at :57), thehive:24/33/55, grafana:15, cortex:15. Some optional chaining exists (e.g. agent fields wazuh:94-97, sca?.data at :104), but the top-level data objects are unguarded, so an unexpected 200-with-error body throws a TypeError swallowed into the generic 502 at the route's catch. Low/cleanliness is appropriate.

confirmed.title: Duplicated proxy boilerplate across five routes with no shared helper
confirmed.evidence: Each +server.ts independently reimplements env-var reading, fetch-with-timeout, ok-check, JSON parse, and the identical `catch { return json({error,status:'error'},{status:502}) }` block. No shared server-side fetch utility exists (lib/api.ts is client-side only). This duplication makes it easy to fix a security issue (e.g. TLS/error-leakage/timeout) in one route but miss another.
confirmed.recommendation: Extract a `src/lib/server/upstream.ts` helper (timeout, CA-pinned undici dispatcher, ok-check, safe generic error mapping, optional schema validation) and route all five +server.ts handlers through it so security fixes apply uniformly.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. src/lib/api.ts is client-side only (it calls /api/* via browser fetch and updates Svelte stores — fetchHealth/fetchMetrics/startPolling), so there is no server-side shared helper. Each +server.ts independently reimplements env reading, fetch-with-timeout (AbortSignal.timeout), ok-check, JSON parse, and the identical `catch { return json({ error, status: 'error' }, { status: 502 }) }` block (wazuh:126-131, opencti:67-72, grafana:28-33, cortex:42-47, thehive:63-68). The duplication makes uniform security fixes (TLS pinning, error mapping, timeouts) error-prone. The cited file:line points to where the helper is recommended to live, not a defect site, but the underlying observation is accurate. Low/design appropriate.

confirmed.title: Health check treats only 302 specially; other auth-redirect/2xx-on-error cases mishandled
confirmed.evidence: health/+server.ts:22 marks a service 'online' when `resp.ok || resp.status === 302`. The wazuh entry probes the Dashboard root (https://localhost:5601) which commonly returns 200/302 to a login page even when the backend OpenSearch is unhealthy, and other services may return 301/307/401. The single-value 302 heuristic is brittle and can both false-positive (login page up, service broken) and false-negative (301/307).
confirmed.recommendation: Probe a real per-service health endpoint (Wazuh API status via WAZUH_API_URL rather than the Dashboard root), and classify 2xx plus only the specific expected redirect code(s) as healthy, otherwise degraded. Consider `redirect: 'manual'` so the status code is meaningful.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. health/+server.ts:22 sets status to 'online' when `resp.ok || resp.status === 302`, else 'degraded'. The wazuh entry probes the Dashboard root https://localhost:5601 (endpoints map, health:6), which commonly returns 200/302 to a login page even when the OpenSearch backend is unhealthy; other services could return 301/307/401. The single 302 heuristic can both false-positive and false-negative. Note: by default fetch follows redirects, so a 302 only surfaces here if the target returns 302 without a Location or with redirect handling that leaves status 302 — the heuristic is brittle either way. Low/functionality appropriate.

confirmed.title: Module-level Wazuh JWT cache is fine for single-tenant but undocumented and not reset on auth failure
confirmed.evidence: jwtToken/tokenExpiry are module-scoped singletons (wazuh:9-11) with a fixed 850s TTL. If the cached token is revoked server-side before expiry, every wazuhFetch fails for up to ~14 minutes because the token is only refreshed on TTL, not on a 401. Acceptable for a single-user SOC but fragile.
confirmed.recommendation: On a 401/403 from wazuhFetch, null out jwtToken/tokenExpiry and retry getToken() once before failing. Add a brief comment documenting the single-tenant module-scope cache assumption.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. wazuh/+server.ts:9-11 declares module-scoped `jwtToken`/`tokenExpiry` with `TOKEN_TTL_MS = 850_000` (~14 min). getToken() (13-28) only refreshes on TTL expiry; wazuhFetch (30-38) throws `Wazuh API ${resp.status}` on any non-ok including 401/403 without clearing the cached token. A token revoked before TTL would make every call fail until expiry. Acceptable for single-user SOC. Nit is appropriate.

confirmed.title: GrafanaEmbed error detection is a fragile 10s timeout with no onerror handler
confirmed.evidence: The iframe has only `onload={() => { loading = false; loadError = false; }}` and no `onerror`. Error state is inferred purely by a 10s timeout: `setTimeout(() => { if (loading) { loadError = true; loading = false; } }, 10_000)`. A cross-origin iframe fires `onload` even when Grafana returns an HTTP error page or a login redirect, so a broken/unauthenticated Grafana will show as 'loaded' (blank/login frame) rather than the error overlay; conversely a slow-but-healthy Grafana shows a false 'Could not reach Grafana' error after 10s even though it later loads. Because the iframe is cross-origin, JS truly cannot detect content failure, so the timeout heuristic is the only option but it is mis-tuned and not paired with onload-resets-the-timer logic.
confirmed.recommendation: Add a code comment acknowledging the cross-origin detection limitation. Optionally drive the error overlay from the existing /api/grafana reachability endpoint (routes/api/grafana/+server.ts confirmed to exist) instead of a blind timer, and/or raise the timeout. Low priority.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. The iframe (lines 92-97) has only onload={() => { loading = false; loadError = false; }} and no onerror. Error state is inferred purely by the 10s setTimeout in the $effect at lines 41-48 (`if (loading) { loadError = true; loading = false; }`, 10_000). The finding's own analysis is accurate: a cross-origin iframe fires onload even for HTTP error/login pages, and JS genuinely cannot inspect cross-origin content, so the timeout heuristic is the only client-side option. The line range was slightly off (effect starts at 40/41, not 41-48 exactly; second range 92-98 vs cited 92-98 is fine). Downgraded medium->low: this is a local homelab single-user dashboard, the failure modes (false 'could not reach' after 10s, or blank login frame shown as loaded) are cosmetic/diagnostic rather than functional data loss, and the finding admits the core limitation is unavoidable.

confirmed.title: Duplicated polling: overview metrics fetched by both global poller and each service page
confirmed.evidence: `startPolling()` in +layout.svelte runs `fetchAllMetrics()` (wazuh, grafana, opencti, thehive, cortex) every 60s for the whole app lifetime. Each per-service page (e.g. wazuh/+page.svelte) ALSO runs its own `setInterval(fetchData, 60_000)` hitting the same `/api/wazuh`. When viewing a service page, that endpoint is therefore polled twice on overlapping timers (the global metrics poll and the page poll), doubling backend load and the chance of overlapping in-flight requests against the rate-limited SOC APIs.
confirmed.recommendation: Pick one ownership model: either have pages read from metricsStore (already populated by the global poller) instead of fetching, or pause the global metrics poll for the active service. Add visibilitychange gating to pause when the tab is hidden.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. startPolling() (api.ts:42-53) calls fetchAllMetrics() which fires fetchMetrics for wazuh/grafana/opencti/thehive/cortex (lines 32-40) on a 60s setInterval (line 47). It is invoked once from +layout.svelte onMount (lines 10-16) for the app lifetime. Each service page independently runs onMount(() => { fetchData(); const interval = setInterval(fetchData, 60_000); ...}) hitting the same /api/{service} endpoint (wazuh +page.svelte:49-53, identical pattern in thehive/cortex/opencti). So while viewing a service page, /api/{service} is polled by two overlapping 60s timers. Real duplication; severity low is correct given it is a single-user local dashboard.

confirmed.title: Polling does not pause when tab is hidden or on fetch failure (no backoff)
confirmed.evidence: `setInterval(fetchHealth, 30_000)` and `setInterval(fetchAllMetrics, 60_000)` run unconditionally. There is no `document.visibilitychange` gating, so a backgrounded tab keeps hammering the SOC APIs indefinitely, and there is no exponential backoff when a service is offline — a down service is retried at the same fixed cadence forever, generating constant failed requests and console noise.
confirmed.recommendation: Pause/resume intervals on visibilitychange; add simple exponential backoff (double interval to a cap) after consecutive failures, reset on success.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. startPolling() sets setInterval(fetchHealth, 30_000) and setInterval(fetchAllMetrics, 60_000) unconditionally (api.ts:46-47). No document.visibilitychange handling exists anywhere, and fetchHealth/fetchMetrics (lines 4-30) just console.warn on failure and retry at the same fixed cadence with no backoff. A backgrounded tab keeps polling and a down service is retried forever at the same rate. Severity low is appropriate (design/efficiency, single-user local app).

confirmed.title: No accessibility: clickable card not keyboard-operable, a11y warnings suppressed
confirmed.evidence: The whole card is a `<div onclick={navigate}>` with the Svelte a11y warnings explicitly silenced: `<!-- svelte-ignore a11y_click_events_have_key_events -->` and `<!-- svelte-ignore a11y_no_static_element_interactions -->`. The card cannot be reached by Tab or activated by Enter/Space, so keyboard and screen-reader users cannot navigate to a service from the overview. The nested external-link <button> inside the clickable div also nests interactive controls.
confirmed.recommendation: Make the card a real <a href={`/${service.id}`}> for navigation and move the external-launch button to a sibling outside the anchor (avoid nested interactive controls), or add role=link + tabindex=0 + keydown(Enter/Space). Remove the svelte-ignore suppressions once fixed.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. ServiceCard.svelte lines 64-65 carry the two svelte-ignore suppressions (a11y_click_events_have_key_events, a11y_no_static_element_interactions) and line 66 is `<div class="card" onclick={navigate}>`. There is no tabindex, role, or keydown handler, so the card is not Tab-reachable nor Enter/Space-activatable. It also nests an interactive <button class="open-btn" onclick={openExternal}> (lines 75-82) inside the click handler div. Genuine keyboard/screen-reader inaccessibility for the primary overview navigation. Medium is fair.

confirmed.title: Icon-only buttons/links lack accessible names (aria-label); status conveyed by color/animation only
confirmed.evidence: Reload/external-link/open buttons render only a lucide icon with a `title` (not announced reliably) and no `aria-label`/visible text. StatusDot is a bare `<span class="dot">` with no text alternative or `role`/`aria-label`, so status (online/degraded/offline) is communicated purely via color + blink animation — inaccessible to screen readers and colorblind users. grep confirms zero `aria-*`, `role=`, `alt=` attributes in the entire src tree.
confirmed.recommendation: Add aria-label to all icon-only controls; give StatusDot an aria-label or sr-only text (e.g. 'Wazuh: online') plus a non-color cue (shape/icon).
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed on all three. GrafanaEmbed reload button (63-65) and external-link <a> (66-74) render only a lucide icon with title= and no aria-label. ServiceCard open-btn (75-82) likewise icon-only with title only. StatusDot is a bare <span class="dot"> (5-11) with no text alternative or role/aria-label, so online/degraded/offline is conveyed solely by color + blink. A repo-wide grep for aria-*, role=, and alt= returned zero matches (exit code 1) in the entire src tree, confirming the no-accessible-names claim. Low is appropriate.

confirmed.title: Three perpetual CSS animations ignore prefers-reduced-motion
confirmed.evidence: `.scanline` runs an 8s infinite top-to-bottom sweep across the whole viewport (app.css:75), and StatusDot uses `animation: blink` for degraded (2s) and offline (1s) states, all infinite. There is no `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)` block anywhere, so users who request reduced motion still get a constantly moving scanline and blinking dots — a vestibular/accessibility concern and needless GPU churn.
confirmed.recommendation: Add a `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)` block that disables animation on .scanline and .dot (set animation: none / static state).
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. .scanline (app.css:67-78) runs `animation: scanline 8s linear infinite` sweeping top:0 -> top:100vh (keyframes 79-82). StatusDot uses `animation: blink 2s ease-in-out infinite` for .degraded (line 28) and `animation: blink 1s ease-in-out infinite` for .offline (line 33), both infinite. grep for prefers-reduced-motion across src returned zero matches (exit 1). Real vestibular/accessibility gap. Note: the cited app.css line for the scanline animation property is line 75 (within the 67-94 range given), accurate. Severity low/trivial-effort is right.

confirmed.title: No +error.svelte route boundary
confirmed.evidence: There is no `src/routes/+error.svelte`. An unhandled error in a load/render path (or a 404 to an unknown route) falls back to SvelteKit's default unstyled error page, breaking out of the MatchBox shell entirely. Confirmed by `find` returning no +error.svelte / +page.ts / +layout.ts.
confirmed.recommendation: Add a styled src/routes/+error.svelte that renders inside the app shell with a link back to Overview.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. A find across dashboard/src/routes for +error.svelte / +page.ts / +layout.ts / +page.server.ts returned nothing. The only layout is +layout.svelte (no load logic). With no +error.svelte, unhandled render/load errors and unknown routes fall back to SvelteKit's default unstyled error page, breaking out of the Header/Sidebar shell. The finding cites +layout.svelte:1-54 as the location for context, which is reasonable since the missing file would live alongside it. Severity low/trivial.

confirmed.title: Hardcoded service URLs duplicated across config, GrafanaEmbed, and per-service pages
confirmed.evidence: `http://localhost:3000` is hardcoded in buildSrc and externalHref in GrafanaEmbed even though config.ts already defines `dashboardUrl`/`port` for grafana. The wazuh page hardcodes `https://localhost:5601/app/wazuh` and the StatBox shows literal `localhost:55000`/`localhost:5601`. The same base URLs live in config.ts AND are re-typed in components, so a host/port change requires edits in multiple files and they can drift.
confirmed.recommendation: Source all external URLs from config.ts (single source) and derive the Grafana base host + dashboard paths from config rather than re-hardcoding in components.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. GrafanaEmbed hardcodes `http://localhost:3000` in buildSrc (line 16) and externalHref (line 22) despite config.ts defining grafana dashboardUrl 'http://localhost:3000' and port 3000 (lines 32-34). wazuh/+page.svelte hardcodes `https://localhost:5601/app/wazuh` (line 67) and StatBox literals 'localhost:55000' (line 116) and 'localhost:5601' (line 117). Other pages also hardcode their service URLs (thehive http://localhost:9000, opencti :4000, cortex :9001) that duplicate config.ts dashboardUrl values. Genuine duplication/drift risk. Low is correct.

confirmed.title: Version drift: sidebar shows v1.5.0 while package.json is 1.0.0
confirmed.evidence: Sidebar footer hardcodes `v1.5.0` but package.json declares `"version": "1.0.0"`. The displayed version is a hand-edited literal disconnected from the package version, so it is already wrong and will keep drifting.
confirmed.recommendation: Inject version from package.json via Vite define / import, or remove the footer version entirely.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. Sidebar.svelte:52 hardcodes v1.5.0; package.json:4 declares "version": "1.0.0". The displayed value is a disconnected hand-edited literal and is already wrong. Real but purely cosmetic on a local dashboard, so nit is more accurate than low.

confirmed.title: Five near-identical service pages duplicate fetch/poll/error scaffolding and styles
confirmed.evidence: thehive, cortex, opencti (and wazuh) each repeat the same `data/error/loading` $state, identical `fetchData()` with `setInterval(fetchData, 60_000)`, the same page-bar/action-btn/center-msg/info-card markup, and a copy-pasted ~40-line <style> block (page-bar, action-btn, center-msg, spinner all identical). Maintaining a style or behavior change means editing it in 4-5 files.
confirmed.recommendation: Extract a ServiceDetailLayout/ServicePage component (props: title, icon, endpoint, externalUrl + a stats slot) and move shared styles to app.css. Per-service pages become just the unique stats markup.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. thehive (132 lines), cortex (140), opencti (137), and wazuh (307) each repeat the same data/error/loading $state block, an identical async fetchData() with onMount setInterval(fetchData, 60_000), the same page-bar/action-btn/center-msg/info-card markup, and a copy-pasted ~30-40 line <style> block (page-bar, action-btn, center-msg, :global(.spinner) all identical across files; verified by grep). Actual line counts are 132/140/137/307, off by 1 from the cited 133/141/138/308. Real maintainability concern; low severity (cleanliness) is right.

confirmed.title: Duplicated iconMap and spin keyframes across components
confirmed.evidence: The `iconMap` (Shield/ChartColumn/Radar/ShieldAlert/Brain) is declared identically in both ServiceCard.svelte and Sidebar.svelte. The `@keyframes spin` is defined in app.css:84, again in GrafanaEmbed.svelte:208, and `:global(.spinner)` with its own spin is redeclared in wazuh/thehive/cortex/opencti pages.
confirmed.recommendation: Export a single iconMap from config.ts (or a shared module); keep one @keyframes spin + one shared .spinner rule in app.css and remove per-file copies.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. The identical iconMap (Shield, ChartColumn, Radar, ShieldAlert, Brain) is declared in ServiceCard.svelte:11-13 and Sidebar.svelte:8-10. @keyframes spin is defined in app.css:84 and again in GrafanaEmbed.svelte:208. :global(.spinner) with its own spin animation is redeclared in wazuh:306, thehive:131, cortex:139, opencti:136 (grep confirmed 5 copies of :global(.spinner)). All duplication claims accurate. nit is correct.

confirmed.title: Sidebar omits a11y suppression dependency and StatBox allows arbitrary inline color injection
confirmed.evidence: StatBox sets `style={color ? `color: ${color}` : ''}` from a `color` prop. Today all callers pass trusted CSS-var literals, but the pattern interpolates an arbitrary string directly into a style attribute; if a caller ever forwarded user/remote data this becomes a CSS-injection vector. It is also untyped beyond `string`.
confirmed.recommendation: Constrain color to a known variant enum ('accent'|'green'|'default') mapped to CSS classes instead of free-form inline style, centralizing theming and removing the theoretical surface. Drop the unsubstantiated Sidebar clause from the finding.
confirmed.verifyNote: Partially confirmed. StatBox.svelte:11 does interpolate the color prop straight into a style attribute: style={color ? `color: ${color}` : ''}. ServiceCard.svelte:87 passes color={stat.color}. All current callers pass trusted CSS-var literals or '' (verified across ServiceCard stats, wazuh/thehive/opencti/cortex pages), so the 'CSS-injection vector' framing is purely theoretical on this single-user local dashboard with no user/remote data path into color. The title's 'Sidebar omits a11y suppression dependency' clause is unclear/unsupported and does not correspond to anything I could verify in Sidebar.svelte. The real, verifiable part is the untyped free-form inline color. Downgraded low->nit: no actual injection surface exists today.

confirmed.title: Theme applied via JS on mount causes redundant FOUC-guard logic and effect placement
confirmed.evidence: The anti-FOUC inline script in app.html sets `document.body.style.background`, then +layout.svelte onMount clears it, and Header.svelte onMount separately re-applies `data-theme` from the store. The data-theme attribute is set in three places (inline script, toggleTheme, Header onMount) and the body background is set/cleared in three places (inline script, layout onMount, toggleTheme). This scattered, order-dependent logic is brittle: Header (a child) is responsible for applying the document-level theme attribute, which is the wrong layer and will silently break if Header is ever removed/reordered.
confirmed.recommendation: Centralize theme application in +layout.svelte (one $effect syncing data-theme from themeStore and managing body background), and have the inline script set only data-theme (let CSS vars drive background) to remove the set/clear dance.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. app.html:8-10 inline script sets documentElement data-theme AND body.style.background. +layout.svelte onMount (lines 10-16) clears document.body.style.background. Header.svelte onMount (lines 11-12) separately re-applies document.documentElement.setAttribute('data-theme', $themeStore). So data-theme is set in the inline script and in Header (and toggleTheme in stores), and body background is set in the inline script then cleared in the layout. The finding's core claim is accurate: a child component (Header) owns document-level theme application, which is the wrong layer and is order-dependent. Severity low (functionality/design, brittle but currently working).

confirmed.title: Status pages have no empty/zero-state distinction and 'note' fallback text is awkwardly concatenated
confirmed.evidence: When the backend returns `data.note` (e.g. missing API key), the UI renders `{data.note}. Set THEHIVE_API_KEY ...` — the trailing period is hardcoded after the interpolation, producing odd output if note already ends in punctuation, and the message mixes a server-provided string with a client-hardcoded instruction. There is also no distinct empty state: a successfully-reached service with zero cases/indicators looks identical to a never-populated one (just zeros).
confirmed.recommendation: Render the server note and the client hint as separate elements (no string concatenation); let one side own the full message. Optionally add a 'no data yet' empty state distinct from genuine zeros.
confirmed.verifyNote:
Confirmed. thehive +page.svelte:70-74 renders `

{data.note}. Set THEHIVE_API_KEY in dashboard/.env to see full metrics.

` with a hardcoded trailing period after the interpolation; opencti +page.svelte:74-78 is identical with OPENCTI_TOKEN. This mixes a server-provided string with a client-hardcoded instruction and would double-punctuate if note ends in '.'. Also confirmed no distinct empty state: a reached service with zero cases/indicators renders the same zeros as a never-populated one. Low is appropriate.

confirmed.title: Grafana iframe uses mix-blend-mode: multiply to fake light theme instead of native theme param
confirmed.evidence: In light mode the iframe gets `class:blend={$themeStore !== 'dark'}` applying `mix-blend-mode: multiply`. The src already appends `&theme=${grafanaTheme}` (line 16), so the blend hack double-applies a visual transform on top of Grafana's own theming, which can wash out colors and is fragile across Grafana versions.
confirmed.recommendation: Rely on Grafana's &theme=light and drop the mix-blend-mode hack (pick one approach). Verify the installed Grafana version honors the theme query param for embedded/kiosk panels.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. The iframe gets class:blend={$themeStore !== 'dark'} (line 95) which applies .frame-container iframe.blend { mix-blend-mode: multiply } (lines 171-173). The src already appends &theme=${grafanaTheme} where grafanaTheme is 'dark' or 'light' (lines 15-16). So in light mode both Grafana's native theme param AND the mix-blend-mode hack apply, a double transform that can wash out colors. Cited 91-98 covers the iframe element block (blend class is at line 95, not 91-98 exactly) and 171-173 is exact. nit is correct.

confirmed.title: ServiceCard fmt() and StatusDot status fallback duplicate the same '...'/'checking' default pattern
confirmed.evidence: The `health?.status ?? 'checking'` fallback is repeated in ServiceCard, Header, and Sidebar, and the `fmt` helper that maps null/undefined to '...' lives only in ServiceCard while the same '...' default is re-typed inline in every per-service StatBox (`String(data.version ?? '...')`). The default placeholders are scattered string literals.
confirmed.recommendation: Centralize the status fallback ('checking') and the '...' placeholder as shared constants/helpers so the not-loaded representation is consistent and changeable in one place.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed with a line-number correction. The `health?.status ?? 'checking'` fallback appears in ServiceCard.svelte:74, Header.svelte:49 (title attr) and :51 (StatusDot status prop), and Sidebar.svelte:32. The finding cited Header.svelte:48, but line 48 is `class="status-item"`; the actual fallback uses are lines 49 and 51. The fmt() helper mapping null/undefined to '...' lives only in ServiceCard.svelte:25-28, while the same '...' default is re-typed inline in per-service StatBoxes (e.g. wazuh:114 String(data.version ?? '...'), ServiceCard:41). Pattern duplication is real; nit is correct.

confirmed.title: opensearch-certs secret referenced by manager/opensearch but never created
confirmed.evidence: Manager StatefulSet mounts volume `opensearch-certs` (secret `opensearch-certs`), and `k8s/shared/opensearch.yaml:47-50` mounts the same secret into OpenSearch. Repo-wide grep finds NO manifest or script that creates this secret (no `kubectl create secret`, no openssl/cfssl cert generation in scripts/). ossec.conf expects /etc/ssl/opensearch/root-ca.pem, kirk.pem, kirk-key.pem. Without the secret, both the manager pod and OpenSearch pod fail to start (volume mount of a non-existent secret blocks the pod), so the entire SIEM never comes up.
confirmed.recommendation: Add a cert-provisioning step before shared+wazuh deploy: an openssl/cfssl script (or cert-manager Certificate) that produces root-ca.pem, esnode.pem/esnode-key.pem (with the K8s service SANs used in opensearch.yaml), and kirk.pem/kirk-key.pem, then `kubectl create secret generic opensearch-certs` in BOTH the shared and wazuh namespaces. Wire it into deploy-stack.sh deploy_shared() and deploy_wazuh().
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. deployment.yaml:135-137 defines volume `opensearch-certs` from secret `opensearch-certs`, mounted read-only at /etc/ssl/opensearch (lines 107-108). opensearch.yaml:47-50 mounts the same `opensearch-certs` secret at /usr/share/opensearch/config/certs. Repo-wide grep for `opensearch-certs` returns ONLY these three reference sites and zero creators (no `kubectl create secret`, no openssl/cfssl/cert-manager Certificate generating it; the only `kubectl create secret generic` in deploy-stack.sh:170 creates soc-opencti-secrets). opensearch-ca-cert.yaml only ships a placeholder ConfigMap (REPLACE_WITH_YOUR_OPENSEARCH_CA_CERTIFICATE) in opencti/wazuh namespaces, not the `opensearch-certs` secret, and is not even applied by deploy-stack.sh. ossec.conf (configmap-ossec.yaml:44-46) and opensearch.yaml:71-78 both expect PEMs (root-ca/kirk/esnode) from that secret. deploy_shared() runs the OpenSearch Helm install with --wait (line 87-89) and would block on the missing secret volume; deploy_wazuh() likewise. A missing referenced secret leaves pods stuck (volume cannot mount), so the SIEM never comes up. Severity critical is correct.

confirmed.title: wazuh-dashboard-certs secret referenced but never created
confirmed.evidence: Dashboard mounts volume `certs` from secret `wazuh-dashboard-certs` (readOnly) and opensearch_dashboards.yml (configmap-dashboard.yaml:16-18) requires dashboard-key.pem, dashboard.pem, root-ca.pem at that path with server.ssl.enabled: true. Repo-wide grep shows this secret is created by no manifest or script. The dashboard pod cannot schedule (missing secret volume) and would have no TLS material even if it did.
confirmed.recommendation: Create the `wazuh-dashboard-certs` secret with keys dashboard.pem, dashboard-key.pem, root-ca.pem (matching configmap-dashboard.yaml:16-18) — either generate via openssl + `kubectl create secret generic` in deploy-stack.sh, or issue via cert-manager (a selfsigned-issuer ClusterIssuer already exists in k8s/ingress/self-signed-issuer.yaml). Until then the dashboard deployment cannot run.
confirmed.verifyNote: CONFIRMED. deployment.yaml:135-137 defines volume `certs` from secret `wazuh-dashboard-certs` (mounted readOnly at /usr/share/wazuh-dashboard/config/certs, line 107-109, non-optional). configmap-dashboard.yaml:15-18 sets server.ssl.enabled: true and requires dashboard-key.pem, dashboard.pem, root-ca.pem at exactly that path. Repo-wide grep for `wazuh-dashboard-certs` returns only deployment.yaml:137 — no manifest, Helm values (k8s/wazuh/helm-values.yaml has no certs), or script creates it. deploy-stack.sh creates only soc-opencti-secrets; no openssl/cert generation for the dashboard; the referenced scripts/generate-tls-cert.sh does not exist. A non-optional secret volume means the pod cannot schedule without the secret. Note: deploy-stack.sh does not even apply dashboard/deployment.yaml today (wazuh section applies only manager configmaps + agent daemonset), but the manifest itself is broken as written. Severity critical is correct: pod is unschedulable and has no TLS material.

confirmed.title: deploy_wazuh() never applies the Manager StatefulSet or Dashboard Deployment
confirmed.evidence: deploy_wazuh() applies only configmap-ossec/rules/decoders and agents/daemonset.yaml, then prints 'NOTE: Wazuh Helm chart may need manual setup.' It never runs `kubectl apply -f k8s/wazuh/manager/deployment.yaml` or `.../dashboard/deployment.yaml`. teardown.sh:75 does delete `wazuh/manager/`, confirming the manifests exist and are intended to be applied. Result: agents are deployed but there is no manager to receive them and no dashboard — the core SIEM is never created by the documented deploy path.
confirmed.recommendation: In deploy_wazuh() (scripts/deploy-stack.sh), after applying the ConfigMaps and before/with the agent DaemonSet, add: kubectl apply -f "$K8S_DIR/wazuh/manager/deployment.yaml" and kubectl apply -f "$K8S_DIR/wazuh/dashboard/deployment.yaml" (and likely also the dashboard configmap-dashboard.yaml at k8s/wazuh/dashboard/configmap-dashboard.yaml, plus k8s/wazuh/indexer/ if it contains manifests, to keep parity). Also consider a 'kubectl wait' on the wazuh-manager pod since agents depend on it. Remove or correct the misleading Helm note at line 130. Note: teardown_wazuh deletes k8s/wazuh/manager/ but not k8s/wazuh/dashboard/ — fix that asymmetry too for clean teardown.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading the file. deploy_wazuh() (lines 117-134) applies ONLY configmap-ossec.yaml, configmap-rules.yaml, configmap-decoders.yaml (lines 121-123) and agents/daemonset.yaml (line 126), then prints the misleading 'NOTE: Wazuh Helm chart may need manual setup.' (line 130). It never runs kubectl apply for k8s/wazuh/manager/deployment.yaml or k8s/wazuh/dashboard/deployment.yaml. Both manifests exist and are real, applyable workloads: k8s/wazuh/manager/deployment.yaml defines a StatefulSet named wazuh-manager (kind: StatefulSet, apps/v1) plus its headless Service exposing 1514/1515/55000 — the SIEM/XDR core; k8s/wazuh/dashboard/deployment.yaml defines a Deployment named wazuh-dashboard plus its Service on 5601. teardown.sh:75 deletes the whole k8s/wazuh/manager/ directory (which includes deployment.yaml), confirming the manifests are intended to be applied as part of the lifecycle. Net effect: the documented deploy path ('deploy-stack.sh all' -> deploy_wazuh at line 227, or 'deploy-stack.sh wazuh') deploys agent DaemonSets that point at wazuh-manager.wazuh.svc but the manager and dashboard are never created, leaving the core SIEM (described as 'always on' in CLAUDE.md) non-functional. Title wording is accurate: manager is a StatefulSet, dashboard is a Deployment. Severity critical is appropriate — the deploy script is the documented entry point and silently produces a broken core SIEM.

confirmed.title: Agent→Manager traffic blocked: NetworkPolicy allows only pod sources but agents use hostNetwork
confirmed.evidence: allow-wazuh-manager ingress is `from: [podSelector: {}]` on ports 1514/1515/55000. The agent DaemonSet runs with `hostNetwork: true` (k8s/wazuh/agents/daemonset.yaml:23), so agent connections originate from the node host IP, not a pod IP in the wazuh namespace. CNIs evaluate hostNetwork pod traffic as node traffic, which a podSelector does not match. With default-deny-ingress also present (lines 166-174), agent enrollment/log shipping is dropped.
confirmed.recommendation: Add an ingress rule on allow-wazuh-manager admitting the node source for 1514/1515 — e.g. an additional `from: [- ipBlock: {cidr: <node CIDR>}]` covering the node IPs (and/or pod CIDR). Alternatively drop hostNetwork on the agents if host visibility can be met via the existing hostPath mounts of / and /var/log alone. Validate the fix on the actual k3s cluster (kubectl exec test from an agent pod to the manager on 1514/1515) since enforcement depends on the embedded NetworkPolicy controller.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading both files. network-policies.yaml lines 191-199: allow-wazuh-manager ingress is `from: [- podSelector: {}]` on ports 1514/1515/55000, with comment 'Agent log ingestion and registration from same namespace'. daemonset.yaml line 23: `hostNetwork: true` (and hostPID line 24). default-deny-ingress for wazuh ns confirmed at lines 166-174. hostNetwork pods share the node's network namespace, so their source IP is the node IP, not a Pod-network IP; a podSelector matches by Pod IP and does NOT match hostNetwork (node) source traffic. k3s here uses default flannel (no --flannel-backend=none in lima/k3s-soc.yaml) but k3s ships an embedded kube-router-based NetworkPolicy controller that DOES enforce policies, so the default-deny + podSelector-only allow will drop agent enrollment (1515) and log shipping (1514). Note: exact behavior is CNI-dependent, but under k3s's enforced policy this is a real functional break. File:line accurate. Severity high justified — agents cannot enroll or ship logs.

confirmed.title: Agent registration keys (client.keys) stored on emptyDir — wiped on every manager restart
confirmed.evidence: Wazuh persists agent registration in /var/ossec/etc/client.keys. The StatefulSet mounts /var/ossec/etc from the `etc-writable` volume which is `emptyDir: {}` (line 128). The 5Gi PVC only backs /var/ossec/data (line 99-100). On any pod restart the emptyDir is recreated from the image defaults via the initContainer, so all client.keys are lost and every agent must re-enroll; with <purge>yes</purge> in ossec.conf this also drops historical agent identities.
confirmed.recommendation: Persist /var/ossec/etc (or at least client.keys) on a PVC instead of emptyDir, while still overlaying custom ossec.conf/rules. Pattern: PVC-backed etc with an initContainer that seeds image defaults only when the volume is empty, then applies the ConfigMap ossec.conf overlay.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. /var/ossec/etc is mounted from volume `etc-writable` (deployment.yaml:101-102) which is `emptyDir: {}` (line 127-128). The only PVC (volumeClaimTemplates `data`, lines 138-146) backs /var/ossec/data (line 99-100), NOT /var/ossec/etc. Wazuh stores agent registration in /var/ossec/etc/client.keys, so on pod restart the init-config initContainer (lines 49-63) re-seeds etc-writable from the image defaults (`cp -a /var/ossec/etc/. /etc-init/`) and overlays ossec.conf, losing client.keys. configmap-ossec.yaml:27 sets <purge>yes</purge>, so historical agent identities are also dropped. Note: agents (daemonset.yaml) use auto-enrollment with no persisted keys themselves, so they would re-enroll, but the manager-side identity churn and event-history disruption is real. Severity high is appropriate (data/operational loss, not arbitrary code exec).

confirmed.title: Open agent enrollment with no password and use_source_ip disabled
confirmed.evidence: <auth><disabled>no</disabled><use_source_ip>no</use_source_ip><purge>yes</purge></auth> on port 1515 with no enrollment password configured and no WAZUH_REGISTRATION_PASSWORD passed to agents (daemonset.yaml sets only WAZUH_MANAGER/WAZUH_AGENT_GROUP). Any host that can reach 1515 can self-register an agent and inject events into the SIEM. The weak NetworkPolicy (and hostNetwork) widen the exposure surface.
confirmed.recommendation: Set an enrollment password (authd <use_password>yes</use_password> + authd.pass on the manager, WAZUH_REGISTRATION_PASSWORD from a Secret on agents) or move to cert-based enrollment. Keep the 1515 NetworkPolicy tight and verify it actually constrains the hostNetwork agents.
confirmed.verifyNote: Partially confirmed; severity over-stated. configmap-ossec.yaml:22-28 does set <auth><disabled>no</disabled><port>1515</port><use_source_ip>no</use_source_ip><purge>yes</purge></auth> with no <use_password> / authd.pass, and daemonset.yaml:36-39 sets only WAZUH_MANAGER and WAZUH_AGENT_GROUP (no WAZUH_REGISTRATION_PASSWORD) — so enrollment is open and unauthenticated. That part is real. HOWEVER the evidence overstates exposure: the wazuh namespace has a default-deny ingress (network-policies.yaml:165+) plus allow-wazuh-manager (lines 177-199) that permits 1514/1515/55000 ONLY from `podSelector: {}` within the wazuh namespace — not cluster-wide and not external. So it is NOT true that 'any host that can reach 1515' can register from outside the namespace under normal CNI enforcement. The agent DaemonSet's hostNetwork:true (daemonset.yaml:23) does muddy NetworkPolicy matching (node-IP-sourced traffic), but that is a caveat, not 'any host.' Given the in-cluster-only reachability, downgrading to medium. The underlying weakness (no enrollment auth, purge=yes) is still worth fixing.

confirmed.title: Agent DaemonSet runs privileged + hostNetwork + hostPID with full host root mount
confirmed.evidence: securityContext: privileged: true, plus hostNetwork: true, hostPID: true, and hostPath mount of `/` at /host (read-only) and `/etc` at /host/etc. A privileged container with the host root filesystem and host PID namespace is effectively root on the node; privileged also overrides the readOnly hostPath protections for many operations. This is the maximum-privilege pod posture.
confirmed.recommendation: Drop `privileged: true` and instead set `capabilities: {drop: [ALL], add: [...]}` with only the capabilities FIM/rootcheck need (likely CAP_DAC_READ_SEARCH for FIM reads; CAP_SYS_PTRACE only if rootcheck process inspection is required). Re-evaluate whether hostPID is actually needed by the Wazuh agent's checks. Keep the readOnly hostPath mounts and document why each (/ , /var/log, /etc) is required. Test FIM/rootcheck still function after dropping privileged before rolling out.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading daemonset.yaml. securityContext.privileged: true (lines 64-65), hostNetwork: true (line 23), hostPID: true (line 24), host-root hostPath `/` mounted at /host readOnly (volumeMount lines 42-44; volume lines 67-69), and host-etc hostPath `/etc` mounted at /host/etc readOnly (volumeMount lines 48-50; volume lines 73-75). All cited line numbers accurate. A privileged container with hostPID and a hostPath mount of `/` is effectively root on the node — full host compromise surface. (Minor imprecision in the finding's wording: a readOnly hostPath mount is enforced as a kernel mount flag and is not directly 'overridden' by privileged; but a privileged container can access /dev, remount, and use the host PID namespace, so the overall maximum-privilege conclusion is correct.) Adjusted correctedFile to 64-75 to include the volume definitions referenced by the evidence. Severity high justified.

confirmed.title: INDEXER_PASSWORD/API_PASSWORD secretKeyRefs marked optional:true — silent empty-credential startup
confirmed.evidence: INDEXER_PASSWORD uses secretKeyRef with `optional: true`; dashboard/deployment.yaml:78-101 does the same for INDEXER_PASSWORD, DASHBOARD_PASSWORD, and API_PASSWORD. If the secret/key is absent the container starts with the variable unset/empty rather than failing, producing confusing auth errors against OpenSearch and a security risk of running with blank credentials. deploy-stack.sh validates only k8s/shared/secrets.yaml placeholders, not presence of soc-wazuh-secrets.
confirmed.recommendation: Remove `optional: true` from all four secretKeyRefs (manager INDEXER_PASSWORD; dashboard INDEXER_PASSWORD/DASHBOARD_PASSWORD/API_PASSWORD) so pods hit CreateContainerConfigError when credentials are absent. Add a deploy-stack.sh check that soc-shared-secrets and soc-wazuh-secrets exist before deploying wazuh workloads.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. Manager INDEXER_PASSWORD uses secretKeyRef name=soc-shared-secrets key=opensearch-password with `optional: true` (deployment.yaml:88-93). dashboard/deployment.yaml mirrors this: INDEXER_PASSWORD (78-83, soc-shared-secrets), DASHBOARD_PASSWORD (88-93, soc-shared-secrets), and API_PASSWORD (96-101, soc-wazuh-secrets/wazuh-api-password) all carry `optional: true`. With optional:true a missing secret/key leaves the env var unset rather than failing the pod, yielding blank-credential auth attempts and confusing failures. deploy-stack.sh validate_secrets() (lines 50-65) only greps secrets.yaml for the base64 CHANGE_ME marker 'Q0hBTkdFX01F'; it never verifies that soc-shared-secrets / soc-wazuh-secrets exist or are applied (these manifests live in secrets.yaml and are not separately applied by the script's wazuh path). Note one evidence inaccuracy: the finding implies a `soc-wazuh-secrets` ref on the manager, but the manager only references soc-shared-secrets; soc-wazuh-secrets is used by the dashboard. Severity high retained for the fail-open credential behavior.

confirmed.title: Filebeat CA path env points to ca.crt while certs are named root-ca.pem/kirk.pem
confirmed.evidence: FILEBEAT_SSL_CERTIFICATE_AUTHORITIES="/etc/ssl/opensearch/ca.crt" but ossec.conf (configmap-ossec.yaml:44-46) references /etc/ssl/opensearch/root-ca.pem, kirk.pem, kirk-key.pem, and opensearch.yaml mounts esnode/root-ca PEMs. The opensearch-certs secret (whichever way it's eventually created) is unlikely to contain a `ca.crt` key, so Filebeat→OpenSearch TLS verification will fail with a missing-CA error.
confirmed.recommendation: Set FILEBEAT_SSL_CERTIFICATE_AUTHORITIES to the actual CA filename in the opensearch-certs secret (root-ca.pem) so it aligns with ossec.conf, the dashboard config, and the secret keys.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. deployment.yaml:96-97 sets FILEBEAT_SSL_CERTIFICATE_AUTHORITIES=/etc/ssl/opensearch/ca.crt, while configmap-ossec.yaml:44-46 references /etc/ssl/opensearch/root-ca.pem (+ kirk.pem/kirk-key.pem) and opensearch.yaml:71-78 uses root-ca.pem/esnode.pem. The dashboard config (configmap-dashboard.yaml:18) also points at root-ca.pem. Since the opensearch-certs secret (per ossec.conf and opensearch.yaml usage) is expected to hold root-ca.pem, not ca.crt, the Filebeat->OpenSearch TLS verification env points at a filename that the secret almost certainly will not contain, causing a missing-CA failure. This is a genuine naming inconsistency. Severity medium is fine; it is partly subsumed by the critical missing-secret finding (the cert filenames only matter once the secret exists).

confirmed.title: opensearch-ca-cert ConfigMap (wazuh ns) is unused and ships placeholder cert
confirmed.evidence: Defines `opensearch-ca-cert` ConfigMap in the wazuh namespace with body `REPLACE_WITH_YOUR_OPENSEARCH_CA_CERTIFICATE`. The wazuh manager mounts the `opensearch-certs` Secret (not this ConfigMap) for CA material, so this ConfigMap is never consumed in the wazuh namespace. CHANGELOG claims it 'Distributes CA cert to opencti and wazuh namespaces for TLS verification' but the manager wiring doesn't reference it. Applying the file as-is installs an invalid placeholder cert.
confirmed.recommendation: Remove the wazuh-namespace block (lines 28-40) from k8s/shared/opensearch-ca-cert.yaml since the wazuh manager gets its CA from the opensearch-certs Secret, OR wire the manager to mount opensearch-ca-cert and drop the CA from opensearch-certs -- pick one source of truth. Either way, correct CHANGELOG.md:16 to stop claiming distribution to the wazuh namespace.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading the files. opensearch-ca-cert.yaml lines 29-40 define the ConfigMap in the wazuh namespace with body 'REPLACE_WITH_YOUR_OPENSEARCH_CA_CERTIFICATE' (lines 38-40). The wazuh manager (k8s/wazuh/manager/deployment.yaml) mounts the opensearch-certs Secret (volume lines 135-137) at /etc/ssl/opensearch and points FILEBEAT_SSL_CERTIFICATE_AUTHORITIES at /etc/ssl/opensearch/ca.crt (lines 96-97) plus indexer SSL CA at /etc/ssl/opensearch/root-ca.pem (configmap-ossec.yaml:44) -- all sourced from the opensearch-certs Secret, never the opensearch-ca-cert ConfigMap. grep confirms the ConfigMap is referenced only in k8s/opencti/deployment.yaml (configMap name opensearch-ca-cert at lines 160-161, consumed via /etc/ssl/opensearch/ca.crt at lines 96/98); nothing in the wazuh manifests or deploy-stack.sh mounts it. CHANGELOG.md:16 does claim it 'Distributes CA cert to opencti and wazuh namespaces for TLS verification', which is false for wazuh. Note: opensearch-ca-cert.yaml is not applied by deploy-stack.sh at all (no kubectl apply of it anywhere), so it is only installed if someone manually applies the file -- the 'applying installs an invalid placeholder cert' caveat holds only on manual apply. Severity medium retained: an unused, placeholder-bearing manifest in the wazuh ns plus a false CHANGELOG claim is a real correctness/consistency defect, though impact is contained since the ConfigMap is never mounted in wazuh.

confirmed.title: No runAsNonRoot / readOnlyRootFilesystem / capability drop / seccomp on manager or dashboard
confirmed.evidence: Manager securityContext sets only `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false`; dashboard the same (dashboard/deployment.yaml:61-62) and its initContainer runs as runAsUser:0 (line 50). No runAsNonRoot, runAsUser, fsGroup, readOnlyRootFilesystem, seccompProfile, or capabilities.drop on any wazuh workload (grep across wazuh manifests returns nothing except the dashboard root init). Pods run as root by default with a writable root filesystem.
confirmed.recommendation: Add container/pod securityContext to manager and dashboard: seccompProfile RuntimeDefault and capabilities.drop:[ALL]; add runAsNonRoot/runAsUser and readOnlyRootFilesystem where the Wazuh 4.14.3 images tolerate it (use emptyDir/PVC for writable paths). Scope the dashboard init-container's runAsUser:0 to only the chown it needs.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. grep of securityContext across k8s/wazuh/ returns exactly: manager container allowPrivilegeEscalation:false only (deployment.yaml:67-68); dashboard container allowPrivilegeEscalation:false only (dashboard/deployment.yaml:61-62); dashboard init-container runAsUser:0 (dashboard/deployment.yaml:49-50); and the agent DaemonSet privileged:true (daemonset.yaml:64-65). No runAsNonRoot, runAsUser(!=0), fsGroup, readOnlyRootFilesystem, seccompProfile, or capabilities.drop on the manager or dashboard. So pods run as root with a writable root filesystem. The finding is accurate. Severity: the cited file is the manager/dashboard, both of which run privileged-by-default-root but without privileged:true; this is hardening debt rather than an exploitable hole on its own, so medium (as labeled) is reasonable.

confirmed.title: NFS-backed PV declares ReadWriteMany but PVC requests it from local-path (RWO) NFS server backing
confirmed.file: k8s/storage/nfs-server.yaml:11-21 (PVC nfs-data, local-path/RWO) + nfs-server.yaml:23-65 (Deployment, no node affinity); k8s/storage/pv-claims.yaml:14-19 (cortex-shared-nfs PV, RWM/nfs)
confirmed.evidence: cortex-shared-nfs PV is `nfs:` with ReadWriteMany (correct for NFS). However the NFS server itself is backed by PVC `nfs-data` using storageClassName local-path with accessModes ReadWriteOnce (nfs-server.yaml:16-21). local-path provisions node-local hostPath, which is fine for a single replica NFS server, but the design relies on the NFS-server pod never rescheduling to another node (no node affinity / it's a Deployment, not a StatefulSet) — on reschedule the local-path volume (and exported data) is lost/orphaned.
confirmed.recommendation: Low priority given the documented single-node design. If/when moving to multi-node, convert nfs-server to a StatefulSet with a stable PVC, or pin the Deployment pod via nodeSelector/nodeName/affinity to the node holding its local-path volume, or use a hostPath PV with a fixed node. The existing comment in pv-claims.yaml:1-4 already documents the single-node assumption; optionally add a matching note in nfs-server.yaml near the nfs-data PVC to make the constraint explicit there too.
confirmed.verifyNote: Facts confirmed by reading both files. cortex-shared-nfs PV is nfs: with ReadWriteMany (pv-claims.yaml:14-18) — correct for NFS. The NFS server's backing PVC nfs-data uses storageClassName: local-path with accessModes ReadWriteOnce (nfs-server.yaml:16-18). The NFS server is a kind: Deployment (line 24) with replicas: 1, and its pod spec (lines 39-65) has NO nodeName/nodeSelector/affinity. So the cited risk — a local-path (node-local hostPath) volume not following the pod on reschedule to another node — is technically accurate. However severity is overstated: the cluster is explicitly single-node (CLAUDE.md: k3s in one Lima VM on a Mac Mini; pv-claims.yaml:1-4 documents 'single-node cluster' and advises replacing with distributed storage for multi-node). On a single-node cluster there is no other node to reschedule onto, so the failure mode is unreachable in the actual topology. Also, 'data lost' overstates impact: local-path data persists on the node's disk (it is not deleted on pod restart), and a reschedule onto the same node re-binds. This is a forward-looking hardening/documentation nit, and the manifest comments already flag the single-node assumption. Note the file:line in the finding was slightly off — the Deployment-vs-StatefulSet and no-affinity facts live at nfs-server.yaml:23-65, not 16-21.

confirmed.title: Service selectors use app.kubernetes.io/name while ConfigMaps use legacy app: label inconsistently
confirmed.evidence: Workloads/Services/NetworkPolicies use `app.kubernetes.io/name: wazuh-manager`, but the ConfigMaps use the legacy `app: wazuh-manager` label (configmap-ossec.yaml:9, configmap-rules.yaml:10, configmap-decoders.yaml:10, dashboard/configmap-dashboard.yaml:10). Mixed label conventions hamper label-selector ops (kubectl get -l) and consistency.
confirmed.recommendation: Add app.kubernetes.io/name (and optionally part-of/component) labels to the four wazuh ConfigMaps (and the wazuh secret) to match the workloads/Services, completing the standardization the CHANGELOG already claims was done.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. configmap-ossec.yaml:9 = 'app: wazuh-manager', configmap-rules.yaml:10 = 'app: wazuh-manager', configmap-decoders.yaml:10 = 'app: wazuh-manager', dashboard/configmap-dashboard.yaml:10 = 'app: wazuh-dashboard'. Meanwhile workloads/Services use app.kubernetes.io/name (manager deployment.yaml:9,12,31,37,41; dashboard deployment.yaml:9,12,24,29,33; agents daemonset.yaml:9,13,17). CHANGELOG.md:22 explicitly states the app:->app.kubernetes.io/name conversion was applied to 'Manager, Dashboard, and Agent manifests' -- the ConfigMaps were missed, so the inconsistency is corroborated and was an oversight. Downgraded medium->low: this is purely cosmetic/cleanliness. ConfigMaps are mounted by name (configMap.name references in the deployments), not by label selector, so there is zero functional impact; only ad-hoc 'kubectl get -l' ops are mildly affected.

confirmed.title: Container images not digest-pinned (tag-only)
confirmed.evidence: All images use mutable tags: wazuh/wazuh-manager:4.14.3 (lines 51,66), wazuh/wazuh-dashboard:4.14.3 (dashboard 42,60), wazuh/wazuh-agent:4.14.3 (agents:27), erichough/nfs-server:2.2.1 (nfs-server:42), opensearch image tag 2.19.1 (opensearch.yaml:15). No imagePullPolicy is set and no @sha256 digest. CLAUDE.md claims 'All container images pinned (no :latest tags)', but version tags are still mutable in the registry.
confirmed.recommendation: Pin by digest (image@sha256:...) for supply-chain integrity, prioritizing the third-party erichough/nfs-server:2.2.1. At minimum add imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent and record digests in a lockfile, and soften the CLAUDE.md wording from 'pinned' to 'version-tagged'.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed on every cited line. wazuh/wazuh-manager:4.14.3 at deployment.yaml:51 (initContainer) and :66 (main container); wazuh/wazuh-dashboard:4.14.3 at dashboard/deployment.yaml:42 (init) and :60 (main); wazuh/wazuh-agent:4.14.3 at agents/daemonset.yaml:27; erichough/nfs-server:2.2.1 at storage/nfs-server.yaml:42; opensearch image tag '2.19.1' at shared/opensearch.yaml:15. grep across k8s/ found zero @sha256 digests, zero imagePullPolicy entries on these resources, and zero :latest tags. CLAUDE.md does claim 'All container images pinned (no :latest tags)' -- literally true (no :latest) but the finding's nuance that version tags remain mutable is accurate. Severity low is appropriate for a home-lab supply-chain hardening gap.

confirmed.title: Agent DaemonSet has no resource-conflicting hostPath overlap clarity and lacks updateStrategy/priorityClass
confirmed.evidence: host-root mounts `/` at /host (readOnly) and host-etc separately mounts `/etc` at /host/etc — the latter is redundant since /etc is already visible under /host/etc via the root mount. The DaemonSet also sets no updateStrategy (defaults to RollingUpdate, acceptable) and no priorityClassName for a security-critical agent that should survive eviction. WAZUH_AGENT_GROUP=k8s-nodes references a group that must be pre-created on the manager.
confirmed.recommendation: Remove the redundant host-etc volume + volumeMount (it duplicates /host/etc already exposed via host-root). Add `priorityClassName: system-node-critical` (or a custom high-priority class) so the security agent survives node-pressure eviction. Ensure the `k8s-nodes` agent group exists on the manager (create it via an init step or document the prerequisite).
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. host-root mounts `/` at /host readOnly (lines 42-44, volume 67-69) and host-etc separately mounts `/etc` at /host/etc readOnly (lines 48-50, volume 73-75) — the host's /etc is already reachable at /host/etc through the root mount, so the host-etc mount is redundant (both are readOnly, so no protection is added). No `updateStrategy` set (defaults to RollingUpdate) and no `priorityClassName` anywhere in the spec — both confirmed absent. WAZUH_AGENT_GROUP=k8s-nodes (env lines 38-39) references a manager-side agent group that must be pre-created. All sub-claims accurate. Severity low appropriate — cleanliness/robustness, not a functional or security defect.

confirmed.title: indexer-password key in soc-wazuh-secrets is dead (never referenced)
confirmed.evidence: soc-wazuh-secrets defines `indexer-password`, but grep across k8s/ shows the manager and dashboard reference `opensearch-password` from soc-shared-secrets for the indexer, and only `wazuh-api-password` from soc-wazuh-secrets is consumed (dashboard/deployment.yaml:100). The indexer-password key is unused dead config that can drift from the real OpenSearch password.
confirmed.recommendation: Remove the unused indexer-password key from soc-wazuh-secrets in k8s/shared/secrets.yaml (line 100) and from secrets.yaml.example (line 100) to eliminate the dead/drift-prone duplicate. If a separate indexer credential is ever genuinely needed, wire the wazuh manager and dashboard INDEXER_PASSWORD to it explicitly; until then keep soc-shared-secrets/opensearch-password as the single source of truth for the OpenSearch connection.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading the file and grepping the whole repo. k8s/shared/secrets.yaml:100 defines `indexer-password` under the soc-wazuh-secrets Secret (namespace wazuh). A grep for `indexer-password` across the repo returns only its two definition lines (secrets.yaml:100 and secrets.yaml.example:100) -- zero consuming workloads. The actual indexer/OpenSearch credential is wired via soc-shared-secrets/opensearch-password: wazuh dashboard INDEXER_PASSWORD (deployment.yaml:78-83) and wazuh manager INDEXER_PASSWORD (deployment.yaml:88-93) both reference name: soc-shared-secrets, key: opensearch-password. The only key consumed from soc-wazuh-secrets is wazuh-api-password (dashboard deployment.yaml: name at line 99, key at line 100). Drift risk is concrete: indexer-password (secrets.yaml:100) holds a different base64 value than opensearch-password (secrets.yaml:20), so the dead key can silently diverge from the real OpenSearch password it appears to represent. Severity low is correct: dead/misleading config, no runtime impact since workloads correctly use opensearch-password. Minor citation note: evidence attributes wazuh-api-password consumption to 'dashboard/deployment.yaml:100' which is the key line; the soc-wazuh-secrets name is on line 99 -- does not affect the finding.
