confirmed.title: TCP-socket-only probes can't detect manager process health, only port listen
confirmed.evidence: readiness/liveness probe both `tcpSocket: 1514`. The remote daemon binding 1514 does not guarantee analysisd/wazuh-db/indexer connector are healthy; the manager can be partially degraded (e.g. indexer integration failing) while 1514 still listens, so alerts silently stop flowing without the probe firing. No probe on 55000 API either.
confirmed.recommendation: Add an exec liveness/readiness probe on /var/ossec/bin/wazuh-control status (and/or an authenticated check of the 55000 API), and consider a startupProbe so slow first boot doesn't trip liveness.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. readinessProbe (deployment.yaml:110-115) and livenessProbe (116-122) both use tcpSocket port 1514 only; there is no probe on 55000 (API) and no exec probe of wazuh-control/analysisd. A bound 1514 socket does not prove analysisd/wazuh-db/the indexer connector are healthy, so a partially degraded manager (e.g. indexer integration failing) can keep passing probes while alerts stop flowing to OpenSearch. Design observation, low severity, correct as labeled.

confirmed.title: Dashboard→OpenSearch TLS verification disabled (verificationMode: none)
confirmed.evidence: opensearch.ssl.verificationMode: none disables TLS cert verification on the dashboard→OpenSearch connection, despite root-ca.pem being mounted and available. This permits MITM within the cluster path and contradicts the project's stated 'TLS verification enabled on all OpenSearch connections' (CHANGELOG:28).
confirmed.recommendation: Set opensearch.ssl.verificationMode: full and add opensearch.ssl.certificateAuthorities pointing at the mounted /usr/share/wazuh-dashboard/config/certs/root-ca.pem. (Duplicate of the next finding — fix once.)
confirmed.verifyNote: CONFIRMED at configmap-dashboard.yaml:24 (`opensearch.ssl.verificationMode: none`). root-ca.pem is mounted/available (line 18), so the fix is trivial. The connection target is https://opensearch-cluster-master.shared.svc.cluster.local:9200 (line 23 / deployment.yaml:74) using INDEXER_USERNAME=admin with the admin password — verification being off does permit in-cluster MITM of an admin-credentialed session. It also contradicts CHANGELOG.md:28 ('TLS certificate verification enabled on all OpenSearch connections'), which I confirmed. NOTE: this and the next finding are the SAME line/issue filed twice at conflicting severities (low vs high); I am normalizing both to medium. Low understates it (admin creds + contradicts documented hardening + trivial fix); high overstates it (cluster-internal path, self-signed homelab, attacker needs in-cluster position).

confirmed.title: Headless Service (clusterIP: None) used for dashboard/API access path
confirmed.evidence: wazuh-manager Service is headless (clusterIP: None), appropriate for the StatefulSet's stable network identity. However the dashboard connects to https://wazuh-manager.wazuh.svc.cluster.local (dashboard/deployment.yaml:85) for the API on 55000; a headless service returns the pod IP directly which works for a single replica but provides no load-balanced VIP and will not gracefully handle pod IP churn during rollouts.
confirmed.recommendation: Acceptable for single replica. If 55000 API stability during rollouts matters, add a separate ClusterIP Service for the API and keep the headless Service for StatefulSet identity.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed factually. The wazuh-manager Service is headless (deployment.yaml:23, clusterIP: None) and the dashboard targets https://wazuh-manager.wazuh.svc.cluster.local for the 55000 API (dashboard/deployment.yaml:84-85). A headless service resolves to pod IP(s) with no VIP/load-balancing; for the single StatefulSet replica it works but won't gracefully absorb pod-IP churn during rollouts. The finding itself notes this is acceptable for single-replica, so it is really a design nit rather than a low-severity issue; downgrading low->nit to match its own conclusion.

confirmed.title: helm-values.yaml is aspirational and diverges from the actually-applied raw manifests
confirmed.evidence: helm-values.yaml documents a `helm install wazuh ./k8s/wazuh/` flow and a TheHive integration (enabled: true, alertLevel 7) that the raw manifests don't implement (ossec.conf has the integration commented out). deploy-stack.sh never uses this values file. Two divergent sources of truth for the same component invite drift.
confirmed.recommendation: Delete k8s/wazuh/helm-values.yaml (deployment is via raw manifests) or commit to a real chart; and reconcile the TheHive integration claim so the values file and configmap-ossec.yaml agree on whether the integration is enabled.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. helm-values.yaml:7-9 documents 'helm install wazuh ./k8s/wazuh/ -n wazuh -f helm-values.yaml'; lines 25-30 declare integration.thehive.enabled: true with alertLevel: 7. The raw manifest configmap-ossec.yaml has the TheHive <integration> block commented out (lines 50-59, comment 'Disabled: integration script not yet deployed', level 7). deploy-stack.sh deploy_wazuh() (lines 117-134) only kubectl-applies the raw ConfigMaps and agent DaemonSet and never references helm-values.yaml (the file appears nowhere in deploy-stack.sh; only thehive/cortex/opencti helm-values.yaml are used). So two divergent sources of truth exist exactly as described. Severity nit is appropriate -- documentation/design drift with no runtime effect since the helm path is never executed.

confirmed.title: Empty k8s/wazuh/indexer/ directory implies a missing/abandoned component
confirmed.evidence: The directory k8s/wazuh/indexer/ exists but is empty. CLAUDE.md lists 'Indexer/OpenSearch' as a Wazuh sub-component and helm-values comments reference an indexer/ directory of raw manifests. The indexer is actually the shared OpenSearch, so this empty dir is misleading scaffolding.
confirmed.recommendation: Remove the empty k8s/wazuh/indexer/ directory, or add a README.md inside it (and update helm-values.yaml lines 4-5) clarifying that the Wazuh indexer is the shared OpenSearch cluster in the `shared` namespace and has no dedicated manifests in this directory.
confirmed.verifyNote: Independently confirmed. `ls -la k8s/wazuh/indexer/` shows the directory exists but contains only `.` and `..` (empty), while sibling dirs agents/ (daemonset.yaml), dashboard/ (configmap-dashboard.yaml, deployment.yaml), and manager/ (4 manifests) are all populated — indexer/ is the lone empty dir. CLAUDE.md line 8 lists 'Indexer/OpenSearch' as a Wazuh sub-component and line 48 lists 'Indexer' under wazuh/. helm-values.yaml lines 4-5 explicitly say 'deploy using the raw manifests in manager/, indexer/, dashboard/ directories', yet indexer/ has no manifests. The manager config (helm-values.yaml line 22-23) and dashboard (line 48-49) both point to the shared external OpenSearch at opensearch-cluster-master.shared.svc.cluster.local:9200, confirming the indexer is the shared OpenSearch cluster and the empty dir is misleading scaffolding. All evidence claims verified.

confirmed.title: No PodDisruptionBudget or topology constraints; single replica with no anti-restart safeguards
confirmed.evidence: replicas: 1 with no PodDisruptionBudget. Acceptable for a home lab single node, but combined with the emptyDir client.keys issue, any voluntary disruption (node drain, upgrade) both loses agent keys and drops the SIEM with no minAvailable guard.
confirmed.recommendation: Low priority on single-node. If uptime matters, add a PDB (minAvailable:1) and, more importantly, fix the /var/ossec/etc persistence so drains/upgrades are non-destructive.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. StatefulSet sets replicas:1 (deployment.yaml:34) and there is no PodDisruptionBudget anywhere in k8s/wazuh/ (no PDB kind present). For a single-node home lab this is expected; the only real bite is its interaction with the emptyDir client.keys issue (a voluntary disruption both loses keys and drops the SIEM). Already labeled nit and that is correct.

confirmed.title: rabbitmq-deployment.yaml references a ConfigMap (rabbitmq-config) that is never defined anywhere
confirmed.evidence: The StatefulSet mounts a volume `config` from `configMap: name: rabbitmq-config` (lines 101-103) at /etc/rabbitmq/conf.d/20-matchbox.conf via subPath rabbitmq.conf. `grep -rn rabbitmq-config k8s/ scripts/` returns only this one reference — the ConfigMap is never created by any manifest or deploy script. If this StatefulSet were applied, the pod would be stuck in ContainerCreating (volume not found).
confirmed.recommendation: Resolve jointly with finding 2. If keeping the official-image path, add a `rabbitmq-config` ConfigMap (namespace shared) with key rabbitmq.conf defining e.g. vm_memory_high_watermark.absolute=200MB and disk_free_limit.absolute=500MB, and wire it into deploy-stack.sh BEFORE the StatefulSet apply. If deleting rabbitmq-deployment.yaml (the simpler fix, since the Bitnami chart is the active path), this finding disappears with it.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading the file. The StatefulSet volume 'config' (lines 100-103) sources `configMap: name: rabbitmq-config`, mounted at lines 97-99 (/etc/rabbitmq/conf.d/20-matchbox.conf, subPath rabbitmq.conf). Repo-wide `grep -rn rabbitmq-config` returns ONLY line 103 — no ConfigMap manifest defines it and deploy-stack.sh never creates it (its kubectl apply / configmap calls are for wazuh, namespaces, storage, opencti — never rabbitmq-config). So as written the StatefulSet would stick in ContainerCreating. Caveat that lowers real-world blast radius: this StatefulSet is never applied by any script (see finding 2), so the break only manifests if someone runs `kubectl apply -f rabbitmq-deployment.yaml` manually. Cited range said 101-103; the volume block actually spans 100-103 (corrected). Severity kept high because the manifest is genuinely non-functional, though one could argue medium since it is dead/unwired.

confirmed.title: Two conflicting RabbitMQ definitions; rabbitmq-deployment.yaml is dead and contradicts the active Bitnami chart path
confirmed.evidence: rabbitmq-deployment.yaml header says 'Official image deployment (replaces Bitnami chart)' using rabbitmq:4.1-management-alpine, but scripts/deploy-stack.sh:97-99 still does `helm upgrade --install rabbitmq bitnami/rabbitmq -f shared/rabbitmq.yaml`. The official-image StatefulSet is never applied (no reference in any script). The two files also disagree: rabbitmq.yaml configures an Erlang cookie secret and rabbitmq_peer_discovery_k8s plugin; the deployment.yaml has neither. Having two sources of truth for the same service is a correctness landmine.
confirmed.recommendation: Pick one source of truth. Recommended: delete rabbitmq-deployment.yaml since the Bitnami chart is the wired, working path (this also kills finding 1's missing ConfigMap). If instead migrating to the official image, switch deploy-stack.sh:97-99 and teardown.sh:84 from helm to `kubectl apply/delete -f shared/rabbitmq-deployment.yaml`, add the missing rabbitmq-config ConfigMap and Erlang cookie handling, and delete rabbitmq.yaml. Record the decision in CHANGELOG.md.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. rabbitmq-deployment.yaml line 1 header: 'RabbitMQ — Official image deployment (replaces Bitnami chart)'; image at line 53 is rabbitmq:4.1-management-alpine. The active path is the Bitnami chart: deploy-stack.sh:97-99 runs `helm upgrade --install rabbitmq bitnami/rabbitmq -f shared/rabbitmq.yaml`, and teardown.sh:84 runs `helm uninstall rabbitmq -n shared`. Repo-wide grep shows NO script references rabbitmq-deployment.yaml — it is dead. The two files genuinely disagree: rabbitmq.yaml configures the Erlang cookie (existingErlangSecret, lines 15-16) and rabbitmq_peer_discovery_k8s plugin (line 34); rabbitmq-deployment.yaml has neither. Two sources of truth for one service is real. Minor extra inconsistency found: line 2 comment says '4-management-alpine' but the actual image tag (line 53) is '4.1-management-alpine'. Severity high is appropriate (correctness landmine / maintenance trap).

confirmed.title: Raw deployment.yaml manifests for TheHive/Cortex/OpenCTI are dead and contradict the Helm deploy path
confirmed.evidence: deploy-stack.sh deploys TheHive/Cortex/OpenCTI exclusively via Helm (`strangebee/thehive` line 143, `strangebee/cortex` line 148, `opencti/opencti` line 184) reading the *helm-values.yaml files. The raw k8s/thehive/deployment.yaml, k8s/thehive/cortex/deployment.yaml and k8s/opencti/deployment.yaml are referenced nowhere (`grep -rn '<name>/deployment.yaml'` returns empty). They are dead manifests that diverge from the Helm values (e.g. raw TheHive sets TH_DB_ES_URI to https with SSL_VERIFY=false, while helm-values uses plain http; raw OpenCTI pins platform 6.6.6 while helm-values pins no image). A maintainer editing one will not affect the deployed system.
confirmed.recommendation: Pick ONE mechanism. Helm is clearly canonical (deploy-stack.sh uses it for all three components), so delete the dead raw manifests: k8s/thehive/deployment.yaml, k8s/thehive/cortex/deployment.yaml, k8s/opencti/deployment.yaml, and k8s/shared/rabbitmq-deployment.yaml. At minimum, delete the false 'StrangeBee doesn't maintain a public Helm chart' comment at deployment.yaml:2 since the script relies on exactly that chart.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading the files. grep across *.sh/*.yaml/*.md for 'thehive/deployment.yaml', 'cortex/deployment.yaml', 'opencti/deployment.yaml' returns empty and no kubectl apply targets any deployment.yaml. deploy-stack.sh deploys exclusively via Helm: strangebee/thehive (line 143), strangebee/cortex (line 148), opencti/opencti (line 184) -- all line numbers exact. Divergences verified: raw TheHive sets TH_DB_ES_URI=https with SSL_VERIFY=false (lines 67-72) vs helm-values plain http (line 23); raw OpenCTI pins opencti/platform:6.6.6 (line 45) vs helm-values no image pin. The self-contradiction is real: deployment.yaml:2 says 'StrangeBee doesn't maintain a public Helm chart' while deploy-stack.sh uses strangebee/thehive and strangebee/cortex Helm charts. rabbitmq-deployment.yaml also exists at k8s/shared/ and is unreferenced (shared deploy uses bitnami/rabbitmq Helm at line 97). High is justified: 4 dead manifests plus a misleading comment a maintainer will trust.

confirmed.title: OpenCTI connector images (6.9.5) are 3 minor versions ahead of the platform; connector registration will likely fail
confirmed.evidence: All four connectors pin 6.9.5 (abuseipdb.yaml:29, alienvault-otx.yaml:29, mitre-attack.yaml:29, virustotal.yaml:28). The only platform version present in-repo is opencti/platform:6.6.6 (deployment.yaml:45 — itself a dead file), and the active opencti/helm-values.yaml pins NO platform image at all, so the deployed version is whatever the chart default is. OpenCTI requires the connector pycti library major.minor to match the platform; a 6.9 connector against a 6.6 platform typically fails GraphQL schema validation on registration.
confirmed.recommendation: Pin the platform AND worker image.tag explicitly in helm-values.yaml and align all four connectors to the exact same major.minor as that pinned platform tag. Add a comment tying connector version to platform version so they upgrade together. Also note the chart-repo inconsistency (deploy-stack.sh:42 adds https://charts.opencti.io/ while helm-values.yaml:2,5 documents devops-ia.github.io/helm-opencti/) — resolve which chart is canonical so the appVersion is known.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed: all four connectors pin 6.9.5 (abuseipdb.yaml:29, alienvault-otx.yaml:29, mitre-attack.yaml:29, virustotal.yaml:28). The only in-repo platform/worker tag is opencti/platform:6.6.6 and opencti/worker:6.6.6 in deployment.yaml:45/189, and I confirmed deployment.yaml is dead — it is referenced by NO script or doc (grep across repo found zero `kubectl apply` of it; the real path is the Helm install at deploy-stack.sh:184-185). helm-values.yaml pins no platform/worker tag, so the actually-deployed version floats with the chart appVersion. The version-mismatch concern is therefore real and accurate as stated. However, I downgrade from high to medium: because the deployed platform version is unpinned (whatever the chart default appVersion is at install time), the claim that registration 'will likely fail' against a 6.6 platform is conditional, not certain — the chart appVersion could well be 6.x near the connectors. The pycti major.minor-match requirement is a well-known real constraint, so the risk is genuine but its certainty is overstated. Severity medium reflects a real, likely-but-not-guaranteed compatibility break.

confirmed.title: Active OpenCTI Helm values pin no image tag, so the deployed platform/worker version is unpinned
confirmed.evidence: opencti/helm-values.yaml (the file deploy-stack.sh:185 actually uses) sets replicaCount, resources, env and extraEnvs but never sets opencti.image.repository/tag or worker.image.tag. The deployed version therefore floats with the Helm chart's appVersion. CLAUDE.md claims 'All container images pinned (no :latest tags)', which is violated for the platform/worker on the real deploy path (only the dead deployment.yaml pins 6.6.6).
confirmed.recommendation: Add explicit image.tag (and repository) for both the opencti platform and the worker in helm-values.yaml, matched to the connector major.minor. This is the single fix that also resolves finding #1's mismatch.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading the whole file: helm-values.yaml sets opencti.replicaCount/resources/env/extraEnvs (lines 12-65) and worker.replicaCount/resources (70-78) but never sets opencti.image.repository/tag or worker.image.tag anywhere. deploy-stack.sh:184-185 is the real deploy path and uses this file. So the deployed platform/worker version floats with the chart appVersion. The cited line range 11-21 is where the opencti block begins and is reasonable. The CLAUDE.md:28 claim 'All container images pinned (no :latest tags)' is technically about :latest tags specifically — an unset tag is not literally ':latest', it defaults to the chart's pinned appVersion, so it is not strictly a CLAUDE.md violation in the literal sense, though it does leave the version implicit. Finding is real; severity medium is fair (slightly closer to medium than the original given real reproducibility/upgrade risk).

confirmed.title: OpenCTI deploy path never applies the opensearch-ca-cert ConfigMap that the platform needs for TLS
confirmed.evidence: The OpenCTI platform talks to OpenSearch over HTTPS with a self-signed CA (ELASTICSEARCH__URL=https://...:9200, ELASTICSEARCH__SSL__CA_CERTS=/etc/ssl/opensearch/ca.crt, NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS=...). That CA is provided by k8s/shared/opensearch-ca-cert.yaml. But deploy-stack.sh never applies opensearch-ca-cert.yaml (grep of the script shows no reference), and the active helm-values.yaml uses plain http (line 29) and mounts no CA. So neither path is internally consistent: the raw manifest needs a CA that is never deployed, and the Helm path silently downgrades OpenSearch traffic to cleartext. opensearch-ca-cert.yaml also ships a literal REPLACE_WITH_YOUR_OPENSEARCH_CA_CERTIFICATE placeholder (lines 26, 39) that would produce an invalid cert if applied as-is.
confirmed.recommendation: Standardize on HTTPS to OpenSearch on the real (Helm) path: populate opensearch-ca-cert.yaml with the real CA (or create the ConfigMap from a file in deploy_opencti, which already does kubectl create for secrets), mount it via Helm extraVolumes/extraVolumeMounts, set ELASTICSEARCH__URL to https and ELASTICSEARCH__SSL__CA_CERTS in helm-values.yaml, and remove the cleartext http URL. Delete or wire up the dead deployment.yaml so the two paths agree.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed all sub-claims. (1) deployment.yaml:86-98 sets ELASTICSEARCH__URL=https://...:9200, ELASTICSEARCH__SSL__CA_CERTS and NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS=/etc/ssl/opensearch/ca.crt, and mounts configMap opensearch-ca-cert (lines 142-161). (2) grep confirms deploy-stack.sh never applies opensearch-ca-cert.yaml — its only references repo-wide are CHANGELOG.md and the manifest's own header comments; the script's deploy_opencti (155-192) only creates the soc-opencti-secrets secret, helm-installs the platform, and applies the connectors directory. (3) helm-values.yaml:29 uses plain http to OpenSearch and mounts no CA. (4) opensearch-ca-cert.yaml:26 and :39 contain the literal REPLACE_WITH_YOUR_OPENSEARCH_CA_CERTIFICATE placeholder. So the raw manifest needs a CA that is never deployed, and the Helm path is cleartext — both internally inconsistent, exactly as claimed. I corrected the primary file to the script (the deploy path is where the omission lives) rather than deployment.yaml:95-98 (which is the dead manifest). Severity medium is fair: cleartext intra-cluster traffic to OpenSearch plus an unworkable raw manifest, but mitigated by NetworkPolicies and a single-node homelab.

confirmed.title: TheHive raw manifest disables TLS certificate verification to OpenSearch
confirmed.evidence: TH_DB_ES_SSL_ENABLED=true with TH_DB_ES_SSL_VERIFY=false (lines 69-72). This connects over TLS but accepts any certificate, defeating MITM protection on the OpenSearch link that carries admin credentials and all case/observable data. (This is in the dead manifest, but it reflects the intended config and should be fixed wherever it lands.) The OpenCTI manifest by contrast does it correctly with a mounted CA.
confirmed.recommendation: Two-part fix tied to finding 1: (1) delete the dead manifest. (2) On the canonical Helm path, OpenSearch is currently reached over plain http (helm-values.yaml line 23) -- if/when that is switched to https, mount the opensearch-ca-cert ConfigMap and set verify=true with a CA path (mirroring OpenCTI), never verify=false. Do not carry verify=false forward to whichever path becomes canonical.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed lines 69-72: TH_DB_ES_SSL_ENABLED=true and TH_DB_ES_SSL_VERIFY=false. Contrast with OpenCTI verified correct: it mounts the opensearch-ca-cert ConfigMap (deployment.yaml lines 143-145, 158-161) and sets ELASTICSEARCH__SSL__CA_CERTS + NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS (lines 95-98). However, downgrading from medium to low: this exists ONLY in the dead manifest (no deploy path applies it, per finding 1), AND the canonical Helm path uses plain http://opensearch... (helm-values.yaml line 23) with no TLS at all -- so verify=false is never actually applied to a running system. The finding's own evidence concedes 'This is in the dead manifest.' It is a real anti-pattern that encodes bad intent, but not a live MITM exposure, so medium overstates it.

confirmed.title: Grafana enables anonymous Viewer access and iframe embedding without auth gating
confirmed.evidence: grafana.ini sets security.allow_embedding: true and auth.anonymous.enabled: true with org_role: Viewer (lines 45-49). Combined, any unauthenticated party that can reach Grafana (e.g. via the Traefik IngressRoute) can view all SOC dashboards — Wazuh alerts, IOC counts, host telemetry — and embed them. For a security operations console this exposes sensitive detection data without a login.
confirmed.recommendation: Note that the IngressRoute already enforces HTTP basic auth (soc-basic-auth middleware), so this is defense-in-depth rather than an open exposure. If anonymous Viewer is only needed for iframe embedding in the SOC Command Center, scope it tightly (dedicated empty org or named anonymous_org_name) and keep allow_embedding. Otherwise disable auth.anonymous and rely on the soc-grafana-secrets admin login. Document the kiosk/embedding intent inline.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed the grafana.ini config: security.allow_embedding: true (line 47) and auth.anonymous.enabled: true with org_role: Viewer (lines 48-50). The cited range 45-49 is slightly off — the anonymous block runs 48-50 (org_role on line 50). However the core threat-model claim is overstated. The finding states 'any unauthenticated party that can reach Grafana via the Traefik IngressRoute can view all dashboards', but k8s/ingress/traefik-routes.yaml:157-174 shows the grafana IngressRoute is fronted by the soc-basic-auth Middleware (Traefik basicAuth backed by soc-auth-secret, defined at lines 53-61). So external HTTP access already requires basic auth before reaching Grafana — anonymous viewing is not exposed to arbitrary external callers. The anonymous-Viewer setting is still real and does mean anyone who passes the proxy (or has in-cluster/port-forward access) gets Viewer without a Grafana login, and allow_embedding is real, but the exposure is meaningfully mitigated. Downgrading medium -> low.

confirmed.title: Grafana OpenSearch datasource skips TLS verification (tlsSkipVerify: true)
confirmed.evidence: The OpenSearch-Wazuh datasource sets `jsonData.tlsSkipVerify: true` while connecting over https to OpenSearch with basicAuth admin creds. Cert not validated -> MITM exposure for the admin credential.
confirmed.recommendation: Set tlsSkipVerify: false and supply the OpenSearch CA via jsonData.tlsCACert (or a mounted CA bundle) so the server cert is validated before the admin credential is transmitted. If OpenSearch uses a self-signed/internal CA, mount that CA and reference it, consistent with how soc-tls-cert is handled elsewhere in the stack.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading the file. The additionalDataSources entry 'OpenSearch-Wazuh' (lines 70-83) connects to url: https://opensearch-cluster-master.shared.svc.cluster.local:9200 with basicAuth: true, basicAuthUser: admin (line 75), and secureJsonData.basicAuthPassword: "$__env{OPENSEARCH_PASSWORD}" (line 83). jsonData.tlsSkipVerify: true is set on line 81. No tlsCACert / CA cert is configured anywhere in k8s/monitoring/ (grepped tlsCACert, caCert, ca.crt, serverName -> no matches). With cert validation disabled, the OpenSearch admin basic-auth credential is sent over an unauthenticated TLS channel, enabling MITM credential capture. Medium is correct: the connection is to an in-cluster service (internal DNS, traffic stays inside the cluster) which lowers practical exploitability, but admin-credential exposure keeps it at medium rather than low.

confirmed.title: MITRE connector imports STIX from the mutable 'master' branch over an unpinned URL (supply chain)
confirmed.evidence: MITRE_ENTERPRISE_FILE_URL = https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mitre/cti/master/enterprise-attack/enterprise-attack.json (line 56). Pulling from a moving `master` ref means any upstream change (or branch compromise/rename) is ingested automatically into the threat-intel knowledge base with no version pinning or integrity check. The mitre/cti repo also publishes tagged releases (ATT&CK-vNN.N) which are stable.
confirmed.recommendation: Point at a pinned MITRE release tag (e.g. .../ATT%26CK-v15.1/enterprise-attack/enterprise-attack.json) and bump it deliberately; optionally verify a checksum. Verify the current default-branch name against the live repo before assuming the master URL still resolves.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed verbatim: mitre-attack.yaml:56 sets MITRE_ENTERPRISE_FILE_URL to https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mitre/cti/master/enterprise-attack/enterprise-attack.json — a moving branch ref with no version pin or integrity check, ingesting whatever is on master into the threat-intel KB. The supply-chain framing is valid. I downgrade security from medium to low: the source is the official MITRE first-party repo fetched over HTTPS (TLS-authenticated origin), the connector runs as a sandboxed weekly CronJob, and the realistic blast radius of an unpinned tag here is stale/altered ATT&CK reference data rather than code execution — closer to a data-integrity/freshness concern than an exploitable supply-chain hole. The recommendation's '404' aside is plausible but I did not (and the finding cannot) verify live HTTP behavior; treat it as a hypothesis, not a confirmed fact.

confirmed.title: Cortex helm-values declare an NFS shared volume and analyzers, but cortex deployment.yaml/rbac don't match — config split across irreconcilable sources
confirmed.evidence: cortex/helm-values.yaml configures job.kubernetes.sharedVolume.nfs (server nfs-server.shared..., path /exports/cortex, lines 32-36) and an analyzers list (VirusTotal_GetReport_3_1, AbuseIPDB_1_0, URLhaus_2_0, MaxMind_GeoIP_4_0, FileInfo_8_0, lines 46-71). The dead cortex/deployment.yaml configures the Kubernetes job runner via env vars (job_runner, job_kubernetes_namespace, etc.) but defines NO NFS volume and NO analyzers. rbac.yaml grants PVC verbs 'for NFS claims' (line 30) consistent with the Helm path. Whether the strangebee/cortex chart actually accepts this `cortex.job.kubernetes.sharedVolume`/`cortex.analyzers` schema is unverified, and the analyzer set here (e.g. URLhaus_2_0) doesn't match the analyzers named in the comment header of deployment.yaml (VirusTotal, AbuseIPDB, GeoIP). Analyzer enablement/config in modern Cortex is normally done at runtime via the API/UI, not Helm values, so these may be silently ignored.
confirmed.recommendation: Verify the strangebee/cortex chart's values schema actually supports cortex.job.kubernetes.sharedVolume and cortex.analyzers; if it does not, remove them from helm-values.yaml and document that analyzers are configured post-install via the Cortex API/UI. Delete the dead cortex/deployment.yaml so config has a single source of truth, and reconcile the analyzer list / NFS settings into the surviving file.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading all three files. helm-values.yaml sets cortex.job.kubernetes.sharedVolume.nfs (server nfs-server.shared.svc.cluster.local, path /exports/cortex, lines 33-35) and an analyzers list at lines 46-71 (VirusTotal_GetReport_3_1, AbuseIPDB_1_0, URLhaus_2_0, MaxMind_GeoIP_4_0, FileInfo_8_0). The cortex/deployment.yaml is genuinely dead: deploy-stack.sh deploys Cortex via 'helm upgrade --install cortex strangebee/cortex -f cortex/helm-values.yaml' (line 148-149) and never applies deployment.yaml; it only applies rbac.yaml. The dead deployment.yaml wires the job runner via env vars (job_runner, job_kubernetes_namespace, job_kubernetes_service_account, lines 63-68) with NO NFS volume and NO analyzers. The analyzer-name mismatch is real: deployment.yaml header (line 2) names only VirusTotal/AbuseIPDB/GeoIP while helm-values adds URLhaus and FileInfo. rbac.yaml line 30 comment ('Manage PVCs for analyzer data (NFS claims)') is consistent with the Helm NFS path. The chart-schema-acceptance caveat is honestly hedged as unverified. Legitimate maintainability/correctness concern: config is split across a live Helm values file and a dead manifest, and the analyzer/sharedVolume values may be silently ignored if the chart schema differs.

confirmed.title: TheHive Cortex integration is configured with bearer auth but no API key is wired into the deploy path
confirmed.evidence: helm-values.yaml configures cortex.servers[0] with auth.type: bearer and a comment 'API key set via Kubernetes Secret' (lines 44-46) but no secretKeyRef. secrets.yaml.example defines soc-thehive-secrets.cortex-api-key (line 75) and api-key (line 70), yet neither is referenced in any thehive manifest or in deploy-stack.sh's secret creation. The Cortex API key in TheHive 5 is generated only after Cortex is first running and a user/key is created, so this is a real bootstrap gap: TheHive will start with a Cortex server that has no usable credential, and analyzers will not run.
confirmed.recommendation: Add a secretKeyRef for the Cortex API key under cortex.servers[0].auth (referencing soc-thehive-secrets/cortex-api-key), and document/automate the post-install step: Cortex must be running first, then a user+API key is generated and patched into soc-thehive-secrets. Drop api-key from secrets.yaml.example if nothing consumes it. Note soc-thehive-secrets is not created by deploy-stack.sh at all, so document that it must be applied via secrets.yaml before the thehive deploy.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed helm-values.yaml lines 40-46: cortex.servers[0] has auth.type 'bearer' with comment 'API key set via Kubernetes Secret' (line 44) but NO secretKeyRef. secrets.yaml.example defines soc-thehive-secrets.cortex-api-key (line 75) and api-key (line 70). grep confirms neither cortex-api-key nor api-key (the thehive ones) is referenced in any manifest or deploy-stack.sh -- the only *-api-key refs in the repo are the opencti connector keys (alienvault/abuseipdb/virustotal). Additionally verified deploy-stack.sh only runs 'kubectl create secret generic soc-opencti-secrets' (line 170) and never creates soc-thehive-secrets at all, so even play-secret relies on a manual secrets.yaml apply. The bootstrap gap is real: TheHive starts a Cortex server entry with bearer auth but no usable credential, so analyzers won't run until a post-install key is created and wired. Medium is appropriate.

confirmed.title: Cortex runs with no pod-level user constraint and no seccomp; relies on in-image su to root
confirmed.evidence: Unlike thehive/opencti/rabbitmq (which set runAsNonRoot, runAsUser 1000, drop ALL caps), the cortex StatefulSet sets only allowPrivilegeEscalation: false at the container level. The comment (lines 40-42) explains pod-level runAsUser was removed because 'the image manages user context internally via su' — meaning the container starts as root and drops via su. No capabilities are dropped and no seccompProfile is set. (Dead file, but it encodes the intended Cortex posture.)
confirmed.recommendation: Since deployment.yaml is dead, the durable fix is to ensure the live Helm-deployed Cortex pod is hardened: set the chart's podSecurityContext/securityContext to drop ALL capabilities, add seccompProfile: RuntimeDefault, and run as a fixed non-root UID, using an initContainer to chown /var/log/cortex and /etc/cortex if the entrypoint needs it. If deployment.yaml is kept for any reason, apply the same hardening there; otherwise delete it so it stops encoding a weaker posture than the rest of the stack.
confirmed.verifyNote: Container facts confirmed: the cortex StatefulSet container securityContext sets only allowPrivilegeEscalation: false (line 47) — no runAsNonRoot, no runAsUser, no capabilities.drop, no seccompProfile, and no pod-level securityContext. The comment at lines 40-42 explains pod-level runAsUser was removed because the image manages user context internally via su. Sibling components do harden: k8s/thehive/deployment.yaml (lines 42-52), k8s/opencti/deployment.yaml (lines 40-50, 184-194) and k8s/shared/rabbitmq-deployment.yaml (lines 48-58) all set runAsNonRoot: true, runAsUser: 1000, allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, and capabilities.drop: [ALL]. HOWEVER this is the DEAD deployment.yaml (never applied — see finding #1); the live Cortex pod is deployed by the strangebee/cortex Helm chart, whose securityContext is not governed by this file and was not in scope here. Downgraded medium->low: the file is not applied to the cluster, so it carries no runtime security impact; it only documents intended posture for a manifest that is slated for deletion.

confirmed.title: OpenCTI connectors and VirusTotal enrichment lack securityContext, resource-pinned but unhardened
confirmed.evidence: None of the four connector pod specs (abuseipdb, alienvault-otx, mitre-attack, virustotal) set a securityContext — no runAsNonRoot, no allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, no capabilities.drop, no automountServiceAccountToken: false. The platform and worker pods in deployment.yaml do set these. Connectors fetch and parse untrusted external threat-intel data (a higher-risk parsing surface), so they would benefit most from hardening.
confirmed.recommendation: Add to all four connector pod templates the same hardening block used in deployment.yaml: pod-level runAsNonRoot/runAsUser:1000/fsGroup:1000 and automountServiceAccountToken:false, plus container-level allowPrivilegeEscalation:false and capabilities.drop:[ALL]; add seccompProfile:RuntimeDefault. Note these connector images may run as a fixed UID, so verify runAsUser matches the image before enforcing.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed: I read all four connector pod specs. None set any securityContext, runAsNonRoot, allowPrivilegeEscalation:false, capabilities.drop, or automountServiceAccountToken:false (alienvault-otx.yaml containers start at line 27, abuseipdb at 27, mitre at 27, virustotal at 26 — all jump straight from `spec:`/template to `containers:` with only image/resources/env). By contrast deployment.yaml:38-50 and 182-194 DO set automountServiceAccountToken:false, pod-level runAsNonRoot/runAsUser:1000/fsGroup:1000, and container-level allowPrivilegeEscalation:false + capabilities.drop ALL on the platform and worker. So the asymmetry is real and the hardening gap is genuine. Severity low is appropriate (defense-in-depth hardening, not an active vuln). The 'fetch and parse untrusted external threat-intel' rationale is fair given these are EXTERNAL_IMPORT/INTERNAL_ENRICHMENT connectors.

confirmed.title: VirusTotal enrichment connector kept at replicas: 0 but with CONNECTOR_AUTO=true — silent no-op enrichment
confirmed.evidence: The Deployment is replicas: 0 by default (line 17, scale-up documented for investigation mode) yet sets CONNECTOR_AUTO: 'true' (lines 52-53) to auto-enrich every new observable. With 0 replicas the connector never registers, so 'auto enrich' silently does nothing until someone manually scales it. This is an easy operational trap (analysts will assume VT enrichment is running).
confirmed.recommendation: Make the scale-up requirement impossible to miss: keep replicas:0 but add a louder inline note that CONNECTOR_AUTO has no effect until replicas>=1, surface the `kubectl scale ... --replicas=1` step in the runbook, or adopt a KEDA scale-to-zero pattern. Given the 128Mi footprint, defaulting to replicas:1 is also defensible if the VT free-tier quota can absorb it.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed exactly: virustotal.yaml:17 sets replicas:0 (with scale-up comment at 15-16), and lines 52-53 set CONNECTOR_AUTO 'true' with the inline comment 'Auto-enrich new observables'. With 0 replicas the Deployment runs no pod, so the connector never registers and auto-enrichment is a silent no-op until someone scales to 1 — a genuine operational trap exactly as described. Severity low is correct (operational/UX footgun, not a security or data-loss bug). Note the free-tier limit comment (line 4: 500 req/day) makes the deliberate scale-to-zero default a reasonable design choice, so this is more a documentation/clarity issue than a defect.

confirmed.title: Cortex analyzer Jobs created via RBAC have no NetworkPolicy/securityContext constraints defined for the spawned pods
confirmed.evidence: The cortex-job-runner Role grants create/delete on batch/jobs and create/delete on PVCs in the thehive namespace (lines 23-33). Cortex spawns one pod per analysis, and those analyzer pods (which execute third-party analyzer code against attacker-controlled observables) inherit whatever podSpec Cortex generates — there is no PodSecurity standard, no default securityContext, and no NetworkPolicy in this unit constraining their egress. Analyzer pods routinely make outbound calls (VirusTotal, AbuseIPDB), so unrestricted egress plus root-capable pods is a meaningful blast radius if an analyzer is exploited.
confirmed.recommendation: Apply a restricted pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce label to the thehive namespace (or a dedicated analyzer namespace) so spawned analyzer pods get a non-root/seccomp floor. Tighten allow-egress-thehive: replace the wildcard 'to: []' on port 443 with explicit CIDRs/FQDNs for the required analyzer APIs (VirusTotal, AbuseIPDB, URLhaus, MaxMind), or scope it to a podSelector so only analyzer/cortex pods (not all thehive pods) get external egress. Optionally scope the PVC create/delete verbs with resourceNames if the analyzer claim names are predictable.
confirmed.verifyNote: Partially confirmed with a material correction to the evidence. The RBAC facts are right: cortex-job-runner Role grants create/get/list/watch/delete on batch/jobs (rbac.yaml lines 23-25) and create/get/list/patch/delete on persistentvolumeclaims (lines 31-33). The 'no PodSecurity standard / no default securityContext' part is confirmed — grep for pod-security.kubernetes.io returns nothing and namespaces.yaml (lines 22-26) puts no PodSecurity enforce label on the thehive namespace, so spawned analyzer pods get whatever podSpec Cortex generates. BUT the claim 'there is no NetworkPolicy in this unit constraining their egress' is factually wrong: k8s/network-policies.yaml defines default-deny-egress for namespace thehive (line 567) AND allow-egress-thehive with podSelector: {} (line 582) which DOES apply to analyzer pods. The real defect is that this egress allow rule permits HTTPS via 'to: []' on TCP/443 (lines 611-615) — i.e. unrestricted egress to ANY destination on 443, not absent egress controls. So the underlying concern (analyzer pods that run third-party code against attacker-controlled observables have effectively unrestricted outbound HTTPS plus no PodSecurity floor and no per-pod securityContext) holds, but the policy lives outside the cortex unit and is permissive rather than missing. Severity low is appropriate.

confirmed.title: Prometheus retentionSize (3GB) exceeds the provisioned PVC headroom (5Gi) — risk of disk pressure
confirmed.evidence: prometheusSpec sets retention 15d AND retentionSize 3GB (lines 20-22) on a 5Gi PVC (line 30). Prometheus needs working room above retentionSize for the head block, WAL, and compaction (rule of thumb ~2x). 3GB retention on a 5Gi volume leaves little margin; WAL + compaction can push usage past 5Gi and trigger the OOM/disk-full failure mode before retentionSize-based deletion kicks in.
confirmed.recommendation: Lower retentionSize to ~2GB on the 5Gi PVC, or raise the PVC to 8-10Gi to leave compaction/WAL headroom. Confirm with real disk usage after deploy (e.g. prometheus_tsdb_storage_blocks_bytes and node filesystem metrics).
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed: retention: '15d' (line 21), retentionSize: '3GB' (line 22), and the storageSpec PVC requests storage: '5Gi' (line 30). The cited lines 20-22/30 are accurate (line 20 is the comment header). retentionSize is a soft cap that triggers deletion of oldest blocks, and Prometheus does need headroom above it for the head block, WAL, and compaction. 3GB on a 5Gi volume is tight (~60% allocated to retained TSDB). This is a legitimate sizing risk, not a guaranteed failure — actual usage depends on ingest. Low severity functionality risk is appropriate.

confirmed.title: Comment says 'Disable components we don't need' but kubeStateMetrics/nodeExporter are kept enabled
confirmed.evidence: Line 119 header reads '--- Disable components we don't need (save RAM) ---', but the blocks beneath only set resource limits for kubeStateMetrics (120-127) and nodeExporter (129-136); neither is disabled (no `enabled: false`). Misleading comment; nothing is actually disabled here.
confirmed.recommendation: Change the heading to 'Resource-limit core exporters' (they are needed and should stay enabled). Keep kubeStateMetrics and nodeExporter.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed exactly. Line 119 header reads '--- Disable components we don't need (save RAM) ---', but the blocks beneath only set resources for kubeStateMetrics (lines 120-127) and nodeExporter (lines 129-136). Neither has enabled: false; both remain enabled and only get resource limits. The comment is misleading. This is a pure cleanliness nit — downgrading low -> nit as it has no functional impact.

confirmed.title: Real secrets.yaml is gated by a placeholder-string check, but example file ships base64 placeholders that decode to predictable values
confirmed.evidence: secrets.yaml.example contains base64 values like minio-root-user: bWluaW8tYWRtaW4= (decodes to 'minio-admin', line 32) and admin-user: YWRtaW4= ('admin', line 88) that are real defaults, not CHANGE_ME. deploy-stack.sh validate_secrets only rejects the literal 'Q0hBTkdFX01F' (CHANGE_ME) base64 marker (line 53), so a copied example with the non-CHANGE_ME defaults (minio-admin username) passes validation. CLAUDE.md says a real secrets.yaml is committed/present; confirm it is gitignored. (No secret values printed here beyond the already-public template defaults.)
confirmed.recommendation: secrets.yaml is already gitignored and untracked, so no action needed there. To close the validation gap, strengthen validate_secrets in scripts/deploy-stack.sh to also reject the known weak example defaults (e.g. grep for the base64 of 'minio-admin' and 'admin', or any unchanged example value), or change the example to use CHANGE_ME placeholders for the username fields too (minio-root-user / admin-user) so they get caught by the existing check. Best long-term: extend the env-var-driven `kubectl create secret --from-literal` path (already used for opencti, deploy-stack.sh:170-177) to the other components instead of applying a static secrets.yaml.
confirmed.verifyNote: Independently confirmed by reading the files and decoding the base64. secrets.yaml.example:32 (minio-root-user: bWluaW8tYWRtaW4=) decodes to 'minio-admin' and :88 (admin-user: YWRtaW4=) decodes to 'admin' -- both are real defaults, not CHANGE_ME. deploy-stack.sh validate_secrets (lines 50-65) only runs grep -q "Q0hBTkdFX01F" (the base64 CHANGE_ME marker), so non-CHANGE_ME values are never rejected. The real secrets.yaml exists locally (3237 bytes) but is correctly listed in .gitignore:36, confirmed by `git check-ignore` and is NOT tracked by git (git ls-files --error-unmatch returns not-found) -- so the committed-secret risk is already mitigated. CAVEAT on the finding's wording: the evidence claim that 'a copied example ... passes validation' is imprecise/overstated -- a verbatim-copied example still contains 17 Q0hBTkdFX01F password lines and WOULD be rejected. The accurate, narrower gap is: the username fields ('minio-admin', 'admin') carry no CHANGE_ME marker, so if an operator replaces only the flagged passwords these predictable default usernames survive validation undetected. The core concern (validate_secrets catches only CHANGE_ME, not weak example defaults) is real. Severity 'low' is appropriate -- gitignore correctly protects the real file; residual issue is weak default usernames, a minor hardening gap not an exposure.

confirmed.title: OpenCTI worker pod cannot reach the platform on the configured intra-namespace URL until platform is healthy; no readiness gating or shared TLS settings
confirmed.evidence: The worker (dead manifest) sets OPENCTI_URL http://opencti.opencti.svc...:4000 and only OPENCTI_TOKEN + WORKER_LOG_LEVEL. It carries none of the OpenSearch/Redis/RabbitMQ/MinIO env that the platform has, which is correct for a worker — but it also has no startup backoff config and will crashloop-retry against the platform until ready. Minor, and moot since this file is dead, but worth noting the worker config lives only in the dead manifest while the Helm worker block (helm-values.yaml:70-78) sets only replicas/resources and no OPENCTI_TOKEN wiring — so on the real path the worker's token source is unverified.
confirmed.recommendation: On the canonical Helm path, confirm the chart injects OPENCTI_TOKEN into the worker from soc-opencti-secrets; if it does not, add a worker.extraEnvs block mirroring the platform's. Delete the dead deployment.yaml worker block when the deployment.yaml cleanup happens so the two paths can't diverge.
confirmed.verifyNote: Verified the factual substrate: the dead worker manifest (deployment.yaml:188-211) sets OPENCTI_URL=http://opencti.opencti.svc...:4000 (line 204), OPENCTI_TOKEN from secret (205-209), and WORKER_LOG_LEVEL (210-211), and carries none of the OpenSearch/Redis/RabbitMQ/MinIO env — correct for a worker. It has no startup backoff config and would crashloop-retry until the platform is ready (the worker has internal retry logic upstream, but the manifest adds nothing). I confirmed helm-values.yaml:70-78 sets only worker replicaCount/resources with NO worker extraEnvs and no OPENCTI_TOKEN wiring, so on the real (Helm) path the worker's token source is indeed unverified from these files alone. The cited line range 203-211 is off by one at the start (env block begins at 202); corrected. Severity dropped from low to nit: the crashloop-until-ready behavior is normal Kubernetes self-healing (not a defect), the file is dead, and the only substantive point — unverified worker token wiring on the Helm path — is genuine but unconfirmable from the repo without the chart's templates.

confirmed.title: Inconsistent TLP value formats across connectors
confirmed.evidence: AlienVault uses ALIENVAULT_TLP: 'White' (lines 62-63), while AbuseIPDB and VirusTotal use the STIX 2.1 form 'TLP:AMBER' (abuseipdb.yaml:61, virustotal.yaml:62). Mixed conventions; depending on connector version 'White' (vs 'TLP:CLEAR'/'TLP:WHITE') may be rejected or silently defaulted.
confirmed.recommendation: Standardize the TLP string format each connector expects (verify each against the 6.x connector docs, since ALIENVAULT_TLP and *_MAX_TLP are different settings) and use a consistent style; consider 'TLP:CLEAR' instead of 'White' since TLP:WHITE was renamed in TLP 2.0. Treat the rejection-risk claim as a thing to test, not an established fact.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed the literal values: alienvault-otx.yaml:62-63 uses ALIENVAULT_TLP 'White'; abuseipdb.yaml:60-61 uses ABUSEIPDB_MAX_TLP 'TLP:AMBER'; virustotal.yaml:61-62 uses VIRUSTOTAL_MAX_TLP 'TLP:AMBER'. The value-format inconsistency ('White' vs 'TLP:AMBER') is real and severity nit is correct. IMPORTANT CAVEAT the finding glosses over: these are NOT the same variable — ALIENVAULT_TLP is the TLP marking *assigned* to imported data, whereas *_MAX_TLP on AbuseIPDB/VT is the *maximum TLP the connector is allowed to process* (a filter). So the 'mixed conventions' comparison is partly apples-to-oranges; only the string-format style ('White' bare word vs 'TLP:AMBER' STIX form) is genuinely comparable. The note that 'White' may be rejected/defaulted depending on connector version, and that TLP:WHITE was renamed TLP:CLEAR in TLP 2.0, is plausible upstream behavior I could not verify from the repo — flag as a caution, not a confirmed bug.

confirmed.title: TheHive headless Service (clusterIP: None) used for a single StatefulSet that other namespaces and ingress consume
confirmed.evidence: The thehive Service sets clusterIP: None (line 19, headless). Cortex/TheHive are reached cross-service via thehive.thehive.svc and via Traefik IngressRoute (test-flow.sh:107). A headless service returns pod IPs directly with no stable VIP/load-balancing; for a single-replica HTTP service fronted by ingress, a normal ClusterIP is the conventional choice (and the Cortex Service correctly uses default ClusterIP). (Dead file, but encodes intent; helm-values uses ClusterIP, line 56-57, so this is another raw-vs-helm divergence.)
confirmed.recommendation: Resolved by deleting the dead raw manifest (finding 1); the canonical Helm path already uses a standard ClusterIP. If the raw manifest is kept for any reason, drop clusterIP: None to match Cortex and the Helm values.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed: raw thehive Service sets clusterIP: None at line 19 (headless), for a single-replica StatefulSet. helm-values.yaml uses service.type ClusterIP (lines 56-57). Contrast confirmed: the Cortex raw Service (cortex/deployment.yaml lines 12-18) sets no clusterIP, defaulting to a normal ClusterIP. test-flow.sh line 107 checks the 'thehive' IngressRoute (Traefik route named thehive at traefik-routes.yaml line 93, backing service 'thehive' at line 102), confirming the service is fronted by ingress. Headless adds nothing for a single HTTP backend behind ingress. This lives in the dead manifest, so it is purely a raw-vs-helm divergence with no live impact; nit is correct.

confirmed.title: soc-auth-secret (Traefik BasicAuth) has no creation source — single auth layer for all dashboards is undefined
confirmed.evidence: Every IngressRoute attaches the `soc-basic-auth` Middleware which references `secret: soc-auth-secret`. That secret is only described in an inline comment (`htpasswd -nb admin <password> | base64`); it is created by no script, no manifest, and is not in secrets.yaml or deploy-stack.sh. If the secret is absent, Traefik's basicAuth middleware errors the route (503) — or if a future change removes the middleware, the dashboards (Wazuh/TheHive/Cortex/OpenCTI/Grafana) are exposed with only their own app auth. This is described in CLAUDE.md as the password layer 'on top of each app's own auth' yet it is unmanaged.
confirmed.recommendation: Add secret creation to deploy_ingress() in scripts/deploy-stack.sh BEFORE applying traefik-routes.yaml: generate htpasswd creds (e.g. `htpasswd -nbB admin "$SOC_DASHBOARD_PASSWORD"`) and `kubectl create secret generic soc-auth-secret -n kube-system --from-literal=users=... --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -`. Add a guard in test-flow.sh ([7/8] Ingress section, ~line 104) asserting the secret exists. Document in docs/runbooks/initial-setup.md.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading the file and grepping the whole repo. The soc-basic-auth Middleware (lines 51-61) references `secret: soc-auth-secret` at line 59, attached to every one of the 5 IngressRoutes (wazuh L81, thehive L105, cortex L127, opencti L149, grafana L171). A repo-wide grep for 'soc-auth-secret' returns ONLY traefik-routes.yaml:59 and the inline comment at L61 (`htpasswd -nb admin <password> | base64`). No script, manifest, runbook, or deploy-stack.sh step creates it; deploy_ingress() (deploy-stack.sh:204-219) only installs Traefik and applies traefik-routes.yaml. Traefik's basicAuth references a non-existent secret -> the middleware fails and the route returns an error, so this is a real correctness+security gap. Severity high is fair (it is the only org-wide auth layer and is entirely unmanaged).

confirmed.title: Two competing sources of truth for soc-opencti-secrets (declarative secrets.yaml vs imperative kubectl create in deploy script)
confirmed.evidence: deploy_opencti() builds `soc-opencti-secrets` imperatively from env vars (admin-token, admin-password, health-access-key, alienvault/abuseipdb/virustotal keys), but k8s/shared/secrets.yaml also defines a full `soc-opencti-secrets` (incl. cortex-api-key analog). validate_secrets() only greps secrets.yaml for placeholders and NEVER applies it. So secrets.yaml is effectively dead for opencti, while the script's version omits keys present in the template — and the script silently substitutes `placeholder` for missing TI keys (lines 174-176), producing connectors that fail at runtime with no hard error.
confirmed.recommendation: Pick one owner for soc-opencti-secrets. Either (a) remove its block from k8s/shared/secrets.yaml and keep the imperative kubectl create as the single source, or (b) apply secrets.yaml and drop the imperative create. Additionally, make missing TI keys fail-fast (or omit the env entirely) instead of substituting the literal 'placeholder', so connectors don't boot with junk keys.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. deploy_opencti() (deploy-stack.sh:170-177) creates soc-opencti-secrets imperatively from env vars with `placeholder` fallbacks for the three TI keys (lines 174-176) and an openssl-random fallback for health-access-key (L173). Separately, k8s/shared/secrets.yaml:36-58 defines a full soc-opencti-secrets (admin-password, admin-token, alienvault/abuseipdb/virustotal-api-key, health-access-key) that is NEVER applied — validate_secrets() (L50-65) only greps for the placeholder string and exits. So two definitions exist and the imperative one silently substitutes 'placeholder' for unset TI keys, with only a soft `echo WARNING` (L178-181), no hard failure -> connectors fail at runtime. Real drift/correctness issue. Minor note on evidence: the OpenCTI secret itself does not contain a cortex-api-key; cortex-api-key lives in soc-thehive-secrets (secrets.yaml:75) — the 'cortex-api-key analog' phrasing is slightly loose but does not change the verdict.

confirmed.title: secrets.yaml is never applied by any script — only soc-opencti-secrets gets created at deploy time
confirmed.evidence: validate_secrets() reads k8s/shared/secrets.yaml only to grep for the `Q0hBTkdFX01F` placeholder, then exits 0. No `kubectl apply -f .../secrets.yaml` exists anywhere in scripts/ or docs/. The Helm values for OpenSearch/Redis/RabbitMQ/MinIO reference `existingSecret: soc-shared-secrets`, and the Wazuh/TheHive/Grafana manifests reference soc-wazuh-secrets / soc-thehive-secrets / soc-grafana-secrets — none of which any script creates. A clean `deploy-stack.sh all` run would leave every Helm chart and StatefulSet waiting on missing secrets.
confirmed.recommendation: Add a deploy_secrets step (or `kubectl apply -f "$K8S_DIR/shared/secrets.yaml"` inside deploy_namespaces, before deploy_shared) so the four declarative secrets exist before consuming charts. Reconcile with finding #2 so soc-opencti-secrets isn't created twice. Note secrets.yaml lives in shared ns but defines secrets across opencti/thehive/monitoring/wazuh namespaces — a single `kubectl apply -f` handles all since each has its own metadata.namespace.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. validate_secrets() (L50-65) only `grep -q Q0hBTkdFX01F` (CHANGE_ME) on secrets.yaml and returns; it never applies it. A grep of every .sh and .md in the repo for 'apply ... secrets.yaml' returns nothing. The only secret created at deploy time is soc-opencti-secrets via the imperative kubectl create at L170-177. Meanwhile consumers reference secrets that no script creates: soc-shared-secrets (opensearch.yaml:59, redis.yaml:14, rabbitmq.yaml:13-16, minio.yaml:12, thehive & opencti & wazuh deployments), soc-thehive-secrets (thehive deployment/helm), soc-grafana-secrets (kube-prometheus-values.yaml:54), soc-wazuh-secrets (wazuh dashboard:99). On a clean `deploy-stack.sh all`, every Helm chart/StatefulSet would wait on missing secrets. Real, high-severity deploy-blocking gap.

confirmed.title: secretKeyRef marked optional:true on Wazuh manager/dashboard — pods start silently with empty passwords
confirmed.evidence: INDEXER_PASSWORD (manager) and INDEXER_PASSWORD/DASHBOARD_PASSWORD/API_PASSWORD (dashboard, lines 83/93/101) all set `optional: true` on the secretKeyRef. If `soc-shared-secrets`/`soc-wazuh-secrets` is missing or the key is absent, the env var is simply unset and the container boots with a blank password to OpenSearch and the Wazuh API instead of failing fast. Given the secret-creation gaps above, this masks misconfiguration and can leave OpenSearch reachable with empty creds during the window before security index init.
confirmed.recommendation: Remove `optional: true` from all four secretKeyRefs (manager deployment.yaml:93; dashboard deployment.yaml:83/93/101) so the kubelet refuses to start the pod when a credential is missing (fail-closed). This also surfaces the missing-secret problem from #3 immediately instead of silently.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading both files. Manager: INDEXER_PASSWORD secretKeyRef (soc-shared-secrets/opensearch-password) has optional:true at deployment.yaml:93. Dashboard: three secretKeyRefs all optional:true — INDEXER_PASSWORD L83, DASHBOARD_PASSWORD L93, API_PASSWORD L101 (file k8s/wazuh/dashboard/deployment.yaml). With optional:true, a missing secret/key leaves the env var unset and the container boots rather than failing fast. Combined with findings #2/#3 (these secrets are never created), this masks misconfiguration. Severity medium is appropriate: it's a fail-open hardening gap, not directly exploitable on its own (the real-world impact depends on how the Wazuh images treat an unset password). The dashboard occurrences (L83/93/101) are part of the same finding and the file:line should reference the dashboard too.

confirmed.title: TheHive disables TLS certificate verification to OpenSearch (TH_DB_ES_SSL_VERIFY=false)
confirmed.evidence: `TH_DB_ES_SSL_ENABLED: "true"` but `TH_DB_ES_SSL_VERIFY: "false"`. TheHive connects to OpenSearch over HTTPS with admin credentials but does not validate the server certificate, so the channel is vulnerable to in-cluster MITM/cred theft. OpenCTI (k8s/opencti/deployment.yaml:95-98) and Wazuh Manager (k8s/wazuh/manager/deployment.yaml:94-97) by contrast mount and trust the CA (`ELASTICSEARCH__SSL__CA_CERTS` / `FILEBEAT_SSL_CERTIFICATE_AUTHORITIES`) — TheHive is the inconsistent outlier.
confirmed.recommendation: Mount the OpenSearch CA into TheHive (a 'shared'-namespace populated copy of the opensearch-ca-cert ConfigMap pattern OpenCTI uses; note the existing ConfigMap is only defined in opencti and wazuh namespaces, so a thehive-namespace copy must be added), set TH_DB_ES_SSL_VERIFY="true", and point TheHive at the mounted ca.crt. Note the CA ConfigMap currently ships a placeholder (see related finding), so it must be populated for verification to actually succeed.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading the file. Lines 67-68 set TH_DB_ES_URI to https://opensearch-cluster-master.shared.svc.cluster.local:9200; lines 69-70 set TH_DB_ES_SSL_ENABLED="true"; lines 71-72 set TH_DB_ES_SSL_VERIFY="false". So TheHive uses HTTPS with admin-level OpenSearch creds (shared opensearch-password used by sibling services) but does not validate the server cert -> in-cluster MITM / credential interception is possible. The cited contrast is accurate: OpenCTI (opencti/deployment.yaml:95-98 ELASTICSEARCH__SSL__CA_CERTS + NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS, CA mounted via configMap at 143-145/158-161) and Wazuh manager (wazuh/manager/deployment.yaml:94-97 FILEBEAT_SSL_VERIFICATION_MODE=certificate + FILEBEAT_SSL_CERTIFICATE_AUTHORITIES, opensearch-certs mounted at 107-109/135-137) both trust the CA. TheHive is the inconsistent outlier. Title line range 71-72 is correct but the SSL_ENABLED context starts at 69, so widened to 69-72. Medium is appropriate (in-cluster network, not internet-exposed; requires an attacker already on the pod network).

confirmed.title: Cortex talks to OpenSearch over plaintext HTTP while the rest of the stack uses HTTPS
confirmed.evidence: `es_uri: "http://opensearch-cluster-master.shared.svc.cluster.local:9200"`. OpenSearch is configured with `plugins.security.ssl.http.enabled: true` (k8s/shared/opensearch.yaml:75) so HTTP on 9200 will either be rejected or, worse, downgrade Cortex's index traffic (case/analyzer data) to cleartext across namespaces. Every other consumer uses `https://...:9200`.
confirmed.recommendation: Change es_uri to https://opensearch-cluster-master.shared.svc.cluster.local:9200 and supply the OpenSearch CA so Cortex trusts the cert (mount a populated opensearch-ca-cert ConfigMap in the thehive namespace and configure Cortex/JVM trust accordingly, e.g. via a truststore or es.ssl settings). Verify Cortex actually connects to and creates its 'cortex' index after the change, since Cortex 3.x ES/TLS config can be finicky.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading the file. cortex/deployment.yaml lines 59-60 set es_uri="http://opensearch-cluster-master.shared.svc.cluster.local:9200" (plaintext HTTP), while TheHive (68), Wazuh manager (85), Wazuh dashboard (75) and OpenCTI (87) all use https://...:9200. OpenSearch is configured with plugins.security.ssl.http.enabled: true (shared/opensearch.yaml:75), so the HTTP listener on 9200 will not speak cleartext HTTP and Cortex's connection will fail at the TLS layer rather than silently downgrade -- the 'or worse, downgrade to cleartext' phrasing in the evidence overstates the risk (OpenSearch will reject, not downgrade). Practically this is both a likely functionality break (Cortex cannot reach its index) and a config inconsistency. Severity medium is reasonable; arguably this is more functionality than security since the connection will fail rather than leak data, but the misconfiguration is real and worth fixing.

confirmed.title: Monitoring egress allows scraping shared exporters but shared namespace has no matching ingress allow — Redis/MinIO/RabbitMQ metrics blocked
confirmed.evidence: allow-egress-monitoring permits Prometheus to reach all namespaces on ports 9100/9200/9000/9001/4000/55000/14269/15692, but the shared namespace's ingress rules only allow monitoring->OpenSearch:9200 (allow-opensearch) and monitoring->RabbitMQ:15692 (allow-rabbitmq). There is no `allow-prometheus-scrape` NetworkPolicy in the shared namespace (it exists for wazuh/thehive/opencti at lines 411/432/455). With default-deny-ingress active in shared, Prometheus cannot scrape Redis exporter (9121), MinIO metrics (9000), or node-exporter (9100) in shared, despite the Helm values enabling those metrics/serviceMonitors. Monitoring egress also omits Redis exporter port 9121 entirely.
confirmed.recommendation: Add an allow-prometheus-scrape NetworkPolicy in the shared namespace (mirroring wazuh/thehive/opencti) permitting monitoring -> 9100 (node-exporter), 9121 (Redis exporter), and 9000 (MinIO metrics). RabbitMQ:15692 is already covered by allow-rabbitmq lines 104-111 so does not need re-adding. Also add port 9121 to allow-egress-monitoring (lines 715-734) so Prometheus egress can reach the Redis exporter.
confirmed.verifyNote: CONFIRMED with one correction. The shared namespace has NO allow-prometheus-scrape policy (grep confirms it exists only in wazuh:412, thehive:433, opencti:456). default-deny-ingress is active in shared (lines 16-24). Helm values confirm metrics ARE enabled: redis.yaml:44-45 (metrics.enabled: true -> bitnami redis exporter on 9121), minio.yaml:62-64 (metrics.serviceMonitor.enabled: true), rabbitmq.yaml:37-38 (metrics.enabled: true -> 15692). Against shared ingress: allow-redis (lines 64-79) permits only opencti on 6379, so Prometheus->Redis exporter:9121 is BLOCKED. allow-minio (lines 118-136) permits only opencti+thehive on 9000, so Prometheus->MinIO metrics:9000 is BLOCKED. There is no shared ingress allow for node-exporter:9100, so 9100 is BLOCKED. CORRECTION to the title/evidence: RabbitMQ metrics are NOT actually blocked — allow-rabbitmq has a dedicated second ingress rule (lines 104-111) explicitly permitting monitoring->15692. OpenSearch:9200 is also allowed (allow-opensearch lines 50-55). Also confirmed allow-egress-monitoring (lines 711-734) lists 9090/9093/9100/9200/4000/9000/9001/55000/14269/15692 but omits Redis exporter 9121, so even if shared ingress were fixed, egress could not reach 9121. Severity medium is correct: real monitoring blind spot for Redis/MinIO/node-exporter metrics with no security impact.

confirmed.title: Egress HTTPS rule `to: []` lets opencti/thehive pods reach ANY destination on 443, including in-cluster and link-local
confirmed.evidence: allow-egress-thehive (612) and allow-egress-opencti (669) use `- to: []` with port 443 to permit internet egress for analyzers/connectors. An empty `to` selector means all IPs — including the cluster pod/service CIDR, the node, and 169.254.0.0/16 (cloud/metadata-style link-local). Combined with SSRF-prone connectors (VirusTotal/AbuseIPDB enrich attacker-supplied observables), a compromised connector can pivot to any 443 endpoint cluster-wide, not just the public internet.
confirmed.recommendation: Replace '- to: []' on the 443 rules (lines 612 and 669) with an ipBlock that whitelists 0.0.0.0/0 and excepts the cluster pod CIDR, service CIDR, 169.254.0.0/16 link-local, and RFC1918 ranges as appropriate (e.g. to: [{ ipBlock: { cidr: 0.0.0.0/0, except: [<podCIDR>, <serviceCIDR>, 169.254.0.0/16, 10.0.0.0/8] } }]) so HTTPS egress reaches genuine internet ranges only and cannot reach internal services or the metadata endpoint.
confirmed.verifyNote: CONFIRMED. allow-egress-thehive uses '- to: []' with TCP/443 at lines 612-615, and allow-egress-opencti uses the identical '- to: []' with TCP/443 at lines 669-672. In NetworkPolicy semantics an empty 'to' array (a peer list with zero entries) matches all destinations, so 443 egress is permitted to the entire pod/service CIDR, the node, and 169.254.0.0/16 link-local — not just public internet. Both policies apply to podSelector: {} (lines 582, 637), so every pod in those namespaces gets this. CLAUDE.md confirms SSRF-prone connectors (VirusTotal, AbuseIPDB, AlienVault OTX) run here and enrich attacker-supplied observables, making cluster-internal pivot on 443 a realistic abuse path. Severity medium is appropriate — defense-in-depth egress weakness, exploitation requires a compromised/abused connector.

confirmed.title: kube-system (Traefik/Grafana datasource) and core-dns paths rely on broad namespace selectors; no policy isolates Traefik->only intended backends
confirmed.evidence: Dashboards allow ingress from the entire `kube-system` namespace (allow-wazuh-dashboard, allow-thehive, allow-cortex, allow-opencti, allow-grafana). Any pod in kube-system (a large, busy namespace in k3s: coredns, metrics-server, local-path-provisioner, traefik, helm jobs) can reach these app ports, not just Traefik. The intent is 'from Traefik only'.
confirmed.recommendation: Combine the kube-system namespaceSelector with a podSelector matching Traefik's labels (e.g. app.kubernetes.io/name: traefik) in the same 'from' peer entry so only Traefik pods can reach these app ports. Apply to allow-wazuh-dashboard (214), allow-thehive (254), allow-cortex (279), allow-opencti (318), and allow-grafana (357).
confirmed.verifyNote: CONFIRMED. allow-wazuh-dashboard (lines 214-221) allows ingress to port 5601 from a bare namespaceSelector matching kube-system with no podSelector, so any pod in kube-system can reach it, not just Traefik. The same broad kube-system namespaceSelector pattern repeats for allow-thehive:9000 (lines 254-262), allow-cortex:9001 (lines 279-285), allow-opencti:4000 (lines 318-324), and allow-grafana:3000 (lines 357-362). In k3s, kube-system is a busy namespace (coredns, metrics-server, local-path-provisioner, traefik, helm install jobs), so this over-grants beyond the stated 'from Traefik only' intent. Severity low/design is appropriate — minor over-permission, no direct exploit absent a compromised kube-system pod.

confirmed.title: No Pod Security Standards enforced at namespace level (no pod-security.kubernetes.io labels)
confirmed.evidence: None of the five namespaces carry `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce` (or warn/audit) labels. PSS is the modern replacement for PodSecurityPolicy and is built into k3s. Without it, nothing prevents a future workload in shared/opencti/etc. from running privileged or as root; the per-pod securityContexts that do exist are advisory only. The privileged Wazuh agent and NFS server would need exemptions, but the rest could be 'restricted'/'baseline'.
confirmed.recommendation: Add pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce labels to the namespaces in k8s/namespaces.yaml: restricted (or baseline) for shared/opencti/thehive/monitoring, and privileged for wazuh (required by the agent DaemonSet and dashboard init). Note the shared namespace currently hosts the privileged NFS server, so either set shared to baseline/privileged or relocate NFS to its own namespace before enforcing restricted there (see NFS finding). Consider starting with warn/audit before enforce to surface violations without breaking existing workloads.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading the full file and grepping the entire k8s/ tree. namespaces.yaml defines exactly 5 namespaces (shared, wazuh, thehive, opencti, monitoring), each carrying only app.kubernetes.io/part-of and purpose labels. No pod-security.kubernetes.io/{enforce,warn,audit} labels exist anywhere under k8s/ (grep returned NONE FOUND). PSS is built into k3s and would otherwise fail-close against privilege regressions; the existing per-pod securityContexts are advisory only. CLAUDE.md confirms the shared namespace colocates OpenSearch/Redis/RabbitMQ/MinIO/NFS, so an unconstrained namespace there is a real defense-in-depth gap. Cited line range 3-43 matches the file (first --- at L3, last namespace block ends at L42-43). Severity medium is defensible: this is a missing fail-closed control rather than an actively exploitable misconfiguration.

confirmed.title: Wazuh agent DaemonSet is privileged + hostPID + hostNetwork + mounts host / — large blast radius (partly inherent but mountPath / is broad)
confirmed.evidence: The agent runs `privileged: true`, `hostNetwork: true`, `hostPID: true`, tolerates all taints, and bind-mounts the entire host root `/` at /host. The mounts are readOnly (good), but a privileged container can remount rw and hostPID exposes all host processes. This is partially inherent to FIM/rootcheck, but mounting all of `/` (rather than the specific dirs Wazuh inspects) maximizes exposure if the agent image is compromised.
confirmed.recommendation: Replace the host-root hostPath (path: /) with the specific directories Wazuh FIM/rootcheck actually inspects, keeping readOnly. Drop hostPID unless rootcheck process inspection requires it. Even with privileged: true, add capabilities.drop: [ALL] (re-adding only what is needed) and a seccompProfile RuntimeDefault to reduce surface. Pin the necessary privileged exemption explicitly via the wazuh-namespace PSS label rather than leaving the whole cluster unenforced. Note host-etc (/etc -> /host/etc) is redundant once / is mounted; this can be cleaned up alongside narrowing the mount.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading the file. spec.template.spec sets hostNetwork: true (L23), hostPID: true (L24), tolerations operator: Exists to run on all nodes including control plane (L20-21), and securityContext.privileged: true (L65). The host-root volume is hostPath path: / (L67-69) mounted at /host with readOnly: true (L42-44); host-var-log (/var/log) and host-etc (/etc) are also mounted readOnly. As the finding correctly notes, readOnly is advisory for a privileged container that can remount rw, and hostPID exposes all host processes. Mounting all of / rather than the specific FIM/rootcheck paths does maximize exposure if the image is compromised. The cited start line 23 is accurate for hostNetwork; I widened the range start to 18 to include the full pod spec (tolerations begin at L20) since the toleration-all-taints claim is part of the finding. Severity low is appropriate: this blast radius is largely inherent to a host-level FIM/rootcheck agent, and the incremental risk is the breadth of the / mount.

confirmed.title: NFS server runs fully privileged with no capability scoping
confirmed.evidence: nfs-ganesha container sets `privileged: true` with the justification 'Required for NFS server'. ganesha/kernel NFS typically needs only SYS_ADMIN (and DAC_READ_SEARCH); full privileged is broader than required. The export uses root_squash (good), but a privileged pod in the shared namespace alongside OpenSearch/Redis/MinIO is a lateral-movement risk.
confirmed.recommendation: Attempt to run with privileged: false plus a scoped capability set (e.g. capabilities.add: [SYS_ADMIN, DAC_READ_SEARCH, SETPCAP]); the erichough/nfs-server image may still require privileged for kernel NFS, so test first. If privileged remains necessary, relocate NFS into a dedicated namespace labeled with PSS privileged rather than colocating it with the shared data stores, so the privileged exemption does not weaken the namespace that hosts OpenSearch/Redis/RabbitMQ/MinIO. Keep root_squash.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading the file. The nfs-ganesha container (image erichough/nfs-server:2.2.1) sets securityContext.privileged: true with the inline justification 'Required for NFS server' at L60-61. The export NFS_EXPORT_0 uses root_squash (L56), which is good. The Deployment is in namespace: shared (L27), which per CLAUDE.md colocates OpenSearch/Redis/RabbitMQ/MinIO — so a privileged pod there is a genuine lateral-movement concern. Cited file:line 60-61 is exact. Severity low is appropriate: privileged is broader than strictly required, but this is a single backing pod with root_squash and the practical exploitability requires first compromising the NFS image.

confirmed.title: Threat-intel API keys in on-disk secrets.yaml are all identical placeholder/test values (alienvault==abuseipdb==virustotal==thehive api-key)
confirmed.evidence: The (gitignored, untracked — correctly not committed) on-disk secrets.yaml has the AlienVault OTX, AbuseIPDB, VirusTotal, and TheHive `api-key` fields decoding to the exact same value. Distinct real vendor keys would never collide; this indicates copy-pasted dummy/test values, so the connectors and TheHive API auth will not actually work and validate_secrets() (which only checks for the CHANGE_ME string) passes them. Note: this is local-file hygiene; nothing is leaked to git.
confirmed.recommendation: Replace with four distinct real keys before deploy. Strengthen validate_secrets() to also flag duplicated values across distinct keys (e.g. detect identical base64 across alienvault/abuseipdb/virustotal/api-key), not just the literal CHANGE_ME marker.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed without decoding the secrets: I string-compared the base64 fields. alienvault-api-key (L51), abuseipdb-api-key (L53), virustotal-api-key (L55), and TheHive api-key (L70) are byte-identical base64 (same md5 of each base64 string). Four real, distinct vendor keys would never collide, so these are copy-pasted dummy values and the connectors + TheHive API auth will not work. validate_secrets() only checks for the CHANGE_ME marker, so these pass. Confirmed the file is gitignored/untracked (`.gitignore:36` lists k8s/shared/secrets.yaml; `git ls-files` returns nothing) so no leak to git — this is local-file hygiene only. cortex-api-key (L75) is distinct, so the collision is limited to the four named fields. Low severity is correct.

confirmed.title: cert-manager / TLS cert generation is documented but not automated; referenced generate-tls-cert.sh does not exist
confirmed.evidence: self-signed-issuer.yaml's header says 'Usage: ./scripts/generate-tls-cert.sh' but that file does not exist in scripts/. soc-tls-cert, opensearch-certs, and wazuh-dashboard-certs are all referenced by manifests (traefik-routes, opensearch.yaml, wazuh manager/dashboard) with no automated creation. deploy-stack.sh never installs cert-manager nor applies self-signed-issuer.yaml, so an operator following the scripts ends up with IngressRoutes and OpenSearch transport TLS pointing at non-existent secrets.
confirmed.recommendation: Either create the referenced scripts/generate-tls-cert.sh (openssl + kubectl create secret tls soc-tls-cert -n kube-system, plus the OpenSearch CA/cert secrets) and wire it into deploy-stack.sh before deploy_ingress, or apply self-signed-issuer.yaml + install cert-manager from deploy-stack.sh. If neither, remove the dangling generate-tls-cert.sh reference at self-signed-issuer.yaml:7 and document the manual cert prerequisite explicitly in the runbook.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. self-signed-issuer.yaml line 7 references './scripts/generate-tls-cert.sh'; find across the repo returns no such file (only scripts present: deploy-stack.sh, setup-ism.sh, setup-lima.sh, teardown.sh, test-flow.sh) and grep finds the string only at self-signed-issuer.yaml:7.
