Read the full deploy-stack.sh (245 lines): it applies namespaces, network-policies, helm charts, wazuh/opencti configmaps+connectors, and traefik routes, but never installs cert-manager, never applies self-signed-issuer.yaml, and never creates any TLS secret (the only kubectl create secret is soc-opencti-secrets, generic, at line 170). soc-tls-cert is required by traefik-routes.yaml (TLSStore:48 and every IngressRoute tls.secretName) with no automated creation. An operator following the scripts ends up with IngressRoutes/TLSStore pointing at a non-existent soc-tls-cert secret. Low severity is appropriate -- this is an operability/docs gap, not an exploitable vuln.

confirmed.title: Duplicate/competing TheHive definitions: raw deployment.yaml StatefulSet vs strangebee Helm chart used by deploy script
confirmed.evidence: deploy-stack.sh:143 installs TheHive via `helm strangebee/thehive -f helm-values.yaml`, yet a full raw StatefulSet manifest (k8s/thehive/deployment.yaml) with its own securityContext, env, and TH_DB_ES_SSL_VERIFY=false also exists and is never applied by any script. Two divergent definitions create confusion about which security settings are actually in effect (the Helm chart's values may differ from the raw manifest the auditor reads). Same pattern exists for Cortex (raw deployment.yaml + cortex/helm-values.yaml) and OpenCTI (raw deployment.yaml + helm-values.yaml).
confirmed.recommendation: Pick one deployment method per component. The deploy script uses Helm exclusively for thehive/cortex/opencti, so make Helm canonical and delete (or move to a clearly-labeled docs/reference dir) the three unused raw manifests: k8s/thehive/deployment.yaml, k8s/thehive/cortex/deployment.yaml, and k8s/opencti/deployment.yaml. This removes the divergent TLS settings (raw https+SSL_VERIFY=false vs Helm plain http) and the stale 'no public Helm chart' comment, leaving a single source of truth. If any raw manifest is intentionally the real deployment path, instead update deploy-stack.sh to `kubectl apply` it and remove the corresponding helm-values.yaml.
confirmed.verifyNote: Independently confirmed by reading all cited files. (1) k8s/thehive/deployment.yaml lines 21-28 are a full raw StatefulSet (kind: StatefulSet at line 22), with securityContext and env including TH_DB_ES_SSL_VERIFY=false at lines 71-72. (2) scripts/deploy-stack.sh line 143 installs TheHive via `helm upgrade --install thehive strangebee/thehive -f .../thehive/helm-values.yaml` (the cited line 143 is exact). Cortex via Helm at line 148, OpenCTI via Helm at line 184. (3) grep across all .sh/.yaml/.yml/.md files found ZERO references to thehive/deployment.yaml, opencti/deployment.yaml, or cortex/deployment.yaml; no kustomization files exist; deploy-stack.sh never runs `kubectl apply` on any deployment.yaml. So all three raw manifests are dead/unapplied. (4) The divergence is concrete and security-relevant: raw thehive manifest uses TH_DB_ES_URI=https://...:9200 with SSL_ENABLED=true/SSL_VERIFY=false, while helm-values.yaml line 23 uses plain http://...:9200 with no TLS at all — genuinely different security postures for the same component. (5) Same pattern confirmed for Cortex (cortex/deployment.yaml raw StatefulSet + cortex/helm-values.yaml) and OpenCTI (opencti/deployment.yaml raw Deployment+Worker + opencti/helm-values.yaml). Additional corroborating contradiction: the raw manifest header (line 2) claims 'StrangeBee doesn't maintain a public Helm chart' yet both the Helm values file and deploy script reference strangebee/thehive — the raw file's own rationale is stale. Severity 'low' is correct: the unused raw manifest's SSL_VERIFY=false is dead config (never applied), so this is a maintainability/single-source-of-truth and audit-confusion issue, not an active vulnerability.

confirmed.title: Cortex job-runner ServiceAccount token is auto-mounted (cannot be disabled) and the SA can create arbitrary Jobs/PVCs in thehive namespace
confirmed.evidence: cortex StatefulSet uses serviceAccountName: cortex-job-runner and (correctly, since it needs the API) does not set automountServiceAccountToken:false. The Role grants create/delete on jobs and create/delete/patch on PVCs in the thehive namespace. A compromised Cortex (it executes analyzers on attacker-influenced observables) can spin up arbitrary Job pods in thehive — and those analyzer Job pods are created with no enforced securityContext/PSS, so they could run as root. This is a contained-but-real privilege path.
confirmed.recommendation: Apply a Pod Security Standards label (pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted or baseline) to the thehive namespace in k8s/namespaces.yaml so dynamically-created analyzer Job pods cannot run as root or privileged. Since analyzers may need some capabilities, consider a dedicated lower-privilege namespace for analyzer Jobs (separate from TheHive's data) and update job_kubernetes_namespace accordingly. If finer control is needed, add a ValidatingAdmissionPolicy or Kyverno policy forcing runAsNonRoot + drop-ALL on Cortex-created Jobs. The Role itself is appropriately namespace-scoped and minimal, so no RBAC change is strictly required.
confirmed.verifyNote: Independently confirmed by reading the manifests. rbac.yaml: kind: Role at line 15, rules block lines 21-33 grant batch/jobs create,get,list,watch,delete (lines 23-25) and persistentvolumeclaims create,get,list,patch,delete (lines 31-33) in namespace thehive. Role is namespace-scoped (not ClusterRole), bound only to the cortex-job-runner SA. deployment.yaml line 39 sets serviceAccountName: cortex-job-runner and does NOT set automountServiceAccountToken:false (the automount:false hits in grep are all in other files: thehive, rabbitmq, opencti, wazuh), so the token is auto-mounted — confirmed. namespaces.yaml (thehive at lines 20-26) has NO pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce label, and there is no Kyverno/Gatekeeper/ValidatingAdmissionPolicy anywhere in the repo (grep returned nothing), confirming nothing forces non-root/drop-ALL on Cortex-created analyzer Job pods, which are created dynamically at runtime (helm-values only sets jobResources, no securityContext). Severity correctly stated as low: contained, namespace-scoped, and requires prior Cortex compromise to exploit. Note the cited range 15-34 is slightly off; the Role block is lines 14-33.

confirmed.title: Egress policies use podSelector:{} (allow all pods in ns) rather than per-app rules; metrics egress lists many ports as one broad block
confirmed.evidence: allow-egress-wazuh/thehive/opencti/monitoring all apply to `podSelector: {}` (every pod in the namespace) and lump all destination ports into single rules. This is acceptable default-deny+allow hygiene, but it means e.g. the Wazuh dashboard pod has the same egress as the manager. Per-app egress (podSelector by app label) would be tighter and is the state-of-the-art pattern.
confirmed.recommendation: Where practical, scope egress allows by app-label podSelector so each workload reaches only what it needs (e.g. separate egress for wazuh-manager vs wazuh-dashboard). This is a defense-in-depth refinement beyond the current namespace-level allows, not a required fix.
confirmed.verifyNote: CONFIRMED. allow-egress-wazuh (line 536), allow-egress-thehive (582), allow-egress-opencti (637), and allow-egress-monitoring (694) all apply to podSelector: {} (every pod in the namespace), and allow-egress-monitoring lumps all 10 destination ports into a single rule (lines 712-734). The cited line 533 is the metadata block; the actual podSelector: {} is at line 536 (allow-egress-wazuh). The finding correctly self-characterizes this as acceptable default-deny+allow hygiene and a defense-in-depth nit rather than a defect — e.g. the Wazuh dashboard pod gets the same egress as the manager. Severity nit/design is correct.

confirmed.title: secrets.yaml.example documents echo -n | base64 workflow that can leak secrets into shell history
confirmed.evidence: The template instructs `echo -n "your-password" | base64`, which writes plaintext secrets into ~/.zsh_history/.bash_history. The deploy-stack.sh imperative path (kubectl create secret --from-literal) has the same exposure for env-var-set values.
confirmed.recommendation: Update the template comment to recommend `read -rs SECRET; printf %s "$SECRET" | base64`, `kubectl create secret --from-file=`, or a SOPS/age-encrypted secrets file. Also note that K8s Secrets are base64 (not encrypted) at rest unless etcd/k3s secrets-encryption is enabled, and document enabling `--secrets-encryption` on k3s.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. secrets.yaml.example:4 reads: `# To encode a value: echo -n "your-password" | base64` (the same comment also appears in the live secrets.yaml:4). Plaintext on the command line lands in ~/.zsh_history / ~/.bash_history. The deploy-stack.sh imperative path (kubectl create secret --from-literal from env vars, L170-177) shares the exposure for any values set inline. Valid hygiene nit; severity nit is appropriate.

confirmed.title: No CI/CD at all — .github has only templates, no workflows
confirmed.file: .github/ (confirmed: no workflows/ subdirectory; only ISSUE_TEMPLATE/, PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md, SECURITY.md)
confirmed.evidence: `.github/` contains only ISSUE_TEMPLATE/, PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md, SECURITY.md. There is no `.github/workflows/` directory. Nothing lints bash (shellcheck), validates k8s manifests (kubeconform/kubeval), scans for secrets (gitleaks/trufflehog), or builds the 3 TypeScript MCP servers. The PR template asks reviewers to manually confirm `npm run build` and `test-flow.sh` pass, but nothing enforces it.
confirmed.recommendation: Add .github/workflows/ci.yml triggered on pull_request and push. At minimum: (1) shellcheck across scripts/*.sh; (2) kubeconform (or kubeval) over k8s/**/*.yaml to validate manifests against the K8s schema; (3) gitleaks secret scan — especially important given the repo handles K8s Secrets/credentials per CLAUDE.md; (4) a matrix job running 'npm ci && npm run build' for each mcp-servers/* package (build shared first since the others depend on it via file:../shared). Pin every action to a full 40-char commit SHA, not a tag. Optionally make these required status checks via branch protection so the manual PR-template checkboxes become enforced gates.
confirmed.verifyNote: Independently confirmed by listing the filesystem. .github/ contains exactly ISSUE_TEMPLATE/ (bug_report.yml, feature_request.yml, config.yml), PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md, and SECURITY.md. No .github/workflows/ directory exists, and no alternative CI config (GitLab/CircleCI/Travis/Jenkins/Azure/Drone) is present anywhere in the repo. The repo has real buildable artifacts that go unchecked: 4 TypeScript packages under mcp-servers/ (shared, opencti-mcp, thehive-mcp, wazuh-mcp), each with a 'build': 'tsc' script and tsconfig.json; bash automation in scripts/ (deploy-stack.sh, setup-ism.sh, setup-lima.sh, teardown.sh, test-flow.sh); and a k8s/ tree of manifests. grep for shellcheck|gitleaks|trufflehog|kubeconform|kubeval returns zero hits anywhere. The PR template (lines 26-28) explicitly asks reviewers to manually confirm './scripts/test-flow.sh' passes and 'MCP servers build cleanly (npm run build)', proving these are check-the-box manual steps with no automated enforcement. Minor inaccuracy in the original finding: it says '3 TypeScript MCP servers' but there are 4 buildable npm packages (the 3 servers plus the shared library) — not material to the verdict. The original file reference pointed at a non-existent path; corrected to cite the actual .github/ directory contents.

confirmed.title: Deploy never generates the TLS cert that all IngressRoutes depend on — HTTPS ingress is broken
confirmed.evidence: deploy_ingress() runs `kubectl apply -f traefik-routes.yaml`, which sets a default cert store and per-route `secretName: soc-tls-cert` (traefik-routes.yaml:48,84,108,130,152,174). But the script never installs cert-manager, never applies k8s/ingress/self-signed-issuer.yaml, and never creates the `soc-tls-cert` secret. CLAUDE.md and SECURITY.md both claim a cert-manager ClusterIssuer auto-generates it, but nothing applies it. Result: Traefik has no cert and HTTPS dashboards at https://soc.homelab.local/* fail.
confirmed.recommendation: Add a cert step to deploy_ingress() before applying routes: gate on the soc-tls-cert secret existing — if absent, either install cert-manager + apply k8s/ingress/self-signed-issuer.yaml (and wait for the Certificate to be Ready), or run the documented openssl + `kubectl create secret tls soc-tls-cert -n kube-system` path. Also fix CLAUDE.md:25 which falsely claims the ClusterIssuer is applied, and create the referenced scripts/generate-tls-cert.sh or remove that reference from self-signed-issuer.yaml:7.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. deploy_ingress() (deploy-stack.sh:204-219) installs Traefik then runs `kubectl apply -f traefik-routes.yaml` with no cert step. traefik-routes.yaml references secretName: soc-tls-cert at lines 48 (TLSStore defaultCertificate) and per-route at 84, 108, 130, 152, 174. The script never installs cert-manager, never applies k8s/ingress/self-signed-issuer.yaml, and never creates the soc-tls-cert secret. Grep across scripts/ and README found zero references to self-signed-issuer.yaml, generate-tls-cert.sh, cert-manager, or `create secret tls`. There is no scripts/generate-tls-cert.sh (the comment in self-signed-issuer.yaml:7 references it but it does not exist). CLAUDE.md:24-25 explicitly claims a cert-manager ClusterIssuer auto-generates the cert, but nothing applies the ClusterIssuer/Certificate. Result: Traefik has no usable default cert and HTTPS routes fail. Severity adjusted critical->high: this is a homelab single-node SOC (CLAUDE.md:4), self-signed cert is generated via documented manual steps, and it is a deploy-time failure that is loud (routes simply don't serve TLS), not a data-loss/security-bypass. Real and impactful, but high fits better than critical for a homelab convenience stack.

confirmed.title: Referenced scripts/generate-tls-cert.sh does not exist
confirmed.evidence: self-signed-issuer.yaml and traefik-routes.yaml comments instruct './scripts/generate-tls-cert.sh', but `ls scripts/` shows only deploy-stack/setup-ism/setup-lima/teardown/test-flow. The TLS cert path documented in the runbooks cannot be executed.
confirmed.recommendation: Remove the './scripts/generate-tls-cert.sh' line from self-signed-issuer.yaml:7 (the cert-manager Certificate in the same file plus the inline openssl fallback already cover cert generation), or create the script if a one-command path is desired. Standardize on cert-manager as the single supported path.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed: self-signed-issuer.yaml:7 references './scripts/generate-tls-cert.sh' and that file does not exist (scripts/ holds only deploy-stack.sh, setup-ism.sh, setup-lima.sh, teardown.sh, test-flow.sh; grep -rn confirms self-signed-issuer.yaml:7 is the ONLY reference in the repo). However the evidence is partly wrong and the severity is overstated. (1) traefik-routes.yaml does NOT instruct './scripts/generate-tls-cert.sh' — its lines 15-21 inline the openssl+kubectl commands directly, never naming the script. (2) self-signed-issuer.yaml is not a broken path: lines 8-14 give the manual openssl+kubectl fallback right under the dead reference, and lines 17-49 ship a fully working cert-manager ClusterIssuer + Certificate, which CLAUDE.md documents as the supported cert path. Nothing in deploy-stack.sh or test-flow.sh invokes the script, so deploys are not blocked. This is a stale documentation reference, not a functional break — downgraded from critical to low.

confirmed.title: opencti standalone deploy creates a Secret before its namespace exists
confirmed.evidence: The case branch `opencti) setup_helm_repos && deploy_opencti ;;` (line 237) does NOT run deploy_namespaces. deploy_opencti() then calls `kubectl create secret generic soc-opencti-secrets -n opencti ...` (line 170). If the cluster was torn down to `all`/namespaces-removed, the opencti namespace won't exist and the secret creation fails, aborting under `set -euo pipefail`. Same latent ordering issue applies to thehive/monitoring/ingress standalone paths that assume namespaces exist.
confirmed.recommendation: Prepend an idempotent namespace ensure to every non-`namespaces` branch — simplest is to call deploy_namespaces (already idempotent via `kubectl apply`) at the top of each component function, or run `kubectl create namespace <ns> --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -` inside deploy_opencti/thehive/monitoring/ingress. Alternatively document that `namespaces` must be deployed before any standalone component.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. The case branch at deploy-stack.sh:237 is `opencti) setup_helm_repos && deploy_opencti ;;` and does NOT call deploy_namespaces. deploy_opencti() (starts at line 155, secret created at 170-177) runs `kubectl create secret generic soc-opencti-secrets -n opencti ... | kubectl apply -f -`. namespaces.yaml (verified) creates the opencti namespace only via deploy_namespaces. Under `set -euo pipefail` (line 5), if the opencti namespace is absent the apply fails and aborts. Same latent issue for the thehive (236), monitoring (238), and ingress (239) standalone branches which assume their namespaces exist; shared writes into the `shared` namespace too. Note the finding cited the secret at 170 — correct — but the branch/function span is more precisely 155-177; left the file ref widened. Severity medium is fair: only bites the standalone-component workflow after a namespace teardown, not the default `all` path which runs deploy_namespaces first (line 225).

confirmed.title: k3s API server bound to 0.0.0.0 and exposed via host port-forward
confirmed.evidence: The provision script rewrites the kubeconfig server to 0.0.0.0 (`sed -i 's/127.0.0.1/0.0.0.0/g'`) and portForwards maps guestPort 6443 -> hostPort 6443. Lima host port-forwards bind on the macOS host; combined with a 0.0.0.0 server address this widens the API exposure beyond loopback. The Wazuh agent ports 1514/1515 and API 55000 are likewise forwarded to the host.
confirmed.recommendation: Set the user kubeconfig server back to 127.0.0.1 (the sed rewrite is unnecessary and misleading since Lima port-forwards to loopback). Explicitly set portForwards hostIP: 127.0.0.1 on 6443/55000 to make the loopback-only intent durable against any global Lima override. Only expose 1514/1515 if remote agent monitoring is actually required.
confirmed.verifyNote: Partially confirmed but the framing overstates LAN risk. CONFIRMED: line 106 (and minimal:78) runs sed -i 's/127.0.0.1/0.0.0.0/g' on the user kubeconfig, and portForwards map 6443->6443 (lines 36-38; minimal 24-26), plus 1514/1515/55000 are forwarded (lines 48-57). REFUTED NUANCE: the sed only rewrites the CLIENT kubeconfig's server URL (~/.kube/config), not the k3s server bind address; 0.0.0.0 as a client connect target resolves to loopback. More importantly, NO portForward sets hostIP (grep confirmed none present), and Lima's DEFAULT hostIP for port-forwards is 127.0.0.1 — so by default these ports bind to loopback on the Mac, NOT the LAN. The claim 'reachable from the LAN' is not substantiated by the config as written; exposure is to any local process/user on the Mac, which is a real but lesser concern. Downgrading high->medium. The 0.0.0.0 in the kubeconfig is confusing/sloppy but is not what binds the server to all interfaces.

confirmed.title: kubeconfig written world-readable (mode 644) inside VM
confirmed.evidence: `--write-kubeconfig-mode=644` makes /etc/rancher/k3s/k3s.yaml (cluster-admin credentials) readable by every user/process in the VM. Comment at k3s-soc.yaml:88 justifies it for non-root access, but any compromised container or local user can read full admin creds.
confirmed.recommendation: Drop --write-kubeconfig-mode=644 (let it default to 600). The provision script already copies the kubeconfig to the user's ~/.kube/config with correct ownership (lines 103-107 / minimal 76-79), so non-root access is preserved without world-readable admin creds at the source.
confirmed.verifyNote: CONFIRMED. k3s-soc.yaml:92 and k3s-soc-minimal.yaml:67 both pass --write-kubeconfig-mode=644, making /etc/rancher/k3s/k3s.yaml (cluster-admin creds) readable by every user/process in the VM. Comment at k3s-soc.yaml:88 justifies it for non-root access, and lines 103-107 (minimal 76-79) already copy the kubeconfig to the user's ~/.kube/config and chown it — so the 644 on the source is indeed redundant for the stated purpose. Severity medium is fair for a single-tenant homelab VM (real weakness, limited blast radius).

confirmed.title: Supply-chain: unpinned curl|sh for k3s and helm-from-main
confirmed.evidence: `curl -sfL https://get.k3s.io | INSTALL_K3S_EXEC=... sh -` installs whatever k3s version is current (no INSTALL_K3S_VERSION pin). `curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/helm/helm/main/scripts/get-helm-3 | bash` fetches the installer from the moving `main` branch and pipes to bash with no checksum. Both are remote-code-exec on first boot with no integrity check, and builds are non-reproducible.
confirmed.recommendation: Pin k3s via INSTALL_K3S_VERSION=v1.xx.x+k3s1. Replace the helm main-branch installer with a tagged release path and verify the SHA256, or install helm from a vendored/apt binary with a checksum check.
confirmed.verifyNote: CONFIRMED exactly as cited. k3s-soc.yaml:90 (minimal:65) pipes curl -sfL https://get.k3s.io | INSTALL_K3S_EXEC=... sh - with no INSTALL_K3S_VERSION pin. k3s-soc.yaml:100 (minimal:74) pipes curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/helm/helm/main/scripts/get-helm-3 | bash from the moving main branch with no checksum. Both are unpinned fetch-and-execute as root at first boot. Medium is appropriate.

confirmed.title: No Helm chart versions pinned — non-reproducible deploys
confirmed.evidence: Every `helm upgrade --install` uses a bare chart ref (e.g. `opensearch/opensearch`, `prometheus-community/kube-prometheus-stack`) with no `--version`. Re-running deploy at a later date pulls whatever is newest in each repo, which can break the slim resource profile and diverge from the pinned image versions CLAUDE.md claims (Wazuh 4.14.3, OpenCTI 6.9.5).
confirmed.recommendation: Add `--version <pinned>` to each of the 9 helm install commands and record pins in a versions.env sourced by the script (or a README compatibility matrix / Chart.lock). Ensure the pinned chart versions ship the image tags CLAUDE.md claims.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. Every `helm upgrade --install` uses a bare chart ref with no `--version`: opensearch/opensearch (87), bitnami/redis (92), bitnami/rabbitmq (97), minio/minio (102), strangebee/thehive (143), strangebee/cortex (148), opencti/opencti (184), prometheus-community/kube-prometheus-stack (197), traefik/traefik (208). All 9 cited lines verified exact. Re-running pulls whatever is newest, which can diverge from the slim resource profile and from the pinned image versions CLAUDE.md claims (Wazuh 4.14.3 at CLAUDE.md:8; note CLAUDE.md:28 also asserts 'All container images pinned (no :latest tags)' — chart pinning is a separate gap). The finding's '(OpenCTI 6.9.5)' detail is not in CLAUDE.md (I only found Wazuh 4.14.3), but the core claim holds. Medium is appropriate for a reproducibility/design issue.

confirmed.title: validate_secrets does not catch insecure default usernames or env-supplied OpenCTI creds
confirmed.evidence: validate_secrets() only greps secrets.yaml for the base64 placeholder `Q0hBTkdFX01F` (correctly covers all CHANGE_ME passwords). But minio-root-user defaults to base64 `minio-admin` and grafana admin-user to `admin` (secrets.yaml.example:32,88) which are not flagged. Separately, OpenCTI creds come from env vars (OPENCTI_ADMIN_TOKEN/PASSWORD) and bypass validate_secrets entirely; missing OTX/AbuseIPDB/VT keys silently fall back to the literal `placeholder` (lines 174-176).
confirmed.recommendation: Extend validate_secrets to reject known-default usernames (admin, minio-admin) and obviously-weak values; validate env-supplied OpenCTI token/password (min length, not a known default). For missing API keys, leave the secret key empty or fail rather than writing the literal `placeholder` (lines 174-176).
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed on all counts. validate_secrets() (50-65) only greps for `Q0hBTkdFX01F`; I verified this base64 prefix matches all 17 CHANGE_ME placeholder lines in secrets.yaml.example (it is the common base64 prefix of every CHANGE_ME_* value), so the parenthetical 'correctly covers all CHANGE_ME passwords' is accurate. The unflagged defaults are real: secrets.yaml.example:32 minio-root-user `bWluaW8tYWRtaW4=` decodes to 'minio-admin' and :88 admin-user `YWRtaW4=` decodes to 'admin' — neither matches the grep. OpenCTI creds come from env vars (deploy-stack.sh:159-160) and bypass validate_secrets entirely; only emptiness is checked (165-168), not strength/defaults. Missing OTX/AbuseIPDB/VT keys fall back to the literal string `placeholder` at lines 174-176. All claims verified. Medium fits: weak-default usernames plus writing a literal 'placeholder' secret value are real hardening gaps, though defaults are usernames not passwords and the env tokens are at least required-non-empty.

confirmed.title: Misplaced redirect: `2>/dev/null || true` applies to assignment, not command substitution
confirmed.evidence: Pattern `KUBECONFIG_PATH="$(limactl list ... )/..." 2>/dev/null || true`. The redirect and `|| true` attach to the variable assignment (which never fails and produces no stderr), not to the `limactl` command substitution. If limactl is missing/errs, its stderr still leaks and the guard is a no-op. In setup-ism.sh/test-flow.sh this is benign only because `set +e` is in effect; in teardown.sh (`set -euo pipefail`) a failing command substitution could still abort.
confirmed.recommendation: Move the guard inside the substitution: `KUBECONFIG_PATH="$( (limactl list k3s-soc --format '{{.Dir}}' 2>/dev/null || true) )/copied-from-guest/kubeconfig.yaml"`, or compute the dir in a separate guarded step before building the path. deploy-stack.sh:13 lacks any guard at all (worth aligning).
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed at all three exact lines. teardown.sh:14, setup-ism.sh:9, and test-flow.sh:13 all use `KUBECONFIG_PATH="$(limactl list k3s-soc --format '{{.Dir}}')/copied-from-guest/kubeconfig.yaml" 2>/dev/null || true`. The `2>/dev/null` and `|| true` bind to the assignment, not to the inner command substitution, so limactl stderr still leaks and the guard is ineffective. setup-ism.sh:5 and test-flow.sh:6 use `set +e` so a failing substitution is benign there; teardown.sh:7 uses `set -euo pipefail` — however a failing command substitution inside an assignment does NOT abort under set -e in bash when the assignment is also subject to `|| true` at statement level... actually the `|| true` makes the whole statement succeed regardless, so teardown won't abort, but the cited risk (stderr leak + no-op guard) is real. Low/cleanliness severity is correct; impact is cosmetic (stray stderr) since the subsequent `export KUBECONFIG="${KUBECONFIG_PATH:-}"` and the cluster-info check (teardown.sh:36) handle the empty case.

confirmed.title: No-op sed in kubeconfig fallback (127.0.0.1 -> 127.0.0.1)
confirmed.evidence: `sed -i '' 's/127\.0\.0\.1/127.0.0.1/g' /tmp/k3s-soc-kubeconfig.yaml` replaces 127.0.0.1 with itself — a no-op. The intent was likely to rewrite the in-VM 127.0.0.1 to a host-reachable address, but the fallback kubeconfig (copied via `limactl shell ... cat`) still points at 127.0.0.1, which only works because port 6443 is forwarded. The primary path (line 63) correctly rewrites 0.0.0.0 -> 127.0.0.1.
confirmed.recommendation: Remove the no-op sed at line 70 entirely (the default k3s.yaml already uses 127.0.0.1, which is host-reachable via the port-forward), or replace it with the same meaningful rewrite used on the primary path (`0.0.0.0 -> 127.0.0.1`) for consistency. Add a brief comment noting 127.0.0.1 is correct because port 6443 is forwarded from the Lima VM.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading scripts/setup-lima.sh:70: `sed -i '' 's/127\.0\.0\.1/127.0.0.1/g' /tmp/k3s-soc-kubeconfig.yaml` substitutes 127.0.0.1 with the identical string — a genuine no-op. Contrast with the primary path at line 63 which performs a meaningful rewrite `0.0.0.0 -> 127.0.0.1`. The fallback kubeconfig is copied from /etc/rancher/k3s/k3s.yaml (line 69), which by k3s default has server https://127.0.0.1:6443, so the pattern matches but yields no change. The fallback still works via Lima's port-forward of 6443. This is dead/ineffective code with zero behavioral impact — purely cosmetic. Downgrading severity from low to nit since it causes no functional problem; it only signals confused intent.

confirmed.title: ISM setup is orphaned — never called by deploy and swallows all failures
confirmed.evidence: setup-ism.sh runs `set +e` and pipes every os_api call through `python3 ... || echo FAIL`, so failures (auth error, OpenSearch down, JSON parse error) print FAIL but the script exits 0. It is referenced only in its own usage comment and opensearch.yaml:66, not invoked by deploy-stack.sh, so retention policies are silently never applied unless an operator remembers to run it manually. Index retention is a stated design goal.
confirmed.recommendation: Invoke setup-ism.sh from deploy_shared() (or a post-deploy hook) after the existing `kubectl wait ... -l app=opensearch` readiness gate at deploy-stack.sh:113. Add `set -euo pipefail` (or at minimum drop `set +e` and add explicit error handling) to setup-ism.sh and make each policy PUT exit non-zero on failure — e.g. check the parsed `_id` and `exit 1` when any policy returns FAIL — so a failed apply is visible in the deploy output rather than silently swallowed.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading the file. Line 5 is `set +e`; the script has no `set -e` and no explicit exit, so it always exits 0. Lines 41, 61, 81 each pipe the os_api PUT through `python3 -c "...print(d.get('_id','FAIL'))" 2>/dev/null || echo "FAIL"`, so an auth error / OpenSearch-down / JSON-parse failure just prints FAIL and the script still succeeds. Orphaned claim confirmed: grep across the repo shows setup-ism referenced only in CHANGELOG.md:49, k8s/shared/opensearch.yaml:66 (a comment: 'ISM policies applied via scripts/setup-ism.sh after OpenSearch starts'), and the script's own usage line (scripts/setup-ism.sh:4). deploy_shared() (scripts/deploy-stack.sh:83-115) deploys OpenSearch and waits for readiness but never invokes setup-ism.sh, and no case branch in deploy-stack.sh calls it. Retention/index lifecycle is a stated design goal (opensearch.yaml:70 enables plugins.index_state_management; CLAUDE.md lists index lifecycle). Note on the cited line range: 16-19 is the os_api helper definition, which is relevant context but the failure-swallowing per-policy `|| echo FAIL` lives at 41/61/81; corrected the file:line to point at the lines that actually demonstrate the swallow. Severity low is fair: this is a single-operator homelab SOC and the script is documented for manual post-deploy run, so policies are not necessarily applied but the impact is bounded and operator-recoverable.

confirmed.title: Unescaped JSON payload interpolated into single-quoted curl -d in pod shell
confirmed.evidence: os_api() builds `kubectl exec ... -- sh -c "curl ... -d '${3:-}'"`. The JSON payloads happen to contain no single quotes today so it works, but any future policy body containing a single quote would break the inner `sh -c` quoting. Also the OpenSearch admin password is referenced via `$OPENSEARCH_INITIAL_ADMIN_PASSWORD` expanded inside the pod (good — not on the host arg list), but the literal user `admin` is hardcoded.
confirmed.recommendation: Pass the JSON body over stdin to eliminate the inner-quote fragility: `printf '%s' "$3" | kubectl exec -i -n "$OS_NS" "$OS_POD" -- sh -c 'curl -sk -u admin:"$OPENSEARCH_INITIAL_ADMIN_PASSWORD" -X'"$1"' "https://localhost:9200'"$2"'" -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d @-'`, keeping the credential expansion inside the pod. Optionally make the OpenSearch username configurable via an env var instead of hardcoding `admin`.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading lines 16-19. os_api() runs `kubectl exec -n "$OS_NS" "$OS_POD" -- sh -c "curl -sk -u admin:\$OPENSEARCH_INITIAL_ADMIN_PASSWORD -X${1} \"https://localhost:9200${2}\" -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '${3:-}'"`. The JSON body `$3` is interpolated inside single quotes within the inner `sh -c` string, so any payload containing a literal single quote would terminate the quoting and break the command. Verified the three current payloads (lines 25-41, 45-61, 65-81) contain only double quotes — no single quotes — so it works today; this is latent fragility, not a live break. Password handling confirmed correct: `\$OPENSEARCH_INITIAL_ADMIN_PASSWORD` is escaped so it expands inside the pod, not on the host arg list (no secret value read or printed). The literal user `admin` is indeed hardcoded in `-u admin:`. This is a code-cleanliness/robustness nit with no current functional or security impact; downgraded from low to nit accordingly.

confirmed.title: secrets.yaml path was tracked in early history (placeholders only) before being gitignored
confirmed.file: k8s/shared/secrets.yaml (git history: f6ba308 initial commit + stash a57a12f/a8773a5; removed/gitignored by 492dc7d)
confirmed.evidence: `git rev-list --all` shows k8s/shared/secrets.yaml present in commits f6ba308, a8773a5, 4afddcb, 1a92cc9 and the index/stash. Inspected without printing values: every tracked copy still contains the `Q0hBTkdFX01F` (CHANGE_ME) placeholders, so no real credentials were committed. The current working-tree secrets.yaml with real values is correctly gitignored (.gitignore:36) and untracked. Risk is the established pattern of a real-credential filename living in history.
confirmed.recommendation: No real-credential version was ever committed (verified: only placeholders + benign usernames in every tracked copy), so this is low risk and not an active leak. Keep only secrets.yaml.example tracked (already the case). Optional hardening: run `git filter-repo`/BFG to purge the historical secrets.yaml so the filename pattern doesn't cause future confusion, and add a gitleaks pre-commit + CI hook to block any real-value commit going forward. Since the repo appears unpushed/private, history rewrite is low-cost here.
confirmed.verifyNote: Independently confirmed by reading git history. `git log --all -- k8s/shared/secrets.yaml` returns f6ba308 (initial release) and the stash commits; `git ls-tree -r f6ba308` lists the file. Inspected content without printing values: the f6ba308 copy has 19 CHANGE_ME/Q0hBTkdFX01F placeholder lines. The ONLY non-placeholder data values decode (base64) to the non-secret usernames 'minio-admin' (minio-root-user) and 'admin' (admin-user) — no real credentials. All other fields are placeholders, comments, or YAML structure. At the latest commit 492dc7d the file is gone (0 matches), confirming removal. Current working-tree file EXISTS with real values but is correctly untracked: `git ls-files --error-unmatch` errors ('did not match any file'), and `git check-ignore -v` resolves to .gitignore:36 'k8s/shared/secrets.yaml'. Only secrets.yaml.example is tracked. Minor correction to the evidence: commits 4afddcb and 1a92cc9 are parents of the stash commit a57a12f (not independent commits that introduced the file), but the substantive claim — tracked in early history with placeholders only — holds.

confirmed.title: Helm repo URLs diverge between setup-lima.sh and deploy-stack.sh
confirmed.evidence: The two scripts add the same repo names from different URLs: strangebee is `charts.thehive-project.org` in deploy-stack.sh:41 but `strangebee.github.io/helm-charts/` in setup-lima.sh:92; opencti is `charts.opencti.io/` vs `devops-ia.github.io/helm-opencti/`; traefik is `helm.traefik.io/traefik` vs `traefik.github.io/charts`; prometheus-community trailing-slash differs. Whichever ran last wins, so chart resolution depends on invocation order and the duplicated logic can drift further.
confirmed.recommendation: Extract one shared setup_helm_repos definition (a sourced helper, or have deploy-stack.sh source setup-lima's repo block) so both paths use identical verified URLs. Adopt the setup-lima.sh URLs (traefik.github.io/charts, strangebee.github.io/helm-charts, devops-ia.github.io/helm-opencti) as canonical and drop the stale ones in deploy-stack.sh:41-44.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed every cited divergence by reading both blocks. strangebee: deploy-stack.sh:41 `https://charts.thehive-project.org` vs setup-lima.sh:92 `https://strangebee.github.io/helm-charts/`. opencti: deploy-stack.sh:42 `https://charts.opencti.io/` vs setup-lima.sh:93 `https://devops-ia.github.io/helm-opencti/`. traefik: deploy-stack.sh:44 `https://helm.traefik.io/traefik` vs setup-lima.sh:95 `https://traefik.github.io/charts`. prometheus-community trailing slash differs (deploy-stack.sh:43 no slash vs setup-lima.sh:94 with slash). opensearch/bitnami/minio match. Because `helm repo add` overwrites by name, whichever ran last wins, so chart resolution depends on invocation order. Low/design severity is correct. (Side note for fixers: the setup-lima.sh URLs — strangebee.github.io, devops-ia helm-opencti, traefik.github.io/charts — are the current canonical ones; deploy-stack.sh's charts.thehive-project.org / charts.opencti.io / helm.traefik.io are stale, so order-dependence can actually cause hard failures.)

confirmed.title: wait-for-ready failures masked with `|| true`, post-deploy health check non-blocking
confirmed.evidence: deploy_shared() does `kubectl wait --for=condition=ready pod -l app=opensearch ... || true`, so a never-ready OpenSearch does not stop subsequent dependent deploys (Wazuh/OpenCTI/TheHive all need OpenSearch). The final health check (252) only WARNs on non-Running pods and the script still exits 0, so `deploy-stack.sh` reports success even when the stack is unhealthy.
confirmed.recommendation: Treat the OpenSearch readiness-wait failure as fatal (drop the `|| true` at line 113, or branch to an explicit error), and make the post-deploy health check exit non-zero when UNHEALTHY > 0 so CI/automation can detect failed deploys.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. deploy_shared() at line 113 does `kubectl wait --for=condition=ready pod -l app=opensearch -n shared --timeout=300s || true`, so a never-ready OpenSearch does not stop the dependent deploys that follow in the `all` path (deploy_wazuh/opencti/thehive at 227-229, all of which need OpenSearch). The post-deploy health check (252) computes UNHEALTHY but only prints a WARNING (254) and never sets a non-zero exit; the script falls through to the final echoes and exits 0 even when pods are not Running. Both cited line refs accurate. Finding self-rated nit; bumping to low because masking a critical-dependency readiness failure plus exiting 0 on an unhealthy stack defeats CI/automation detection — slightly more than cosmetic. Still minor for a manual homelab workflow.

confirmed.title: Provision script duplicated across two Lima defs with no shared source
confirmed.evidence: k3s-soc-minimal.yaml duplicates ~90% of the full provision script (swap, sysctl, limits, k3s install, helm install, kubeconfig export) with only resource numbers and eviction thresholds changed. Any fix (e.g. version pinning, mode 600) must be applied twice and will drift.
confirmed.recommendation: Extract the common provisioning into a shared external script referenced via Lima provision[].file, or template both YAMLs from one source with the few differing variables, so fixes apply once.
confirmed.verifyNote: CONFIRMED. The two provision scripts (k3s-soc.yaml:61-117 and k3s-soc-minimal.yaml:43-85) are near-identical: swap setup, vm.max_map_count sysctl, nofile limits, k3s install, helm install, kubeconfig export/sed/chown, and the readiness wait loop are all duplicated, differing only in resource numbers (4G vs 2G swap; system-reserved 200m/256Mi vs 100m/200Mi) and eviction thresholds. Any security fix (version pinning, mode 600, hostIP) must be applied in both places and will drift. nit severity is correct.

confirmed.title: Dangling DNS comment with no corresponding provisioning
confirmed.evidence: Trailing comment: `# DNS — resolve soc.homelab.local inside the VM (configured in /etc/hosts via provision script)`. The provision script (lines 61-117) never edits /etc/hosts, so soc.homelab.local is not resolvable inside the VM as the comment claims. The README also tells the user to add a host-side /etc/hosts entry with an unspecified `<lima-vm-ip>`.
confirmed.recommendation: Remove the misleading comment (or actually add the /etc/hosts line in the provision script if in-VM resolution is wanted). Fix README.md:44 to use the concrete '127.0.0.1 soc.homelab.local' (matching the other docs) instead of the undefined '<lima-vm-ip>' placeholder, since ingress is port-forwarded to localhost.
confirmed.verifyNote: CONFIRMED. Line 124 is the trailing comment: '# DNS — resolve soc.homelab.local inside the VM (configured in /etc/hosts via provision script)'. The provision script (lines 61-117) never edits /etc/hosts (grep for /etc/hosts in the provision block returns nothing), so soc.homelab.local is NOT made resolvable inside the VM as the comment claims. README.md:42-44 tells the user to add a host-side /etc/hosts entry using a vague '<lima-vm-ip>' placeholder; meanwhile docs/network-diagram.md:109 and docs/runbooks/initial-setup.md:62 correctly document '127.0.0.1 soc.homelab.local'. The comment is misleading and dead. nit is correct.

confirmed.title: Initial-setup runbook omits required opensearch-certs Secret generation; deploy will fail
confirmed.evidence: k8s/shared/opensearch.yaml:47-49 mounts `secretMounts: opensearch-certs` (esnode.pem/esnode-key.pem/root-ca.pem) and sets plugins.security.allow_unsafe_democertificates:false (opensearch.yaml:79). Nothing in scripts/, README, or the setup runbook generates or applies a `opensearch-certs` Secret — `grep -rn 'opensearch-certs|esnode|root-ca|securityadmin' scripts/ docs/ README.md CLAUDE.md` returns empty. Following the runbook (Step 5 `./scripts/deploy-stack.sh all`) deploys OpenSearch with a missing secret mount, so the OpenSearch pod never becomes Ready and the whole stack stalls.
confirmed.recommendation: Add a documented step before Step 5 (and ideally a scripts/generate-opensearch-certs.sh) that generates the OpenSearch TLS PEM materials (root-ca.pem, esnode.pem/esnode-key.pem with the SANs/DNs the config expects: CN=opensearch-node,O=MatchBox,C=US and admin CN=admin,O=MatchBox,C=US) and runs `kubectl create secret generic opensearch-certs -n shared --from-file=root-ca.pem=... --from-file=esnode.pem=... --from-file=esnode-key.pem=...`. The same secret must also be present for the wazuh namespace (k8s/wazuh/manager/deployment.yaml mounts it). Then document populating the opensearch-ca-cert ConfigMap (k8s/shared/opensearch-ca-cert.yaml currently a REPLACE_WITH placeholder) for the opencti and wazuh namespaces. Consider wiring secret generation into deploy_shared() so deploy-stack.sh all works end-to-end.
confirmed.verifyNote: Independently confirmed. k8s/shared/opensearch.yaml:47-49 mounts secretMounts -> name/secretName: opensearch-certs at path /usr/share/opensearch/config/certs, and the embedded opensearch.yml references certs/esnode.pem, certs/esnode-key.pem, certs/root-ca.pem with plugins.security.allow_unsafe_democertificates:false (line 79). The cited grep over scripts/ docs/ README.md CLAUDE.md returns empty (exit 1) — verified. A whole-tree search shows 'opensearch-certs' appears ONLY as a consumer (k8s/shared/opensearch.yaml and k8s/wazuh/manager/deployment.yaml:135-137), never created by any script, Certificate/Issuer, or Helm value. The cert-manager Certificate in k8s/ingress/self-signed-issuer.yaml produces secretName 'soc-tls-cert' (ingress TLS for soc.homelab.local), NOT opensearch-certs; opensearch.yaml has no chart-side cert auto-gen keys. Runbook line 44 is indeed 'Step 5: Deploy the Full Stack' running ./scripts/deploy-stack.sh all; deploy_shared() runs 'helm upgrade --install opensearch ... --wait --timeout 5m', which will hang on the missing secret mount and fail. The opensearch-ca-cert.yaml ConfigMap also contains the REPLACE_WITH_YOUR_OPENSEARCH_CA_CERTIFICATE placeholder, confirmed. Note: impact is broader than the finding states — Wazuh Manager (k8s/wazuh/manager/deployment.yaml:135-137) also mounts the same missing secret, so it fails too. Severity high is correct (hard, deploy-blocking failure).

confirmed.title: cert-manager / TLS cert never created; referenced generate-tls-cert.sh does not exist
confirmed.evidence: The issuer header instructs `./scripts/generate-tls-cert.sh` as the manual path, but no such script exists (scripts/ has only deploy-stack.sh, setup-ism.sh, setup-lima.sh, teardown.sh, test-flow.sh). deploy-stack.sh never installs cert-manager, applies self-signed-issuer.yaml, nor creates the `soc-tls-cert` Secret (`grep -in 'cert-manager|ClusterIssuer|tls' scripts/deploy-stack.sh` finds none). Yet CLAUDE.md Security states 'cert-manager ClusterIssuer for automated cert generation' and traefik-routes reference soc-tls-cert. Result: ingress TLS termination has no certificate after a documented deploy.
confirmed.recommendation: Pick one and wire it into deploy_ingress() in scripts/deploy-stack.sh so a documented deploy yields a working cert: (A) add the cert-manager install (helm install cert-manager jetstack/cert-manager --set installCRDs=true) plus `kubectl apply -f k8s/ingress/self-signed-issuer.yaml` and wait for the Certificate to become Ready before applying traefik-routes.yaml; or (B) actually ship the referenced scripts/generate-tls-cert.sh (it can run the openssl + `kubectl create secret tls soc-tls-cert -n kube-system` commands already documented in the YAML header) and invoke it from deploy_ingress(). Then update CLAUDE.md/README so the cert step is explicit, and fix the self-signed-issuer.yaml header to stop pointing at a script that does not exist.
confirmed.verifyNote: Independently confirmed all claims. (1) self-signed-issuer.yaml lines 1-8 instruct './scripts/generate-tls-cert.sh', but `find` returns no such file; scripts/ contains only deploy-stack.sh, setup-ism.sh, setup-lima.sh, teardown.sh, test-flow.sh. (2) grep for 'cert-manager|ClusterIssuer|tls|self-signed|soc-tls-cert|generate-tls' in deploy-stack.sh returns nothing (exit 1); deploy_ingress() (lines 204-219) only does `helm upgrade --install traefik` and `kubectl apply -f traefik-routes.yaml` -- it never installs cert-manager, applies self-signed-issuer.yaml, nor creates the soc-tls-cert Secret. (3) CLAUDE.md line 25 states 'cert-manager ClusterIssuer for automated cert generation (k8s/ingress/self-signed-issuer.yaml)'. (4) traefik-routes.yaml references secretName: soc-tls-cert in the TLSStore default (line 48) and on every IngressRoute (lines 84, 108, 130, 152, 174). Net: after the documented `./scripts/deploy-stack.sh`, IngressRoutes reference a soc-tls-cert Secret that is never created, so ingress TLS termination has no certificate. Severity kept at high: the documented happy path breaks ingress TLS, and the named manual script genuinely does not exist. Note this is a homelab, not internet-facing, and the YAML does provide manual fallbacks (inline openssl + Option B commented Secret), but neither is wired into the deploy automation. No secret values were printed.

confirmed.title: OpenCTI version inconsistent: docs say 6.9.5, manifest says 6.6.6, helm deploy pins neither
confirmed.evidence: README.md:11, CLAUDE.md (Architecture), CHANGELOG.md:77, and docs/architecture.md all state OpenCTI 6.9.5. The standalone manifest k8s/opencti/deployment.yaml:45,189 pins `opencti/platform:6.6.6` and `opencti/worker:6.6.6`. The actual deploy path (deploy-stack.sh deploy_opencti) uses `helm upgrade --install opencti opencti/opencti -f k8s/opencti/helm-values.yaml`, and helm-values.yaml has no image tag, so it pulls the chart default version — neither 6.9.5 nor 6.6.6.
confirmed.recommendation: Pick one OpenCTI version and pin it in helm-values.yaml (e.g. opencti.image.tag and worker.image.tag), then make README.md:11 and CHANGELOG.md:77 match it. Align the connector image tags (currently 6.9.5) with the platform version. Reconcile the orphan k8s/opencti/deployment.yaml (pins 6.6.6 but is never applied by deploy-stack.sh) — remove it or wire it into the deploy path. Drop the inaccurate claims that CLAUDE.md/architecture.md state a version; they currently state none.
confirmed.verifyNote: Core inconsistency CONFIRMED, but two cited sources are wrong. CONFIRMED: README.md:11 and CHANGELOG.md:77 state 'OpenCTI 6.9.5'. k8s/opencti/deployment.yaml:45 pins opencti/platform:6.6.6 and :189 pins opencti/worker:6.6.6. deploy-stack.sh deploy_opencti (lines 184-185) runs `helm upgrade --install opencti opencti/opencti -f helm-values.yaml`, and helm-values.yaml contains NO image tag/version (grep for tag/6. = empty), so the chart default version is pulled — neither 6.9.5 nor 6.6.6. REFUTED parts of evidence: CLAUDE.md does NOT state any OpenCTI version (grep for '6.9.5'/'6.[0-9]' in CLAUDE.md returned nothing) and docs/architecture.md does NOT state any OpenCTI version either — the evidence's claim that 'CLAUDE.md (Architecture)' and 'docs/architecture.md all state OpenCTI 6.9.5' is false. ADDITIONAL: the 4 connector manifests (k8s/opencti/connectors/{abuseipdb,mitre-attack,virustotal,alienvault-otx}.yaml) pin 6.9.5, so connectors and the orphan platform/worker manifest are mismatched (6.9.5 vs 6.6.6). Severity medium upheld: a real version-skew that affects reproducibility and could break connector/platform compatibility.

confirmed.title: NetworkPolicy counts in docs are wrong (claim 18 / 23, actual 31)
confirmed.evidence: CLAUDE.md Security says 'default-deny ingress + 18 explicit allow rules across 5 namespaces'. CHANGELOG 1.2.0 (line 92) says '23 rules across 5 namespaces'. CHANGELOG 1.4.0 (line 15) says egress added '10 new policies'. Actual k8s/network-policies.yaml: `grep -c 'kind: NetworkPolicy'`=31, comprising 21 allow-* + 5 default-deny-ingress + 5 default-deny-egress. So allow rules=21 (not 18), egress policies=5 deny + ~5 allow (not 10 'new policies'), total=31 (not 23).
confirmed.recommendation: State the real numbers consistently: 31 total NetworkPolicies = 5 default-deny-ingress + 5 default-deny-egress + 21 explicit allow. Fix CLAUDE.md:26 (18 -> 21 allow rules), CHANGELOG.md:92 (23 -> 31 rules). .github/SECURITY.md does not cite a count (only says 'default-deny ingress'), so no change needed there. Consider not hard-coding counts that drift, or add a comment in network-policies.yaml as the single source of truth.
confirmed.verifyNote: CONFIRMED by direct count. k8s/network-policies.yaml has exactly 31 `kind: NetworkPolicy` objects. By metadata.name: 5x default-deny-ingress (one per namespace), 5x default-deny-egress (one per namespace), and 21 allow-* policies (allow-opensearch, allow-redis, allow-rabbitmq, allow-minio, allow-nfs, allow-wazuh-manager, allow-wazuh-dashboard, allow-thehive, allow-cortex, allow-opencti, allow-grafana, allow-prometheus, allow-alertmanager, allow-prometheus-scrape x3, allow-egress-shared, allow-egress-wazuh, allow-egress-thehive, allow-egress-opencti, allow-egress-monitoring). 5+5+21=31. Doc claims CONFIRMED wrong: CLAUDE.md:26 says 'default-deny ingress + 18 explicit allow rules' (actual allow = 21), CHANGELOG.md:92 says '23 rules across 5 namespaces' (actual 31), CHANGELOG.md:15 says egress '10 new policies' (actual egress = 5 deny + 5 allow = 10 egress-related policies, so '10 new policies' is arguably defensible for the 1.4.0 egress addition, but the aggregate totals 18/23 are wrong). Severity lowered from medium to low: this is purely cosmetic documentation drift in counts with no functional/security impact.

confirmed.title: mcp-servers.md documents a run-sca-scan tool and /sca endpoint that do not exist
confirmed.evidence: docs/mcp-servers.md:35 lists `run-sca-scan` (Trigger Security Configuration Assessment) and line 44 lists `PUT /sca/{agent_id}`. The actual wazuh-mcp server registers 7 tools (list-alerts, get-alert, search-agents, get-agent-info, get-vulnerabilities, get-rules, get-decoders) — `grep -c 'server.tool(' mcp-servers/wazuh-mcp/src/index.ts`=7 and `grep -c 'sca' index.ts`=0. The doc table thus advertises 8 wazuh tools incl. a non-existent one.
confirmed.recommendation: In docs/mcp-servers.md remove the `run-sca-scan` row from the wazuh-mcp Tools table (line 35) and remove the `PUT /sca/{agent_id} -> trigger SCA scan` line from API Endpoints Used (line 44), reconciling the doc to the real 7 tools. Alternatively, if SCA triggering is desired, implement the tool in wazuh-mcp/src/index.ts and keep the doc.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading both files. docs/mcp-servers.md line 35 lists `run-sca-scan` (Trigger Security Configuration Assessment, param agent_id) and line 44 lists `PUT /sca/{agent_id}` -> trigger SCA scan. mcp-servers/wazuh-mcp/src/index.ts registers exactly 7 tools via server.tool(): list-alerts, get-alert, search-agents, get-agent-info, get-vulnerabilities, get-rules, get-decoders. grep -c 'server.tool(' = 7 and grep -ic 'sca' = 0 (no SCA tool, no /sca endpoint anywhere in the source). The file's own header comment (lines 5-12) also lists only those 7 tools. So the doc table advertises 8 wazuh tools including one (run-sca-scan) and one endpoint (PUT /sca) that are not implemented. Severity medium is appropriate: it is a documentation-only defect (no runtime impact) but it misleads users/Claude into calling a tool that will not exist.

confirmed.title: mcp-servers.md describes a multi-file package layout and SDK ^1.0.0 that don't match reality
confirmed.evidence: docs/mcp-servers.md:122-152 shows each server as `src/index.ts + client.ts + tools/ + types.ts` and dependency `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk: ^1.0.0`. Actual: each server is a single `src/index.ts` (`ls mcp-servers/wazuh-mcp/src/` -> index.ts only), no shared `tools/`/`client.ts`/`types.ts`, and package.json pins `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk: ^1.9.0` (all 3). The doc also omits the `@matchbox/mcp-shared` package introduced in CHANGELOG 1.4.0.
confirmed.recommendation: Update the Package Structure section (docs/mcp-servers.md ~122-137) to show the single-file src/index.ts layout, bump the Dependencies block (line 143) to @modelcontextprotocol/sdk ^1.9.0, and add the @matchbox/mcp-shared (file:../shared) dependency plus a note that shared utilities live in mcp-servers/shared/ per CHANGELOG 1.4.0.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed all three sub-claims. (1) Package Structure (docs lines 122-137) shows each server as src/index.ts + client.ts + tools/ (one file per tool) + types.ts; actual layout is a single src/index.ts only for all three servers (ls of wazuh-mcp/src, thehive-mcp/src, opencti-mcp/src each returns just index.ts; no client.ts, tools/, or types.ts). (2) Dependencies block (docs line 143) pins @modelcontextprotocol/sdk ^1.0.0; all three package.json files pin ^1.9.0. (3) The doc omits @matchbox/mcp-shared (mcp-servers/shared/, package name @matchbox/mcp-shared, file:../shared dependency in all three package.json), which CHANGELOG 1.4.0 line 17 documents as extracting fetchWithRetry/formatResponse/etc. into the shared package; wazuh-mcp/src/index.ts line 21 imports from @matchbox/mcp-shared. Severity low is correct: stale documentation with no functional/security impact.

confirmed.title: network-diagram.md states Wazuh Dashboard on port 443; actual service/route is 5601
confirmed.evidence: docs/network-diagram.md:13 routes `/wazuh --> wazuh-dashboard:443` and the mermaid graph (line 26-27) labels `Wazuh Dashboard :443`. Actual: k8s/wazuh/dashboard/deployment.yaml exposes port/targetPort/containerPort 5601, and k8s/ingress/traefik-routes.yaml:77-78 routes to `wazuh-dashboard port 5601`. CLAUDE.md Key Ports table correctly says 5601, so the docs disagree internally.
confirmed.recommendation: In docs/network-diagram.md, change line 13 port map from `wazuh-dashboard:443` to `wazuh-dashboard:5601`, and change the mermaid label on line 27 from `WD[Wazuh Dashboard :443]` to `WD[Wazuh Dashboard :5601]`.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading all cited files. docs/network-diagram.md:13 routes `/wazuh --> wazuh-dashboard:443` and line 27 labels `WD[Wazuh Dashboard :443]` in the mermaid graph. Actual port is 5601: k8s/wazuh/dashboard/deployment.yaml Service port/targetPort 5601 (lines 15-16) and containerPort 5601 (line 64); k8s/ingress/traefik-routes.yaml routes to wazuh-dashboard port 5601 scheme https (lines 77-79). CLAUDE.md Key Ports table correctly lists 5601, so network-diagram.md is internally inconsistent with both the manifests and the canonical docs. Minor evidence nit: the `:443` mermaid label is on line 27 only (the evidence said 'line 26-27'); line 26 is an unrelated edge. Substance of the finding is fully accurate.

confirmed.title: README dashboard env setup points to wrong directory and root .env.example is incomplete
confirmed.evidence: README.md:92-101 says `cp .env.example .env` (repo root) then `cd dashboard && npm run dev`. The dashboard reads config via SvelteKit `$env/dynamic/private` (dashboard/src/routes/api/wazuh/+server.ts:3), which loads `.env` from the dashboard CWD (dashboard/.env), not the repo root — so the root .env is ignored. Additionally the root .env.example lacks variables the dashboard needs (GRAFANA_URL, CORTEX_URL, WAZUH_DASHBOARD_URL, NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED) which only exist in dashboard/.env.example.
confirmed.recommendation: Fix README step 1 to `cp dashboard/.env.example dashboard/.env` and edit it there, so the env file lives in the SvelteKit CWD (dashboard/). Clarify in the README that the repo-root .env.example is for the MCP servers only, and that the dashboard uses dashboard/.env.example which additionally defines GRAFANA_URL, CORTEX_URL, WAZUH_DASHBOARD_URL, and NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading the files. README.md step 1 (line 93) says `cp .env.example .env` at repo root, then step 3 (line 100) does `cd dashboard` before `npm run dev`. The dashboard reads config via SvelteKit `$env/dynamic/private` (verified in dashboard/src/routes/api/wazuh/+server.ts:3, plus cortex/grafana/health/opencti/thehive +server.ts). With the dev server run from inside dashboard/ (package.json dev script is `vite dev`, CWD = dashboard/), SvelteKit loads dashboard/.env, NOT the repo-root .env — there is no custom envDir in svelte.config.js or vite.config.ts to override this. So the root .env created in step 1 is ignored. Also confirmed the root .env.example (24 lines) lacks GRAFANA_URL, CORTEX_URL, WAZUH_DASHBOARD_URL, and NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED; all four exist only in dashboard/.env.example (lines 6,11,22,26) and are actually consumed by the dashboard code (grafana/+server.ts:5, cortex/+server.ts:5, health/+server.ts:6-10). One nuance: NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED is also hardcoded in vite.config.ts:5 so it works in dev regardless, but the other three vars genuinely need the dashboard/.env file. Net: README setup instructions are broken for a new user — medium severity stands. The `cp` line is precisely 93 (finding cited the block start at 92).

confirmed.title: SECURITY.md 'Supported Versions' is stale (1.3.x) while project is at 1.4.0
confirmed.evidence: .github/SECURITY.md supported-versions table lists `1.3.x = Yes, < 1.3 = No`, but CHANGELOG.md top entry is [1.4.0] 2026-02-27. The supported matrix omits the current release.
confirmed.recommendation: Update the supported-versions table to reflect the current release (the latest committed version is 1.5.0 per git, while CHANGELOG.md tops out at 1.4.0 — reconcile these first, then set the supported row accordingly). Add a process note to bump SECURITY.md and CHANGELOG on each release. Replace 'Email security concerns to the maintainer directly' (SECURITY.md:15) with a concrete contact (email or GitHub Security Advisories link).
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading .github/SECURITY.md. Line 5-8 supported-versions table lists '1.3.x = Yes' and '< 1.3 = No'. The project has moved past 1.3: CHANGELOG.md:6 top entry is [1.4.0] dated 2026-02-27, and git log shows an even later 'v1.5.0' commit (d36448e) plus v1.4.0 commits. So the matrix omits the current release(s). One correction to the finding: the actual latest is v1.5.0 per git history, not 1.4.0 as the finding states (CHANGELOG itself is also stale and tops out at 1.4.0), but this does not change the verdict. Also confirmed line 15 says 'Email security concerns to the maintainer directly' with no email address or handle provided. Severity low is appropriate: this is a private/unpublished homelab repo, not a distributed package, so the disclosure-process weakness is real but low-impact.

confirmed.title: integrations.md OpenCTI<->TheHive 'Configuration' block is mislabeled (shows Cortex config)
confirmed.evidence: Section 3 (OpenCTI <--> TheHive) line 109 reads 'TheHive needs OpenCTI API URL and token in its application.conf:' but the fenced block (lines 110-120) is a `cortex { servers = [...] url=...cortex...:9001 ... CORTEX_API_KEY }` config — a Cortex connector, not OpenCTI. Copy-paste error; no OpenCTI URL/token example is actually shown.
confirmed.recommendation: Replace the fenced block (lines 110-120) with the actual OpenCTI integration config TheHive uses (the OpenCTI GraphQL API URL and an API token reference), matching the 'TheHive --> OpenCTI (API Query)' flow described above. Alternatively, if the Cortex block was intentional, relabel the section as Cortex configuration. As written, the prose and the code contradict each other.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading docs/integrations.md lines 95-120. Section 3 is 'OpenCTI <--> TheHive (Bidirectional Intel)'. Line 108 is '### Configuration', line 109 reads 'TheHive needs OpenCTI API URL and token in its application.conf:'. The fenced block at lines 110-120 contains a `cortex { servers = [ { name = "Cortex"; url = "http://cortex.thehive.svc.cluster.local:9001"; auth { type = "bearer"; key = "CORTEX_API_KEY" } } ] }` config. That is a Cortex connector config, not OpenCTI -- no OpenCTI URL or token appears anywhere in the block. Genuine copy-paste / mislabeling error. Severity 'low' is appropriate (doc cleanliness, misleading config example, no security impact since no secret values are exposed -- CORTEX_API_KEY is a placeholder).

confirmed.title: adding-agents.md installs Wazuh agent 4.9.2 against a 4.14.3 manager
confirmed.evidence: adding-agents.md:41-42 and :52 install `wazuh-agent-4.9.2-1` (macOS pkg and Windows msi). The deployed manager/dashboard are pinned to 4.14.3 (k8s/wazuh/manager/deployment.yaml:51, dashboard/deployment.yaml:42). Wazuh agents should not exceed and generally should match the manager minor; 4.9.2 is a stale, mismatched version vs the documented 4.14.3 stack.
confirmed.recommendation: Bump the macOS pkg (lines 41-42) and Windows msi (line 52) download URLs to wazuh-agent-4.14.3-1 so external agents match the pinned 4.14.3 stack (manager/dashboard/DaemonSet). Alternatively, replace the hardcoded version with a documented version-pin variable plus a short note on the supported agent-vs-manager version policy to prevent future drift.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading the files. adding-agents.md installs wazuh-agent-4.9.2-1: line 41 (curl macOS .arm64.pkg), line 42 (installer -pkg), and line 52 (Windows .msi download). The deployed stack is uniformly pinned to 4.14.3 -- verified k8s/wazuh/manager/deployment.yaml:51 & :66 (wazuh-manager:4.14.3), k8s/wazuh/dashboard/deployment.yaml:42 & :60 (wazuh-dashboard:4.14.3), and notably the in-cluster DaemonSet agent itself at k8s/wazuh/agents/daemonset.yaml:27 (wazuh-agent:4.14.3). CLAUDE.md also documents 'Wazuh 4.14.3' and 'All container images pinned'. So the runbook tells operators to install a stale 4.9.2 external agent that mismatches both the manager and the cluster's own 4.14.3 agents. This is a real documentation staleness/cleanliness issue. Severity 'low' is correct: a 4.9.2 agent would still connect to a 4.14.3 manager (Wazuh supports older agents against newer managers), so it is doc drift, not a runtime break. The cited line:41 anchor is accurate (first install reference).

confirmed.title: README Resource Profile RAM figures contradict resource-requirements.md
confirmed.evidence: README.md:62-66 'Resource Profile' table claims Steady state ~7.1GB, Investigation ~10.5GB, Full stack ~10.5GB. docs/resource-requirements.md gives Steady ~3.2GB (line 52), Investigation ~5.8GB (line 65), Full Load ~7.2GB (line 78). The two documents disagree by 3-4GB for the same modes; the README figures also push to ~10.5GB which would exceed comfortable headroom on the 10GB VM.
confirmed.recommendation: Reconcile the two documents so a single authoritative RAM budget exists. Either update README's Resource Profile rows to match resource-requirements.md (~3.2GB / ~5.8GB / ~7.2GB) or vice-versa, and ensure no mode is shown exceeding the 10GB VM allocation. CLAUDE.md's figures should be aligned to the same source.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading both files. README.md Resource Profile table (lines 64-66) states Steady state ~7.1GB, Investigation mode ~10.5GB, Full stack ~10.5GB. docs/resource-requirements.md gives Steady State ~3.2GB (line 52), Investigation Mode ~5.8GB (line 65), Full Load ~7.2GB (line 78). The two linked documents disagree by ~3.9GB / ~4.7GB / ~3.3GB respectively for the same operating modes. The README's ~10.5GB figures also exceed the 10GB Lima VM allocation stated on README:60 and resource-requirements.md:10. Note the README's 7.1GB/10.5GB numbers also appear in CLAUDE.md (so they are deliberate, not a typo), but they still contradict the resource-requirements.md doc the README explicitly links as the authoritative 'Resource Requirements'. Documentation inconsistency only, no functional impact — low severity is correct. Cited the Resource Profile table data rows starting at line 64 (header table starts at 62).

confirmed.title: CLAUDE.md Dashboard Integration section is entirely stale (wrong path, port, and references a non-existent layout)
confirmed.evidence: CLAUDE.md 'Dashboard Integration' says SOC is integrated at `http://localhost:3099`, 'Standalone version: public/index.html', and 'Integrated source: ../ServiceMonitor/public/index.html'. Reality: the dashboard is a SvelteKit app in dashboard/ served on port 5173 (dashboard/package.json: `vite dev --port 5173`); there is no public/index.html in the repo (`find -name index.html` finds none under the project), and the only HTML is k8s/dashboard/soc-command-center.html. CLAUDE.md '4 tabs: Overview, Architecture, Components, MCP Servers' describes the old static HTML, not the current SvelteKit per-service pages.
confirmed.recommendation: Rewrite CLAUDE.md 'Dashboard Integration' (around line 55) to describe the SvelteKit dashboard: directory dashboard/, dev port 5173 (vite dev --port 5173), 5 per-service pages (wazuh, grafana, opencti, thehive, cortex) plus overview, Solarized light/dark theme, embedded Grafana. Remove the localhost:3099, public/index.html, and ../ServiceMonitor/public/index.html references.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading CLAUDE.md 'Dashboard Integration' section. It claims SOC is integrated at http://localhost:3099 (3099 appears nowhere else in the repo except this CLAUDE.md line), 'Standalone version: public/index.html', 'Integrated source: ../ServiceMonitor/public/index.html', and '4 tabs: Overview, Architecture, Components, MCP Servers'. Reality verified: dashboard/ is a SvelteKit app (dashboard/package.json: 'dev': 'vite dev --port 5173'), README.md:99-104 confirms port 5173. No public/index.html exists in the SecurityCenter repo (find -name index.html returns only dashboard/src/app.html, the SvelteKit shell, and the actual static dashboard is k8s/dashboard/soc-command-center.html). The SvelteKit app has per-service routes (dashboard/src/routes/: cortex, grafana, opencti, thehive, wazuh = 5 services), Solarized theme in dashboard/src/app.css, and api.ts fetches metrics for those same 5 services. The 4-tab static HTML layout described does not exist in this repo. One nuance: ../ServiceMonitor/public/index.html DOES exist as a sibling project on disk, but it is an unrelated external project, so the reference is still stale/misleading for this repo's dashboard. The finding's claim 'find -name index.html finds none under the project' is accurate for SecurityCenter itself. Corrected file:line to CLAUDE.md:55 where the Dashboard Integration section begins. Severity low is fine: it is documentation drift in an internal CLAUDE.md, no runtime impact.

confirmed.title: architecture.md component diagram shows Mac Mini Disk 256GB while VM disk is 120GB and docs require 120GB+ free
confirmed.evidence: architecture.md:54 ASCII box states 'RAM: 16GB | CPU: M4 | Disk: 256GB+'. README/runbook prerequisites require '120GB+ free disk space' and the Lima VM disk is 120GiB (lima/k3s-soc.yaml:18). The 256GB figure is an undocumented host-disk assumption that conflicts with the stated 120GB requirement and may confuse sizing.
confirmed.recommendation: On docs/architecture.md:53, clarify the 256GB+ figure as total host disk capacity (distinct from the 120GB+ free-space requirement and the 120GiB Lima VM disk), or restate it to match the 120GB sizing used everywhere else so readers aren't confused about sizing.
confirmed.verifyNote: CONFIRMED but cited line is off by one. The ASCII box 'RAM: 16GB | CPU: M4 | Disk: 256GB+' is on docs/architecture.md LINE 53, not line 54 as the finding stated. CONFIRMED supporting facts: README.md:23 requires '120GB+ free disk space'; docs/runbooks/initial-setup.md:6 also requires '120GB+ free disk space'; lima/k3s-soc.yaml:18 sets disk: '120GiB'; docs/resource-requirements.md:94 recommends '120GB disk allocation for Lima VM'. The 256GB+ figure is plausibly intended as total host capacity (host disk) rather than free space or VM disk, but it is undocumented and inconsistent with the otherwise-uniform 120GB sizing. Cosmetic/clarity nit only.

confirmed.title: Hard-coded counts/versions throughout docs invite drift; no single source of truth
confirmed.evidence: Multiple docs hard-code numbers that already disagree with code: NetworkPolicy counts (CLAUDE.md 18, CHANGELOG 23, actual 31), tool totals, OpenCTI version (6.9.5 vs 6.6.6 vs chart-default), Wazuh agent 4.9.2 vs 4.14.3, supported version 1.3.x vs 1.4.0. These are spread across README.md, CLAUDE.md, CHANGELOG.md, .github/SECURITY.md, docs/architecture.md, docs/integrations.md, docs/mcp-servers.md, docs/network-diagram.md.
confirmed.recommendation: Centralize volatile facts (versions, counts, ports) in one referenced source of truth — e.g. a VERSIONS.md or a single Key table — and link other docs to it. Concretely reconcile the known drifts now: NetworkPolicy count to 31 (CLAUDE.md:26, CHANGELOG.md:92), OpenCTI platform/worker to 6.6.6 in README.md:11 / CHANGELOG.md:77 (or bump the manifests to 6.9.5 to match connectors — pick one), and Wazuh agent 4.9.2 -> 4.14.3 in docs/runbooks/adding-agents.md:41-52. Optionally add a CI doc-lint that cross-checks claimed counts/versions against k8s manifests.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed the concrete drift instances by reading files and grepping manifests. NetworkPolicy count: CLAUDE.md:26 says '18 explicit allow rules', CHANGELOG.md:92 says '23 rules', actual count is 31 (grep 'kind: NetworkPolicy' k8s/network-policies.yaml = 31). OpenCTI version: README.md:11 and CHANGELOG.md:77 say 'OpenCTI 6.9.5', but actual k8s/opencti/deployment.yaml:45 and :189 pin opencti/platform and opencti/worker at 6.6.6 (only the connectors are 6.9.5) — genuine platform-vs-docs mismatch. Wazuh agent: docs/runbooks/adding-agents.md:41-52 reference wazuh-agent-4.9.2, while manifests (k8s/wazuh/agents/daemonset.yaml:27 etc.), README, CLAUDE.md, and CHANGELOG all say 4.14.3 — runbook is stale. Supported version 1.3.x vs current per finding 1. The 'tool totals' sub-claim is vague and I could not pin a specific contradicting number in docs/mcp-servers.md (it does not restate a 21/7/8/6 total that disagrees), so treat that one sub-item as unverified; the rest of the design point stands strongly. CHANGELOG.md:92 is a valid concrete anchor for the NetworkPolicy 23-vs-31 drift. Severity: this is a design/maintainability observation about doc drift in an internal repo — low is appropriate (the original finding already labeled it low/design; note the finding listed effort 'medium' which is reasonable for the CI doc-lint option).

confirmed.title: TLS verification globally disabled for the SvelteKit server (NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0)
confirmed.evidence: vite.config.ts sets `process.env.NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED = '0';` at module load, and dashboard/.env (the live runtime file, also .env.example:26) sets `NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0`. This disables TLS certificate verification for ALL outbound fetch() calls in the Node process, including the adapter-node production server. Every server proxy (wazuh +server.ts authenticates with Basic creds over https://localhost:55000) is then vulnerable to MITM on those backend connections. CHANGELOG.md:12 claims this was fixed ("now use proper CA certificate verification") but it is still present.
confirmed.recommendation: Remove the global NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED override from both vite.config.ts:5 and the .env/.env.example files. For the one self-signed HTTPS backend (Wazuh at :55000), trust its specific CA instead: either set NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS to the Wazuh CA cert path, or pass a scoped undici Agent/Dispatcher with a per-request `ca` to the fetch calls in wazuh/+server.ts. All other configured backends (Grafana, OpenCTI, TheHive, Cortex) are plain http:// per .env, so only the Wazuh endpoint needs the scoped trust.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading all cited files. vite.config.ts:5 sets `process.env.NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED = '0';` at module load. dashboard/.env:13 contains `NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0` (the file physically exists on disk as the live runtime file) and .env.example:26 has the same. svelte.config.js uses `@sveltejs/adapter-node` (confirmed in package.json:13 + svelte.config.js), so this is the Node production server, not just dev — the override applies process-wide to all outbound fetch(). The Wazuh proxy (src/routes/api/wazuh/+server.ts:16-19) sends Basic-auth credentials over https://localhost:55000, and I confirmed there is NO scoped undici Agent / dispatcher / ca option / rejectUnauthorized anywhere in that handler, so the global flag is the only TLS posture — disabling cert verification for those credentialed backend calls. The high severity is justified: it nullifies cert verification for an authenticated HTTPS channel, enabling MITM. One correction to the evidence wording: CHANGELOG.md:12 does NOT claim THIS (the dashboard's NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED) was fixed — line 12 only covers OpenCTI and Wazuh Manager Kubernetes components (REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED:false, FILEBEAT_SSL_VERIFICATION_MODE:none) via ConfigMap CA cert; the dashboard env override is never mentioned in the changelog. The core vulnerability is real regardless of that wording nuance.

confirmed.title: TheHive OpenSearch TLS verification disabled (TH_DB_ES_SSL_VERIFY=false)
confirmed.evidence: Env sets `TH_DB_ES_SSL_ENABLED: "true"` then `TH_DB_ES_SSL_VERIFY: "false"`. TheHive talks to the shared OpenSearch (which holds all SIEM/IR data) with encryption but no cert validation, defeating the point of TLS and enabling MITM/credential capture on the cluster network.
confirmed.recommendation: Set TH_DB_ES_SSL_VERIFY="true" and provide the OpenSearch CA to TheHive, mirroring the opencti pattern: mount the opensearch-ca-cert ConfigMap (ca.crt) as a volume and point TheHive's CA setting at it (e.g. TH_DB_ES_KEYSTORE/truststore or the appropriate TH_DB_ES SSL CA env per StrangeBee's elasticsearch SSL config). Confirm an opensearch-ca-cert ConfigMap exists in the thehive namespace -- currently k8s/shared/opensearch-ca-cert.yaml only defines it for the opencti and wazuh namespaces, so a thehive copy must be added.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading the file. Lines 69-72 set TH_DB_ES_SSL_ENABLED="true" then TH_DB_ES_SSL_VERIFY="false", and TH_DB_ES_URI (line 68) is https://opensearch-cluster-master.shared.svc.cluster.local:9200. OpenSearch enforces TLS on the HTTP port (k8s/shared/opensearch.yaml line 75: plugins.security.ssl.http.enabled: true), so TheHive encrypts but does not validate the server cert -> the connection is vulnerable to MITM / cert-spoofing on the cluster network, defeating the configured TLS. The fix pattern is verified to exist: opencti/deployment.yaml lines 95-96 mount the opensearch-ca-cert ConfigMap and point ELASTICSEARCH__SSL__CA_CERTS at it. Severity lowered from high to medium: the cited data-sensitivity argument is valid (shared OpenSearch holds SIEM/IR data), but this is a same-cluster service-to-service hop protected by a default-deny NetworkPolicy mesh (per CLAUDE.md) using a self-signed internal CA in a single-node home lab, which materially reduces exploitability. Note (not part of this finding): TheHive's deployment also has no ES username/password env at all, unlike opencti which supplies ELASTICSEARCH__USERNAME/PASSWORD.

confirmed.title: Wazuh Dashboard OpenSearch TLS verification set to none
confirmed.evidence: `opensearch.ssl.verificationMode: none` — the Wazuh Dashboard connects to https://opensearch-cluster-master...:9200 with admin credentials but does not verify the server certificate, allowing MITM of the admin OpenSearch session.
confirmed.recommendation: Change verificationMode to full and supply opensearch.ssl.certificateAuthorities with the mounted root-ca.pem. Merge with the duplicate low-severity finding for the same line into one item.
confirmed.verifyNote: CONFIRMED — same line as the previous finding (configmap-dashboard.yaml:24, `opensearch.ssl.verificationMode: none`). The admin-credential and https://opensearch-cluster-master...:9200 details are accurate (deployment.yaml:74-82 INDEXER_USERNAME=admin + opensearch-password). This is a genuine but cluster-internal, self-signed-homelab weakness, so 'high' overstates it; normalized to medium. This is a DUPLICATE of 'Dashboard→OpenSearch TLS verification disabled' — same file:line, same root cause — and should be merged into a single ticket.

confirmed.title: Two divergent config systems for the same services; deploy uses Helm values that connect to TLS-only OpenSearch over http:// and omit credentials
confirmed.evidence: deploy-stack.sh installs OpenCTI/TheHive/Cortex via the Helm charts using these helm-values files, which set ELASTICSEARCH__URL/url to `http://opensearch-cluster-master...:9200`. But opensearch.yaml enables `plugins.security.ssl.http.enabled: true` (HTTPS only). The parallel raw deployment.yaml manifests correctly use `https://` + CA certs + credentials, but they are NOT applied by the deploy script. Additionally helm-values omit ELASTICSEARCH credentials entirely (raw deployment.yaml:88-94 has them), so even reaching OpenSearch would fail auth.
confirmed.recommendation: Pick one config path.
