Either (a) delete the raw deployment.yaml files and fix the helm-values to use https:// + the OpenSearch CA + ES username/password env, or (b) have deploy-stack.sh apply the raw manifests and drop the Helm install — but if choosing the raw path, first fix cortex/deployment.yaml:60 (it is http:// with no auth/CA) and add ES credentials to thehive/deployment.yaml. Ensure URLs and credentials match the TLS-secured OpenSearch in all chosen files.
confirmed.verifyNote: Core issue confirmed: deploy-stack.sh installs OpenCTI/TheHive/Cortex via Helm using these values files (lines 184, 143, 148), and all three set the OpenSearch URL to http://opensearch-cluster-master.shared.svc.cluster.local:9200 (opencti helm-values.yaml:29 ELASTICSEARCH__URL, thehive helm-values.yaml:23 database.elasticsearch.url, cortex helm-values.yaml:19 elasticsearch.url), while opensearch.yaml:75 sets plugins.security.ssl.http.enabled: true (HTTPS-only). The helm-values also omit ELASTICSEARCH username/password env. The parallel raw deployment.yaml files are NOT applied by deploy-stack.sh. BUT one cited fact is FALSE and must be corrected: cortex/deployment.yaml:60 does NOT 'correctly use https:// + CA certs + credentials' — it uses 'http://opensearch-cluster-master...:9200' (es_uri) with NO username/password and NO CA, i.e. it has the same defect as the helm-values. Among the raw manifests, only opencti/deployment.yaml:87-96 is fully correct (https + ELASTICSEARCH__USERNAME=admin + password from secret + CA certs at deployment.yaml:88-94 area); thehive/deployment.yaml:68-72 uses https + TH_DB_ES_SSL_ENABLED=true but with SSL_VERIFY=false and no ES user/pass. So 'the raw deployment.yaml manifests correctly use https + CA + credentials' is true only for OpenCTI, not for Cortex (and only partially for TheHive). Severity high is appropriate for the broken Helm-path connectivity.

confirmed.title: OpenCTI Helm values set ADMIN_TOKEN to empty string and never inject it from the secret
confirmed.evidence: `OPENCTI_ADMIN_TOKEN: ""` with a comment "Auto-generated, retrieve after first boot", and extraEnvs (lines 45-65) injects admin-password, rabbitmq, minio, health key — but NOT admin-token. The worker (deployment.yaml:205-209) requires OPENCTI_TOKEN=admin-token to authenticate. Under the Helm path the platform comes up with an empty/random token and the worker/connectors cannot authenticate, breaking ingestion.
confirmed.recommendation: Add an extraEnvs entry under opencti.extraEnvs sourcing the admin token from soc-opencti-secrets/admin-token (mirroring deployment.yaml:75-79), and ensure the chart wires the same value into the worker so platform, worker, and connectors share one token. Also verify the correct chart env key (likely APP__ADMIN__TOKEN) rather than OPENCTI_ADMIN_TOKEN. Alternatively, if the raw deployment.yaml is the only supported path, delete or clearly mark helm-values.yaml as non-functional to avoid a broken alternative.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading the files. helm-values.yaml:26 has `OPENCTI_ADMIN_TOKEN: ""` with comment 'Auto-generated, retrieve after first boot'. extraEnvs (lines 45-65) inject admin-password, rabbitmq-password, minio-secret-key (minio-root-password), and health-access-key, but there is NO entry sourcing the admin token from soc-opencti-secrets/admin-token. The admin-token key DOES exist in the secret (k8s/shared/secrets.yaml:49) and is referenced by the raw deployment (deployment.yaml:75-79 for APP__ADMIN__TOKEN, and deployment.yaml:205-209 for the worker's OPENCTI_TOKEN) plus all four connectors. So under the Helm install path the platform token is empty/auto-generated while the worker and connectors would use the fixed secret value, causing an auth mismatch that breaks ingestion. Two caveats that slightly temper this: (1) the raw deployment.yaml is the primary/active path per CLAUDE.md ('Raw K8s deployment for slim config'), and the Helm values file is a documented alternative install; (2) the env key used is the chart-specific `OPENCTI_ADMIN_TOKEN` rather than the platform-canonical `APP__ADMIN__TOKEN`, so even a correct value here may not map cleanly. The defect (token not injected from the secret on the Helm path) is genuine.

confirmed.title: Traefik basicAuth secret (soc-auth-secret) referenced but never created
confirmed.evidence: The soc-basic-auth Middleware references `secret: soc-auth-secret`, but deploy-stack.sh never creates it (only an instructional comment at line 61). All five IngressRoutes attach this middleware, so Traefik will fail the middleware (typically 500/blocking) until the secret exists, making every dashboard unreachable. This auth layer is also the only thing gating Grafana anonymous access.
confirmed.recommendation: In deploy_ingress() (deploy-stack.sh), create the secret before `kubectl apply` of the routes, sourced from env (not committed), and fail fast if the credential env var is absent -- mirroring the existing deploy_opencti() secret pattern. Traefik basicAuth expects a `users` key in htpasswd format, e.g. `htpasswd -nb "$SOC_AUTH_USER" "$SOC_AUTH_PASSWORD"` piped into `kubectl create secret generic soc-auth-secret -n kube-system --from-file=users=/dev/stdin --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -`. Document the required env vars in a .env.example per repo convention.
confirmed.verifyNote: Independently confirmed by reading the file and grepping the whole repo. traefik-routes.yaml:59 has `secret: soc-auth-secret` inside the `soc-basic-auth` Middleware (kube-system, lines 52-61). A repo-wide grep for 'soc-auth-secret' returns ONLY line 59 (the reference) and line 61 (an instructional comment: '# kubectl create secret generic soc-auth-secret --from-literal=users=...'). No actual creation exists anywhere -- not in any manifest, and not in deploy-stack.sh's deploy_ingress() (lines 204-219), which only installs Traefik and runs `kubectl apply -f traefik-routes.yaml`. Notably the same script DOES create other secrets (soc-opencti-secrets at line 170), so the pattern is known but omitted here. The soc-basic-auth middleware is attached to all FIVE IngressRoutes (lines 81, 105, 127, 149, 171: Wazuh, TheHive, Cortex, OpenCTI, Grafana). With a missing secret, Traefik's basicAuth middleware fails closed (returns 401/blocks; can surface as 500), so every dashboard is unreachable after a fresh deploy until the secret is created. This is a fail-closed access block, not a security exposure, but it does break all dashboard access -- high is appropriate for the functionality dimension. Minor nuance not affecting the verdict: the comment at line 60 also documents the htpasswd format the recommendation calls for.

confirmed.title: Helm repo URLs differ between setup-lima.sh and deploy-stack.sh for strangebee and opencti
confirmed.evidence: setup-lima adds `strangebee https://strangebee.github.io/helm-charts/` and `opencti https://devops-ia.github.io/helm-opencti/`; deploy-stack adds `strangebee https://charts.thehive-project.org` and `opencti https://charts.opencti.io/`. Both use `helm repo add ... || true`, so the second add silently fails (repo already exists with a different URL) and the wrong/likely-dead repo URL persists. helm-values headers also cite yet another opencti chart source. Result: nondeterministic or failing chart resolution.
confirmed.recommendation: Treat as the same fix as the other repo-URL finding: dedupe these into one finding. Define the repo list once (shared sourced file / single function) with --force-update, align deploy-stack.sh:41-44 to the maintained URLs the helm-values headers reference, and remove the divergent copy.
confirmed.verifyNote: The URL divergence is confirmed (same evidence as the medium finding above): deploy-stack.sh:41 strangebee=https://charts.thehive-project.org, :42 opencti=https://charts.opencti.io/, :44 traefik=https://helm.traefik.io/traefik, versus setup-lima.sh:92-95 using strangebee.github.io/helm-charts, devops-ia.github.io/helm-opencti, traefik.github.io/charts. This finding is a DUPLICATE of the 'medium' repo-URL finding, just scoped to deploy-stack alone and rated high. The divergence and silent '|| true' no-op are real. The 'wrong/dead repo' claim is plausible — the helm-values headers cite the setup-lima URLs, not deploy-stack's, and the legacy thehive-project.org / charts.opencti.io hosts are not the maintained chart repos — but I could not make network calls to confirm liveness, so I cannot independently verify 'dead.' Severity reduced from high to medium to match the real impact (nondeterministic chart resolution) and to avoid double-counting with the medium finding above.

confirmed.title: Grafana opensearch-grafana-auth secret / OPENSEARCH_PASSWORD never created
confirmed.evidence: envFromSecrets references `opensearch-grafana-auth` and the datasource uses `basicAuthPassword: "$__env{OPENSEARCH_PASSWORD}"`, but no script or manifest creates that secret or sets OPENSEARCH_PASSWORD. The OpenSearch datasource will authenticate with an empty password and fail.
confirmed.recommendation: Create the opensearch-grafana-auth secret with key OPENSEARCH_PASSWORD in deploy_monitoring() (e.g. derive from soc-shared-secrets opensearch-password via kubectl create secret), OR point envFromSecrets at soc-shared-secrets and map the existing opensearch-password key to OPENSEARCH_PASSWORD. Verify the datasource health-check passes after deploy.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. envFromSecrets references the secret 'opensearch-grafana-auth' (lines 66-67) and the OpenSearch-Wazuh datasource sets basicAuthPassword: '$__env{OPENSEARCH_PASSWORD}' (line 83). A repo-wide grep shows 'opensearch-grafana-auth' and 'OPENSEARCH_PASSWORD' appear ONLY in this values file — no manifest or script creates that secret or sets that env var. deploy_monitoring() in scripts/deploy-stack.sh (lines 194-202) only runs the helm install; it never creates the secret. k8s/shared/secrets.yaml defines soc-shared-secrets with key 'opensearch-password' (lower-case, different secret name and key), which is not consumed here. Result: the env var resolves empty and the Wazuh OpenSearch datasource authenticates with an empty password and fails. Breaks a core SOC function (Wazuh alert dashboards); medium is appropriate.

confirmed.title: OpenSearch CA cert ConfigMap shipped with placeholder; TLS-mount secrets (opensearch-certs, wazuh-dashboard-certs) never generated by deploy flow
confirmed.evidence: opensearch-ca-cert.yaml ships `REPLACE_WITH_YOUR_OPENSEARCH_CA_CERTIFICATE`. Deployments mount Secrets `opensearch-certs` and `wazuh-dashboard-certs`, but no script generates/creates these TLS secrets — the cert generation is only described in comments (self-signed-issuer.yaml, traefik-routes.yaml). Following deploy-stack.sh end-to-end leaves Wazuh/OpenCTI pods crash-looping on missing certs or trusting a placeholder CA.
confirmed.recommendation: Add a cert-generation step to deploy-stack.sh (or cert-manager Certificates) that runs before deploy_shared/deploy_wazuh and produces: the opensearch-certs Secret (esnode.pem/esnode-key.pem/root-ca.pem matching the SANs and admin_dn/nodes_dn in opensearch.yaml:81-84), wazuh-dashboard-certs, and a populated opensearch-ca-cert ConfigMap in the opencti and wazuh namespaces (and a thehive-namespace copy if finding #1 is remediated). Replace the placeholder blocks in opensearch-ca-cert.yaml or generate the ConfigMap at deploy time via kubectl create configmap --from-file.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. opensearch-ca-cert.yaml ships REPLACE_WITH_YOUR_OPENSEARCH_CA_CERTIFICATE at line 26 (opencti namespace ConfigMap) and again at line 39 (wazuh namespace ConfigMap) -- the finding cited only :26, so I added :39. The opensearch-certs Secret is consumed by shared/opensearch.yaml:48-49 (secretMounts) and wazuh/manager/deployment.yaml:135-137; wazuh-dashboard-certs is consumed by wazuh/dashboard/deployment.yaml:135-137 (cited lines exact). Grep confirms no script anywhere creates opensearch-certs, wazuh-dashboard-certs, or a populated opensearch-ca-cert -- deploy-stack.sh only creates soc-opencti-secrets. Following deploy-stack.sh end-to-end, OpenSearch itself will fail to start (its secretMount references the missing opensearch-certs Secret), and Wazuh manager/dashboard pods will fail to mount their cert secrets. Note one nuance: deploy-stack.sh deploys wazuh via raw configmaps+agent daemonset and OpenCTI/TheHive via helm charts (not the raw deployment.yaml files audited), so whether these exact manifests are applied depends on the operator also doing kubectl apply of the raw manifests; regardless, the secrets/CA are required and never generated, so the gap is real. Medium severity is appropriate given it blocks core OpenSearch/Wazuh startup.

confirmed.title: Secret references marked optional:true silently fall back to empty passwords against a security-enabled OpenSearch
confirmed.evidence: INDEXER_PASSWORD, DASHBOARD_PASSWORD, API_PASSWORD use `secretKeyRef ... optional: true`. If the secret/key is missing, the pod starts with an empty credential rather than failing. With OpenSearch security enabled this masks misconfiguration and can leave services running with empty/blank auth attempts.
confirmed.recommendation: Drop optional:true on all four secretKeyRefs so a missing secret/key causes immediate, visible pod-start failure. Note: the existing validate_secrets() gate in scripts/deploy-stack.sh (lines 50-65) only checks shared/secrets.yaml for base64 'CHANGE_ME' placeholders and whether the file exists -- it does NOT verify the soc-shared-secrets / soc-wazuh-secrets objects or their specific keys exist in the cluster. So removing optional:true (which makes the kubelet enforce presence at pod start) is the actual enforcement, not the deploy script. Either harden validate_secrets() to check the live secrets/keys or rely on removing optional:true.
confirmed.verifyNote: Independently confirmed. Manager deployment.yaml lines 89-93: INDEXER_PASSWORD secretKeyRef (soc-shared-secrets/opensearch-password) with optional:true at line 93. Dashboard deployment.yaml: INDEXER_PASSWORD optional:true at line 83, DASHBOARD_PASSWORD optional:true at line 93, API_PASSWORD (soc-wazuh-secrets/wazuh-api-password) optional:true at line 101. All four cited lines match exactly. In Kubernetes, optional:true means a missing secret or key leaves the env var unset rather than failing pod startup. CLAUDE.md confirms OpenSearch security is enabled with internal user auth, so a missing credential degrades to a failed/blank auth attempt instead of a fast visible failure. Severity medium is appropriate: it masks misconfiguration on a security-enabled indexer but does not by itself disable auth on the server side.

confirmed.title: k3s API exposed on 0.0.0.0:6443 with world-readable kubeconfig
confirmed.evidence: Provision uses `--write-kubeconfig-mode=644` (world-readable kubeconfig containing cluster-admin creds) and rewrites the kubeconfig server `127.0.0.1 -> 0.0.0.0`. Combined with port-forwarding 6443 to the host, the cluster-admin API is reachable from any process/user on the Mac and potentially the LAN. Any local user can read the kubeconfig and get cluster-admin.
confirmed.recommendation: Deduplicate: track this under findings #1 and #2. Net actions are the same — drop the 644 mode, revert the kubeconfig server to 127.0.0.1, and pin portForward hostIP to 127.0.0.1.
confirmed.verifyNote: DUPLICATE of findings #1 (0.0.0.0/port-forward) and #2 (644 kubeconfig), merged into one. Both underlying facts are confirmed at the cited lines: --write-kubeconfig-mode=644 (line 92) and the 127.0.0.1->0.0.0.0 sed (line 106). However the headline 'API exposed on 0.0.0.0:6443 ... reachable from the LAN' is overstated for the same reason as #1: the sed rewrites only the client kubeconfig, and Lima port-forwards default to hostIP 127.0.0.1 (no hostIP set in config). Marking real for the confirmed underlying facts but downgrading to low because it is redundant with #1/#2 and its incremental claim (LAN reachability) is the unsubstantiated part. Should be deduplicated against #1 and #2.

confirmed.title: Supply-chain: k3s and Helm installers piped from network to shell, unpinned
confirmed.evidence: `curl -sfL https://get.k3s.io | sh -` (no INSTALL_K3S_VERSION) and `curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/helm/helm/main/scripts/get-helm-3 | bash` (helm `main` branch, moving target). Both fetch-and-execute unpinned remote code at provision time; CLAUDE.md claims everything is pinned. A compromised upstream or MITM yields code execution as root in the VM.
confirmed.recommendation: Deduplicate into finding #3. Pin INSTALL_K3S_VERSION and the helm installer to a tagged release with checksum verification. Drop the 'CLAUDE.md claims everything is pinned' framing — CLAUDE.md only claims container images are pinned, which is a separate (accurate) statement.
confirmed.verifyNote: DUPLICATE of finding #3 (same two curl|sh / curl|bash lines, only k3s-soc.yaml cited here). The technical core is confirmed at lines 90 and 100. CAVEAT on the evidence: it asserts 'CLAUDE.md claims everything is pinned' — CLAUDE.md:28 actually says 'All container images pinned (no :latest tags)', which is specifically about CONTAINER IMAGES, not installer scripts. So there is no direct contradiction with CLAUDE.md as the evidence implies; that framing is an overstatement. Marking real for the confirmed unpinned-installer fact but downgrading to low as a redundant restatement of #3. Should be merged with #3.

confirmed.title: Grafana anonymous Viewer access combined with embedding behind a single shared basic-auth password
confirmed.evidence: grafana.ini enables `auth.anonymous.enabled: true` (org_role Viewer) and `allow_embedding: true`. The only gate in front is the shared soc-basic-auth Traefik middleware (one shared admin password, and that secret is currently not even created). Anyone who has the single basic-auth password gets unauthenticated Viewer access to all SOC dashboards/data with no per-user accountability.
confirmed.recommendation: Disable anonymous auth (auth.anonymous.enabled: false) and require Grafana login using the existing soc-grafana-secrets admin password (provision named per-user accounts if accountability is needed). If iframe embedding into the SOC Command Center is required, keep allow_embedding but rely on Grafana's own session/auth (or auth.proxy) rather than anonymous Viewer, and scope embedding to the dashboard origin. Separately, ensure the soc-auth-secret is actually created (it is referenced by the middleware but never provisioned), otherwise the Traefik basic-auth gate fails open or breaks routing.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. grafana.ini sets security.allow_embedding: true (line 47) and auth.anonymous.enabled: true with org_role: Viewer (lines 48-50). The only access gate is the Traefik soc-basic-auth middleware, applied to the Grafana IngressRoute at k8s/ingress/traefik-routes.yaml:170-172. That middleware (traefik-routes.yaml:51-61) references a single shared secret 'soc-auth-secret' containing one admin htpasswd entry. I verified the secret is NOT created anywhere: grep for soc-auth-secret across the repo returns only the middleware spec reference (line 59) and a how-to comment (line 61); no Secret manifest, deploy-stack.sh, setup script, or runbook creates it. So anyone with the single shared basic-auth password lands on Grafana as an anonymous Viewer with full read access to all SOC dashboards/data and no per-user accountability. Corrected the line range from 45-49 to 46-50 to match the actual security/auth.anonymous block (anonymous enabled is lines 48-49). Severity medium holds: a Grafana admin login secret (soc-grafana-secrets, lines 53-56) already exists, so removing anonymous access is low-effort, and exposure is currently behind the basic-auth layer (defense in depth) which keeps it below high.

confirmed.title: Dead/duplicate RabbitMQ manifest (rabbitmq-deployment.yaml) never applied
confirmed.evidence: rabbitmq-deployment.yaml is a full raw RabbitMQ Deployment (official image, header says "replaces Bitnami chart"), but deploy-stack.sh:97-99 installs RabbitMQ exclusively via the Bitnami Helm chart using rabbitmq.yaml. The raw manifest is dead code that contradicts the active path and will confuse maintainers (and notably the Bitnami chart catalog has since moved/restricted, so the comment's intent is stale).
confirmed.recommendation: Pick one RabbitMQ deployment method and remove the other. Since the active, tested path is the Bitnami Helm chart (deploy-stack.sh:96-99 + rabbitmq.yaml), delete the unused k8s/shared/rabbitmq-deployment.yaml. If the team instead prefers the raw official-image StatefulSet, wire it into deploy-stack.sh/teardown.sh and remove the Helm path and rabbitmq.yaml. Document the chosen approach in CLAUDE.md/README.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading the files. k8s/shared/rabbitmq-deployment.yaml is a complete raw RabbitMQ manifest (headless Service + StatefulSet, official image rabbitmq:4.1-management-alpine at line 53) whose line-1 header says '# RabbitMQ — Official image deployment (replaces Bitnami chart)'. The active deploy path in scripts/deploy-stack.sh (RabbitMQ block at lines 96-99) installs RabbitMQ exclusively via the Bitnami Helm chart using rabbitmq.yaml (a Helm values file). A repo-wide grep for 'rabbitmq-deployment' returns ZERO references anywhere (.sh/.yaml/.yml/.md); there is no kustomization file, no directory-glob 'kubectl apply -f .../shared/', and teardown.sh:84 only does 'helm uninstall rabbitmq'. So the raw StatefulSet is genuinely never applied — confirmed dead code that contradicts the active Helm path. Two corrections to the evidence: cited deploy-stack.sh range '97-99' is more precisely 96-99 (echo line is 96), and the image tag is rabbitmq:4.1-management-alpine, not 'rabbitmq:4-management-alpine'. The aside that the Bitnami catalog 'has since moved/restricted' is an unverified external claim and is not relied upon. Severity kept at medium (not lower) because the two manifests diverge in security posture and resource limits (raw sets automountServiceAccountToken:false, drops all caps, different memory limits), so applying the wrong one yields meaningfully different behavior — a real maintainer trap, though no runtime/security impact today since it is unused.

confirmed.title: Cortex connects to OpenSearch over plaintext http with no credentials
confirmed.evidence: `es_uri: http://opensearch-cluster-master...:9200` with no es username/password env. OpenSearch enforces HTTPS + auth (opensearch.yaml), so this is both a cleartext attempt and an unauthenticated one — Cortex storage init will fail, and any traffic that did flow would be unencrypted/unauthenticated.
confirmed.recommendation: Change es_uri to https://opensearch-cluster-master.shared.svc.cluster.local:9200, mount the OpenSearch CA (opensearch-ca-cert ConfigMap, ca.crt) and configure Cortex's ES SSL truststore/CA setting, and supply OpenSearch credentials (search.username / search.password equivalents -- e.g. es_username/es_password or search.username) from the soc-shared-secrets secret (opensearch-password key), matching the credential pattern used in opencti/deployment.yaml lines 88-94. Add an opensearch-ca-cert ConfigMap in the thehive namespace as well.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading the file. Cortex deployment lines 59-60 set es_uri="http://opensearch-cluster-master.shared.svc.cluster.local:9200" (plaintext http), and the env block (lines 58-68) contains no es username/password. OpenSearch enforces TLS on the HTTP listener (k8s/shared/opensearch.yaml line 75: plugins.security.ssl.http.enabled: true) and has the security plugin enabled with internal-user auth (securityConfig.enabled: true, lines 40-43; probes use tcpSocket precisely because HTTP endpoints require auth, lines 118-121). So the cited evidence is accurate: this is both a cleartext attempt against an HTTPS-only port and an unauthenticated one. In practice this is primarily a functional break (Cortex storage init will fail to connect) rather than a live data-exposure path, since no traffic actually flows over plaintext. low severity is appropriate.

confirmed.title: Live credential files (dashboard/.env) present on disk with real-looking API tokens
confirmed.evidence: dashboard/.env (gitignored, NOT in git history — verified) contains populated OPENCTI_TOKEN and THEHIVE_API_KEY values plus WAZUH_API_PASSWORD=wazuh. Not committed, but it is a plaintext secret file living in the working tree; the weak `wazuh/wazuh` default credential is also notable. Risk is local exposure / accidental future commit (the broad `.env.*` ignore is good but fragile).
confirmed.recommendation: Keep .env untracked (already enforced by .gitignore — do not weaken it). Since the OPENCTI_TOKEN and THEHIVE_API_KEY have been sitting in plaintext on disk, rotate them in their respective platforms. Replace the wazuh/wazuh default credential (lines 4-5) with a strong password. For runtime, the project convention is K8s Secrets (template k8s/shared/secrets.yaml) — prefer loading dashboard runtime secrets from there or a secrets manager rather than a dotfile where feasible.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading the file directly. dashboard/.env exists (453 bytes), is NOT git-tracked (git ls-files empty), has NO git history (git log --all empty), and IS gitignored (matched by .gitignore line 2 '.env'). Line 8 OPENCTI_TOKEN holds a populated UUID-format value and line 10 THEHIVE_API_KEY holds a populated 32-char token — both real-looking, not placeholders (contrast line 12 CORTEX_API_KEY=CHANGE_ME which IS a placeholder). The weak default credential is also confirmed: WAZUH_API_USER=wazuh / WAZUH_API_PASSWORD=wazuh, though that pair is on lines 4-5, not line 8 as the evidence text loosely implied. Gitignore is sound (.env, .env.* with !.env.example exception) and .env.example files are the only tracked env files. All services use localhost URLs (homelab/local-dev). Low severity is correct: genuine plaintext-secrets-at-rest plus weak default cred, but never committed, properly ignored, and scoped to local self-hosted services — no actual exposure. Did not print any secret values.

confirmed.title: Wazuh agent DaemonSet is privileged with hostPID/hostNetwork and hostPath / mount
confirmed.evidence: privileged: true, hostNetwork: true, hostPID: true, and hostPath mount of `/` (readOnly). For a host IDS/FIM agent this is largely inherent and documented, but it is the highest-privilege workload in the cluster and a compromise of the agent image equals host root on the node.
confirmed.recommendation: Accept as inherent for a node-level agent but tighten where feasible: add seccompProfile: RuntimeDefault under securityContext; pin the agent image by digest rather than the 4.14.3 tag; evaluate dropping hostPID if rootcheck/FIM does not require host process visibility; and prefer scoping hostPath mounts to specific directories (note /etc and /var/log are already mounted separately, so the broad '/' host-root mount at lines 67-69 may be reducible). Document the residual risk that compromise of the agent image equals host root on the node.
confirmed.verifyNote: Independently confirmed. daemonset.yaml line 23 hostNetwork:true, line 24 hostPID:true, lines 64-65 securityContext with privileged:true, and lines 67-69 host-root volume hostPath path:/ (mounted readOnly:true at line 44). Image pinned by tag wazuh/wazuh-agent:4.14.3 (line 27), not by digest. grep confirms no seccompProfile/RuntimeDefault anywhere in k8s/. The cited line range '64-75' is slightly loose: the relevant securityContext is 64-65 and the hostPath '/' mount is 67-69; lines 70-75 are the additional host-var-log and host-etc hostPath volumes. Corrected to 23-24,64-69. Severity low is accurate -- this is largely inherent to a host IDS/FIM agent and is documented inline (comments at lines 19, 22, 40, 65), and the host-root mount is readOnly.

confirmed.title: Wazuh MCP startup connectivity probe to base URL relies on global TLS bypass / may hang
confirmed.evidence: The startup self-test does a plain `fetch("${WAZUH_URL}/")` to an https self-signed endpoint without a configured CA; it only works if NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED is globally disabled by the operator's env (as the dashboard does), which is an implicit, unsafe dependency. Tool calls themselves go through fetchWithRetry which has the same trust assumption.
confirmed.recommendation: Resolve via the main TLS finding: require NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS or use a scoped undici Agent with the pinned CA for both the probe and tool calls, instead of relying on operators globally disabling TLS verification. Note the probe already has a 5s timeout, so it will not hang. Consider de-duplicating with the TLS finding.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. main() at line 206 does fetch(`${WAZUH_URL}/`) to the https self-signed endpoint with no configured CA; it only succeeds if the operator has globally disabled TLS verification (NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0), an implicit unsafe dependency. The probe IS bounded by a 5s AbortController timeout (lines 204-206), so 'may hang' is inaccurate — it times out and falls into the catch at line 208 logging a warning; startup still proceeds. Tool calls go through fetchWithRetry which shares the same no-CA assumption. This overlaps heavily with the dedicated TLS finding (file 43,63,206); to avoid double-counting it is best treated as low and folded into the TLS remediation. isReal=true; 'may hang' over-stated.

confirmed.title: No automated tests or CI for MCP servers, dashboard, or manifests
confirmed.evidence: There is no .github/workflows directory; no unit tests, no kubeconform/kubeval manifest validation, no npm audit / dependency scanning, no helm lint. For a security product, the absence of CI means the config-divergence and TLS regressions above can ship unnoticed (and did, per the CHANGELOG drift).
confirmed.recommendation: Add a GitHub Actions workflow: build/typecheck all three MCP packages (which would immediately fail today, surfacing Finding 1) and the dashboard, kubeconform on k8s/, helm lint on values, and a secret scanner (gitleaks) to enforce the no-committed-secrets invariant. Pin actions by commit SHA. Add at least smoke tests for fetchWithRetry/formatResponse in the shared package.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. .github/ exists (ISSUE_TEMPLATE, PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md, SECURITY.md) but there is NO .github/workflows directory and no CI of any kind. No project test files exist (only node_modules vendor tests). No root package.json to orchestrate builds. The CHANGELOG-drift claim is independently verified: CHANGELOG 1.4.0 (2026-02-27) states '@matchbox/mcp-shared package ... All 3 MCP servers now import from shared' and 'MCP servers now depend on @matchbox/mcp-shared (file: link)', yet shared was never built/installed and the committed dist/ is stale — exactly the regression CI would have caught (this is the root cause that let Finding 1 ship). I bumped severity from nit to low: the absence of CI is the systemic gap that enabled a build-breaking change to be committed and documented as done. Still a process/design issue, not a runtime defect itself, so low (not medium).

confirmed.title: Module-level JWT/token cache in SvelteKit endpoint is shared across all requests with no concurrency guard
confirmed.evidence: jwtToken/tokenExpiry are module-scoped; concurrent first requests can trigger multiple simultaneous auth calls (thundering herd) and the cache is process-global. Minor for a single-user homelab, but not a robust pattern.
confirmed.recommendation: Coalesce concurrent refreshes by memoizing the pending getToken() promise in a module-scoped variable (e.g. `let inflight: Promise<string> | null`): if a refresh is already underway, await the same promise; clear it in a finally block. Optional given the single-user homelab scope.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading the file. jwtToken (line 9) and tokenExpiry (line 10) are module-scoped, hence process-global and shared across all requests. getToken() (lines 13-28) checks the cache then performs an awaited fetch with no in-flight-promise memoization, so concurrent first requests (e.g. the Promise.all of 5 wazuhFetch calls at lines 42-48 on a cold cache) can each fall through the `if (jwtToken && Date.now() < tokenExpiry)` guard and issue separate /security/user/authenticate POSTs — a genuine thundering-herd / redundant-auth pattern. Accurately characterized as minor for a single-user homelab; nit severity is correct.

confirmed.title: deploy-stack.sh never deploys the Wazuh Manager or Dashboard
confirmed.evidence: deploy_wazuh() applies only configmap-ossec/rules/decoders and the agent DaemonSet. It never applies k8s/wazuh/manager/deployment.yaml (the StatefulSet) or k8s/wazuh/dashboard/deployment.yaml. It prints 'Wazuh Helm chart may need manual setup' and does no helm install for wazuh either. Yet test-flow.sh:71-72 asserts wazuh-manager and wazuh-dashboard pods are Running.
confirmed.recommendation: In deploy_wazuh(), kubectl apply k8s/wazuh/manager/deployment.yaml, k8s/wazuh/dashboard/deployment.yaml, and k8s/wazuh/dashboard/configmap-dashboard.yaml; remove the misleading 'Helm chart may need manual setup' note. Or commit to a Helm path and delete the raw manifests. Pick a single deploy method so test-flow.sh:71-72 can pass.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed exactly. deploy_wazuh() (lines 117-134) applies only configmap-ossec.yaml (121), configmap-rules.yaml (122), configmap-decoders.yaml (123), and agents/daemonset.yaml (126). It prints 'NOTE: Wazuh Helm chart may need manual setup' (130) and does no helm install for wazuh. grep confirms deploy-stack.sh never references manager/deployment.yaml, dashboard/deployment.yaml, or configmap-dashboard.yaml. Those manifests DO exist: k8s/wazuh/manager/deployment.yaml (kind StatefulSet, line 26) and k8s/wazuh/dashboard/deployment.yaml (kind Deployment, line 19), but are never applied. Meanwhile test-flow.sh:71 asserts 'Wazuh Manager running' and :72 'Wazuh Dashboard running' (and :114 execs into wazuh-manager-0). Both assertions are guaranteed to fail after a fresh deploy. Critical is correct — a core SIEM component the project is built around is silently never deployed.

confirmed.title: Ingress TLS is non-functional: cert-manager/issuer/soc-tls-cert/soc-auth-secret are never created
confirmed.evidence: deploy_ingress() installs Traefik and applies traefik-routes.yaml, but never installs cert-manager, never applies k8s/ingress/self-signed-issuer.yaml, and never creates the soc-tls-cert TLS secret or the soc-basic-auth backing secret soc-auth-secret. Every IngressRoute references tls.secretName: soc-tls-cert and middleware soc-basic-auth (traefik-routes.yaml:48,59,80-82,107-108...). Grep confirms 'cert-manager never installed by any script' and 'deploy script never creates soc-tls-cert or soc-auth-secret'. Result: HTTPS ingress and basic-auth fail after a clean deploy.
confirmed.recommendation: In deploy_ingress(), before applying traefik-routes.yaml, either (Option A) install cert-manager (e.g. `helm repo add jetstack https://charts.jetstack.io && helm upgrade --install cert-manager jetstack/cert-manager -n cert-manager --create-namespace --set installCRDs=true --wait`) then `kubectl apply -f k8s/ingress/self-signed-issuer.yaml`; or (Option B) generate a self-signed cert with openssl and run `kubectl create secret tls soc-tls-cert --cert=tls.crt --key=tls.key -n kube-system` plus `kubectl create secret generic soc-auth-secret -n kube-system --from-literal=users="$(htpasswd -nbB admin <pw>)"`. Gate both with `kubectl get secret ... || create` idempotency checks, and verify soc-tls-cert and soc-auth-secret exist before `kubectl apply -f traefik-routes.yaml`. Also add a generate-tls-cert.sh (currently referenced by comments but missing) or remove that dangling reference.
confirmed.verifyNote: Independently confirmed by reading deploy-stack.sh and grepping the whole repo. deploy_ingress() (lines 204-219) only runs `helm upgrade --install traefik` (208-211) and `kubectl apply -f k8s/ingress/traefik-routes.yaml` (214). It never installs cert-manager, never applies k8s/ingress/self-signed-issuer.yaml, never runs `kubectl create secret tls soc-tls-cert`, and never creates soc-auth-secret. Grep results: cert-manager/jetstack appear ONLY in comments inside self-signed-issuer.yaml (lines 16,18,19,20,28,51); no .sh file references self-signed-issuer at all; soc-tls-cert is referenced only by its consumers in traefik-routes.yaml (TLSStore line 48; IngressRoute tls.secretName at 84,108,130,152,174) and the unapplied Certificate in self-signed-issuer.yaml — nothing creates it; soc-auth-secret is referenced only by the soc-basic-auth Middleware at traefik-routes.yaml:59, with the htpasswd/create-secret lines commented out (60-61). The scripts/generate-tls-cert.sh referenced in self-signed-issuer.yaml comments does not exist. test-flow.sh and teardown.sh only touch the unrelated `soc-opencti-secrets`. So after a clean `./scripts/deploy-stack.sh`, TLS termination has no cert (default TLSStore + per-route secretName point at a non-existent secret) and the basicAuth middleware cannot resolve its backing secret, breaking every IngressRoute. Severity corrected from critical to high: this is a single-node homelab SOC (per CLAUDE.md), not an internet-facing production system, but it still fully breaks the documented one-command HTTPS+auth ingress path.

confirmed.title: opensearch-certs Secret and opensearch-ca-cert ConfigMap are never created but are mounted by core pods
confirmed.evidence: opensearch.yaml mounts secret opensearch-certs; wazuh/manager/deployment.yaml:135-137 mounts secret opensearch-certs; opencti/deployment.yaml:159-161 mounts configmap opensearch-ca-cert. Grep of scripts/ returns '(NOT created by any script)'. There is a k8s/shared/opensearch-ca-cert.yaml file but it is never applied by deploy-stack.sh. OpenSearch, Wazuh Manager and OpenCTI pods will fail to schedule (missing volume) on a clean deploy.
confirmed.recommendation: In deploy_shared(), before the OpenSearch helm install, generate the OpenSearch node/CA certs (e.g. via openssl with the SANs/DNs declared in opensearch.yaml: CN=opensearch-node,O=MatchBox,C=US and CN=admin,O=MatchBox,C=US) and create the opensearch-certs secret in the shared namespace from esnode.pem/esnode-key.pem/root-ca.pem. Then populate and kubectl apply k8s/shared/opensearch-ca-cert.yaml (currently placeholder certs) for the opencti and wazuh namespaces. Separately, fix the deploy script so the wazuh manager Deployment and the consumer of opensearch-ca-cert are actually deployed, or remove those orphaned raw manifests if the Helm charts are the source of truth.
confirmed.verifyNote: CONFIRMED with corrections. opensearch.yaml:47-50 declares secretMounts -> secretName opensearch-certs. A repo-wide grep (excluding node_modules) shows opensearch-certs is mounted in opensearch.yaml and wazuh/manager/deployment.yaml:135-137, but is NEVER created: the only kubectl-create-secret calls in scripts/deploy-stack.sh create soc-opencti-secrets (line 170) and there is no cert generation (no openssl genrsa, no kubectl create secret opensearch-certs anywhere). opensearch-ca-cert.yaml exists as a manifest (configmaps in opencti+wazuh namespaces, both placeholder REPLACE_WITH_YOUR... certs) but deploy-stack.sh never applies it (grep for opensearch-ca-cert / opensearch-ca-cert.yaml in scripts returns nothing). The OpenSearch Helm install IS run by deploy_shared() at lines 87-89 with --wait, and its secretMounts reference the missing opensearch-certs secret, so the OpenSearch pod will fail to start on a clean deploy -> genuinely critical. TWO INACCURACIES in the evidence: (1) wazuh/manager/deployment.yaml is NOT applied by deploy_wazuh() (it only applies configmap-ossec/rules/decoders + agents/daemonset; the manager Deployment is never kubectl-applied), and (2) opencti/deployment.yaml (the raw manifest mounting opensearch-ca-cert at lines 159-161) is NOT applied either -- deploy_opencti() uses the opencti/opencti Helm chart, not the raw deployment.yaml. So the claim that 'Wazuh Manager and OpenCTI pods will fail to schedule' is overstated for those two specific raw manifests since the deploy script never schedules them. The OpenSearch failure alone keeps this critical.

confirmed.title: Helm repo URLs differ between setup-lima.sh and deploy-stack.sh; several are wrong/dead
confirmed.evidence: deploy-stack.sh setup_helm_repos uses strangebee=https://charts.thehive-project.org and opencti=https://charts.opencti.io/, while setup-lima.sh:92-93 uses strangebee=https://strangebee.github.io/helm-charts/ and opencti=https://devops-ia.github.io/helm-opencti/. Traefik repo also differs (helm.traefik.io/traefik vs traefik.github.io/charts). charts.thehive-project.org and charts.opencti.io are not the maintained chart repos.
confirmed.recommendation: Treat as the same fix as the other repo-URL finding: dedupe these into one finding. Define the repo list once (shared sourced file / single function) with --force-update, align deploy-stack.sh:41-44 to the maintained URLs the helm-values headers reference, and remove the divergent copy.
confirmed.verifyNote: The URL divergence is confirmed (same evidence as the medium finding above): deploy-stack.sh:41 strangebee=https://charts.thehive-project.org, :42 opencti=https://charts.opencti.io/, :44 traefik=https://helm.traefik.io/traefik, versus setup-lima.sh:92-95 using strangebee.github.io/helm-charts, devops-ia.github.io/helm-opencti, traefik.github.io/charts. This finding is a DUPLICATE of the 'medium' repo-URL finding, just scoped to deploy-stack alone and rated high. The divergence and silent '|| true' no-op are real. The 'wrong/dead repo' claim is plausible — the helm-values headers cite the setup-lima URLs, not deploy-stack's, and the legacy thehive-project.org / charts.opencti.io hosts are not the maintained chart repos — but I could not make network calls to confirm liveness, so I cannot independently verify 'dead.' Severity reduced from high to medium to match the real impact (nondeterministic chart resolution) and to avoid double-counting with the medium finding above.

confirmed.title: Dual/triple deployment implementations: raw manifests vs Helm values vs deploy script, no single source of truth
confirmed.evidence: For OpenCTI, TheHive, Cortex, RabbitMQ and Redis there are BOTH raw manifests (opencti/deployment.yaml, thehive/deployment.yaml, cortex/deployment.yaml, shared/rabbitmq-deployment.yaml) AND helm-values files; deploy-stack.sh uses Helm and never applies any of these raw manifests (grep: only shared/opensearch.yaml is referenced). TheHive is even self-contradictory: helm-values.yaml:2 cites a StrangeBee chart while deployment.yaml:2 says 'StrangeBee doesn't maintain a public Helm chart'. This is a large maintenance trap and the manifests drift (e.g. OpenCTI image versions).
confirmed.recommendation: Choose ONE deploy strategy per component and delete the unused parallel set. The genuinely duplicated components are OpenCTI, TheHive, Cortex, and RabbitMQ (raw deployment.yaml + helm-values.yaml, with deploy-stack.sh using Helm and ignoring the raw manifests). Redis is NOT duplicated -- it only has a Helm values file, so exclude it. A 2026 best practice is a single Kustomize base/overlays tree or a Helm umbrella chart with subchart deps, deployed via Argo CD/Flux. Also fix the TheHive doc contradiction (deployment.yaml:2 vs helm-values.yaml:2).
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading the files and scripts/deploy-stack.sh. OpenCTI, TheHive, and Cortex each have BOTH a raw deployment.yaml (k8s/opencti/deployment.yaml, k8s/thehive/deployment.yaml, k8s/thehive/cortex/deployment.yaml -- all real K8s Deployment/StatefulSet kinds) AND a helm-values.yaml. RabbitMQ also has both: k8s/shared/rabbitmq.yaml (Helm values header 'Chart: bitnami/rabbitmq') and k8s/shared/rabbitmq-deployment.yaml (raw Service+StatefulSet, line 5 'kind: Service', line 28 'kind: StatefulSet'). deploy-stack.sh deploys all of these via 'helm upgrade --install' (opencti L184, thehive L143, cortex L148, rabbitmq L97) and NEVER 'kubectl apply's the raw opencti/thehive/cortex/rabbitmq-deployment manifests (grep of scripts/ for those filenames returns nothing). The TheHive self-contradiction is REAL: deployment.yaml:2 says '(StrangeBee doesn't maintain a public Helm chart)' while helm-values.yaml:2 cites 'Chart: strangebee/thehive (https://strangebee.github.io/helm-charts/)' and deploy-stack.sh:143 actually runs 'helm ... strangebee/thehive'. ONE EVIDENCE ERROR: the finding lists Redis as having dual implementations citing 'shared/redis.yaml' as a raw manifest -- that is FALSE. k8s/shared/redis.yaml is a Helm values file (header 'Redis Helm Values ... Chart: bitnami/redis', no top-level 'kind:'), and there is no redis-deployment.yaml. Redis has only one (Helm) implementation. Core finding is valid; drop Redis from the affected list.

confirmed.title: OpenCTI core pinned to 6.6.6 but connectors and docs say 6.9.5 (version skew)
confirmed.evidence: platform and worker are opencti/platform:6.6.6 / opencti/worker:6.6.6 (deployment.yaml:45,189) while connectors are opencti/connector-*:6.9.5 (virustotal.yaml:28, abuseipdb.yaml:29, etc.) and README.md:11 / CHANGELOG.md:77 advertise 'OpenCTI 6.9.5'. OpenCTI requires platform and connectors to be on the same minor version; this skew typically breaks the connector registration API.
confirmed.recommendation: Pin platform, worker, and all connectors to a single OpenCTI minor version and update README.md:11 / CHANGELOG.md:77 to match. Also verify the actually-deployed version in k8s/opencti/helm-values.yaml, since deploy-stack.sh installs via Helm rather than the raw deployment.yaml.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. k8s/opencti/deployment.yaml uses opencti/platform:6.6.6 (line 45) and opencti/worker:6.6.6 (line 189). All four connectors are 6.9.5: virustotal.yaml:28, abuseipdb.yaml:29, alienvault-otx.yaml:29, mitre-attack.yaml:29. README.md:11 advertises 'OpenCTI 6.9.5' and CHANGELOG.md:77 says 'OpenCTI 6.9.5'. CLAUDE.md does not pin a version but lists those connectors. The platform/worker vs connector minor-version skew (6.6 vs 6.9) is real and OpenCTI does expect platform and connectors on a compatible version. NOTE: per the design finding above, the raw deployment.yaml is NOT what actually gets deployed (Helm chart is), so the runtime version actually depends on opencti/helm-values.yaml; nonetheless the raw manifest and docs are internally inconsistent and would mislead, and effort to fix is trivial. High severity retained.

confirmed.title: NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 set globally, disabling all TLS validation process-wide
confirmed.evidence: vite.config.ts sets process.env.NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED = '0' at module load, and dashboard/.env.example:30 ships the same. This disables certificate validation for the entire Node process (every outbound fetch, including the production adapter-node server), not just the Wazuh self-signed endpoint, enabling silent MITM.
confirmed.recommendation: Remove the global NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 from vite.config.ts, .env, and .env.example. Scope trust to the SOC self-signed CA: pass a per-request undici/https Agent (or set NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS to the SOC CA cert) only on the Wazuh fetches in src/routes/api/wazuh/+server.ts. Never globally disable TLS verification in code or committed env. Note: the env-file copy is the line that actually affects the adapter-node production server, so prioritize removing it there.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading the file: vite.config.ts:5 sets `process.env.NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED = '0'` at module load with a comment about self-signed Wazuh certs. This disables certificate validation for the entire Node process, not just the one Wazuh call. The same var is also shipped in committed env files: dashboard/.env.example line 26 (the finding cited :30, which is wrong — the file is only 27 lines) and dashboard/.env. One accuracy caveat on the evidence's reasoning: vite.config.ts itself only executes during dev/build, NOT in the adapter-node production server, so vite.config alone does not weaken the production runtime. However the committed .env carrying NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 can poison the production runtime if dotenv-loaded. The core security claim (global TLS-validation disable, enabling MITM on every outbound fetch including all the http(s) service calls in src/routes/api/*) is real. High severity is appropriate.

confirmed.title: dashboard/.env contains real credentials on disk (gitignored, but a live secret-sprawl surface)
confirmed.evidence: git check-ignore confirms dashboard/.env is ignored (good), but it physically holds WAZUH_API_PASSWORD/OPENCTI_TOKEN(36)/THEHIVE_API_KEY(32)/CORTEX_API_KEY in plaintext on disk alongside the source tree. Combined with NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 also living there, this is a fragile secret-handling pattern.
confirmed.recommendation: Source dashboard secrets at runtime from the same K8s Secrets used by the rest of the stack (or a local secret manager) rather than a long-lived plaintext .env in the working tree. Rotate the OPENCTI_TOKEN and THEHIVE_API_KEY that have been sitting in this file. Document that .env is for ephemeral local dev only. The short WAZUH_API_PASSWORD/CORTEX_API_KEY appear to be placeholders — confirm before treating them as live secrets.
confirmed.verifyNote: Verified: `git check-ignore dashboard/.env` returns the path (exit 0) so it is gitignored — not committed. Inspected key names and value lengths WITHOUT printing values: OPENCTI_TOKEN is 36 chars and THEHIVE_API_KEY is 32 chars — these are clearly real, token-shaped secrets, so the finding's central claim holds. Two sub-claims are weaker than stated: WAZUH_API_PASSWORD is only 5 chars and CORTEX_API_KEY only 9 chars, which look like placeholders/stubs rather than strong real credentials, so calling out WAZUH_API_PASSWORD specifically overstates it. The NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 also living in this file is confirmed. Downgrading severity to low: the file is gitignored, on a single-user home-lab Mac Mini, and the genuinely sensitive values (OPENCTI/THEHIVE tokens) are exposed only via local-disk/secret-sprawl risk, not via the repo. This is a hygiene smell rather than a medium-impact exposure.

confirmed.title: Grafana iframe and service links hardcode http://localhost:3000 (client-side), breaking the single-pane view off-localhost
confirmed.evidence: buildSrc() returns `http://localhost:3000${path}` and externalHref (line 22) does the same; these execute in the browser, so they ignore the server-side GRAFANA_URL env used by /api/grafana. config.ts:21-65 likewise hardcodes localhost dashboardUrls, and per-service pages link to http://localhost:9000/9001/4000 (thehive/cortex/opencti +page.svelte:43). Any user not browsing from the host machine gets broken embeds/links.
confirmed.recommendation: Expose a public-facing base URL via SvelteKit public/runtime env (e.g. PUBLIC_GRAFANA_URL and per-service PUBLIC_*_URL) and build all iframe srcs, config.dashboardUrl values, and the per-service +page.svelte links from it. Remove hardcoded http://localhost from GrafanaEmbed.svelte, config.ts, and the thehive/cortex/opencti/wazuh +page.svelte files.
confirmed.verifyNote: Fully confirmed by reading the source. GrafanaEmbed.svelte:16 buildSrc() returns `http://localhost:3000${path}` and externalHref at :22 does the same; both are <script>/$derived values that run in the browser, so they ignore the server-side GRAFANA_URL env that src/routes/api/grafana/+server.ts:5 reads. config.ts dashboardUrls are hardcoded localhost at lines 21/32/43/54/65 (finding said 21-65 — accurate range). I additionally verified these config values are consumed client-side: Sidebar.svelte:41 renders `href={svc.dashboardUrl}` and ServiceCard.svelte:18 calls `window.open(service.dashboardUrl)`, so they genuinely break off-host. Per-service page links confirmed: thehive/+page.svelte:43 -> localhost:9000, cortex/+page.svelte:43 -> localhost:9001, opencti/+page.svelte:43 -> localhost:4000, wazuh/+page.svelte:67 -> https://localhost:5601. Every cited line checks out. Keeping high: this breaks the core single-pane-of-glass functionality for any non-localhost browser.

confirmed.title: w2thive.py crashes when full_log is JSON null
confirmed.evidence: `alert.get('full_log', 'N/A')[:500]` uses the default only when the key is absent. If the alert JSON contains "full_log": null, .get returns None and None[:500] raises TypeError, aborting alert creation for that event.
confirmed.recommendation: Replace with (alert.get('full_log') or 'N/A')[:500] so an explicit JSON null falls back to 'N/A'. For defense in depth, also guard the list joins against explicit null, e.g. ', '.join(rule.get('groups') or []) at line 126 and the mitre['id'] iteration at lines 142-143. response.text[:200] needs no change.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading line 129: f"- **Full Log:** {alert.get('full_log', 'N/A')[:500]}\n". The .get default 'N/A' applies ONLY when the key is absent. A valid alert JSON containing "full_log": null returns Python None, and None[:500] raises TypeError. That exception propagates out of create_thehive_alert before the requests.post call (the try/except at lines 152-172 wraps only the HTTP send, not the payload-building f-string), so the entire alert creation is aborted for that event. Wazuh full_log is usually a string, so this triggers only on an explicit null value, which is why medium (not high) is appropriate. Note the evidence's broad 'apply same guard to other .get(...)[:N]/join() usages' is partially overstated: response.text[:200] at line 165 is always a str and safe; ', '.join(rule.get('groups', [])) at line 126 is safe against a missing key but would likewise crash on an explicit "groups": null. So the only other genuinely-null-vulnerable join is groups/mitre.

confirmed.title: w2thive.py integration is never deployed (orphaned)
confirmed.evidence: The script docstring says it is 'Deployed as a ConfigMap and mounted into the Wazuh Manager pod', but no ConfigMap wraps it, the Wazuh manager deployment does not mount it, ossec.conf has no <integration> block referencing custom-w2thive, and no script deploys it. The advertised Wazuh->TheHive pipeline does not exist as wired.
confirmed.recommendation: Either (a) fully wire it: package w2thive.py as a ConfigMap, mount it at /var/ossec/integrations/custom-w2thive.py with mode 0755 (executable), uncomment and parameterize the <integration> stanza in configmap-ossec.yaml injecting the TheHive API key from a Secret (replace THEHIVE_API_KEY_PLACEHOLDER), and apply all of it in deploy_wazuh(); or (b) if it remains future work, correct the script docstring (lines 9-10) to say 'not yet deployed' so it matches reality and the CHANGELOG. Also note the ossec.conf integration uses the legacy <api_key> form passed as argv to the script, matching create_thehive_alert(hook_url, api_key, ...); ensure the Secret-sourced value flows through correctly when enabling.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. (1) The script docstring (lines 9-10) claims it is 'Deployed as a ConfigMap and mounted into the Wazuh Manager pod at /var/ossec/integrations/custom-w2thive.py'. (2) The only <integration> stanza naming custom-w2thive lives in k8s/wazuh/manager/configmap-ossec.yaml lines 52-58 but is wholly inside an XML comment block: line 51 '<!-- Disabled: integration script not yet deployed. Uncomment when w2thive.py is installed.' and the closing '-->' at line 59 — so it is inert. (3) The wazuh-manager deployment (k8s/wazuh/manager/deployment.yaml) mounts only config-staging, etc-writable, custom-rules, custom-decoders, opensearch-certs (volumes lines 123-137; container volumeMounts lines 98-109); no ConfigMap wraps w2thive.py and nothing mounts it into /var/ossec/integrations/. (4) deploy-stack.sh deploy_wazuh() (lines 117-126) applies the ossec/rules/decoders configmaps and the agent daemonset but never creates or applies the integration script. CHANGELOG.md:62 itself states 'TheHive integration commented out in ossec.conf (w2thive script not yet deployed)', corroborating that the pipeline is intentionally not wired. The advertised Wazuh->TheHive pipeline does not exist as deployed. Severity medium is fair: it is a missing/aspirational feature rather than a broken-in-production data path, and the inert state is documented in the CHANGELOG, but the script's own docstring and docs/integrations.md / docs/architecture.md present it as deployed, which is misleading.

confirmed.title: Wazuh Manager INDEXER_PASSWORD marked optional, allowing silent passwordless start
confirmed.evidence: INDEXER_PASSWORD uses secretKeyRef with optional: true and INDEXER_USERNAME is hardcoded 'admin'. If soc-shared-secrets/opensearch-password is missing, the manager starts with an empty indexer password instead of failing fast, which can silently break Filebeat shipping or fall back to insecure behavior.
confirmed.recommendation: Remove 'optional: true' (line 93) so the pod fails fast when soc-shared-secrets/opensearch-password is absent. For consistency, source INDEXER_USERNAME from the secret as well instead of hardcoding 'admin'.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. k8s/wazuh/manager/deployment.yaml lines 88-93: INDEXER_PASSWORD is sourced via secretKeyRef name=soc-shared-secrets key=opensearch-password with 'optional: true' (line 93). INDEXER_USERNAME is hardcoded 'admin' (line 87). With optional:true, a missing secret yields an empty/unset env var rather than a failed pod start, so the manager could come up with no indexer password. Cited line range matches exactly. Severity medium is reasonable (fail-open on a credential in a homelab; not internet-exposed and the secret is normally present, so not high).

confirmed.title: Zero automated tests for the three MCP servers, the shared lib, or the dashboard
confirmed.evidence: No *.test.ts/*.spec.ts, no vitest/jest config anywhere (find returned nothing). The shared fetchWithRetry/formatResponse logic, Zod input validation, OpenCTI GraphQL builders and TheHive query builders are entirely untested. test-flow.sh is an integration smoke test against a live cluster, not unit tests.
confirmed.recommendation: Add Vitest with a `test` script per package. Highest value: unit-test the pure shared functions (formatResponse truncation boundary, and fetchWithRetry retry/timeout behavior via a mocked global fetch and fake timers), and the three safeId regexes (accept/reject cases). For the dashboard, test the +server.ts handlers (api/wazuh, api/thehive, api/opencti, api/cortex, api/grafana, api/health) with a mocked fetch. Wire `npm test` into the package.json files and ideally into the GitHub workflow. Severity reduced from high to medium: real maintainability gap but no correctness defect demonstrated, small codebase with mostly pure, easily-testable functions, single-operator homelab context.
confirmed.verifyNote: Independently confirmed. find for *.test.*/*.spec.* returns nothing across the whole repo; no vitest/jest/mocha config files exist; grep for vitest|jest|mocha|@testing-library|playwright|"test" across all 5 package.json files (shared, wazuh-mcp, thehive-mcp, opencti-mcp, dashboard) returns nothing. shared/src/index.ts holds the untested core: fetchWithRetry (retry-on-429/503 + AbortController timeout, lines 18-46) and formatResponse (JSON truncation, lines 49-53), both imported and used by all three servers via @matchbox/mcp-shared. Untested Zod safeId regexes confirmed at thehive-mcp:33, opencti-mcp:31, wazuh-mcp:34. OpenCTI inline GraphQL query/mutation builders confirmed (opencti-mcp lines 77,113,162,197,231,257). TheHive _name/_field/_value query builders confirmed (thehive-mcp lines 74-77,196-197). test-flow.sh header states it is an end-to-end pipeline test that runs against a live k3s/Lima cluster, not unit tests. Adjusted file:line from :1 (file header comment) to :18 where the first untested function begins.

confirmed.title: No CI/CD: no GitHub Actions workflows, no lint/format config, no manifest validation
confirmed.evidence: .github has only issue/PR templates and SECURITY.md; there are no workflows. There is no ESLint/Prettier/Biome config, no `npm run lint`, no tsc --noEmit gate, no kubeconform/kubeval/helm lint, no container image scanning (Trivy), no svelte-check in CI. svelte-check is a devDependency but never invoked automatically.
confirmed.recommendation: Add a GitHub Actions workflow that: (1) installs and builds the three MCP packages + shared lib and the SvelteKit dashboard; (2) runs tsc --noEmit across MCP packages and svelte-check on the dashboard (the dep is already present, just never run); (3) validates k8s manifests with kubeconform and `helm template | kubeconform` for any Helm values; (4) adds a Trivy/Grype filesystem scan and, if images are built, an image scan. Optionally add a lightweight formatter/linter (Biome or ESLint+Prettier) with a `lint` script wired into the same workflow. Unit tests can follow once test scaffolding exists.
confirmed.verifyNote: Independently confirmed by reading the tree. .github/ contains only PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md, SECURITY.md, and ISSUE_TEMPLATE/{feature_request,bug_report,config}.yml — there is NO .github/workflows directory, so no GitHub Actions CI exists. A find for .eslintrc*/eslint.config.*/.prettierrc*/prettier.config.*/biome.json* (excluding node_modules) returned nothing — no lint/format config. dashboard/package.json scripts are only dev/build/preview/start (no lint, no check/svelte-check script); svelte-check@^4.0.0 IS a devDependency (line 20) but is never invoked by any script. All four mcp-servers packages (opencti/shared/thehive/wazuh) have only build/dev/start scripts — no lint, no test, no tsc --noEmit gate. No root package.json. grep for kubeconform/kubeval/helm lint/trivy/grype across tracked .sh/.md/.yaml/.yml files returned zero matches, confirming no k8s manifest validation or image scanning. The cited file:line is an arbitrary anchor for an absence-of-files finding but points to a real file in the .github dir, so it's acceptable. Severity lowered from high to medium: this is a CI/process-maturity gap (regression risk from unvalidated manifests, no type/lint gates, no image scanning) on a single-maintainer home-lab SOC project, not an active vulnerability or runtime defect.

confirmed.title: No developer-experience tooling: no Makefile/Taskfile, no devcontainer, no lint/format, no Node engines pin
confirmed.evidence: find shows no Makefile/Taskfile.yml/.devcontainer and no eslint/prettier/biome config. package.json files have no `engines` field and there is no .nvmrc, so Node version is unpinned across 4 TS packages. Common operations (build all MCP servers, run dashboard, deploy) are only documented as ad-hoc shell.
confirmed.recommendation: Add a Makefile or Taskfile.yml with build/test/lint/deploy targets wrapping the existing scripts (deploy-stack.sh, teardown.sh, test-flow.sh). Pin the Node version with an .nvmrc plus an `engines` field in each of the 5 package.json files. Add a shared eslint+prettier (or Biome) config at the repo root with lint/format npm scripts. A devcontainer is optional/nice-to-have for reproducible setup.
confirmed.verifyNote: Independently confirmed by filesystem inspection of /Users/donniebot/DonnieBot/SecurityCenter. No Makefile/Taskfile.yml/justfile exist outside node_modules. No .devcontainer directory. No .nvmrc. No eslint/prettier/biome config anywhere outside node_modules (the only hits are vendored dependency configs). All 5 non-vendored package.json files (dashboard, mcp-servers/shared, thehive-mcp, opencti-mcp, wazuh-mcp) lack an `engines` field and have no lint/format scripts. CLAUDE.md:61 is the '## Common Commands' header, which documents operations (deploy-stack.sh, teardown.sh, test-flow.sh, limactl) only as ad-hoc shell, accurately anchoring the evidence. Minor nuance: there are 5 package.json files, not 4 — the 'shared' package is an additional one beyond the stated '4 TS packages' (dashboard + 3 MCP servers); this does not change the substance. Severity 'medium' is appropriate: this is a maintainability/reproducibility (design/DX) gap on a homelab SOC project, not a correctness or security defect.

confirmed.title: Boilerplate duplicated across the three MCP servers (auth/connectivity/startup) instead of in the shared lib
confirmed.evidence: main() connectivity-test + StdioServerTransport startup is copy-pasted verbatim in wazuh-mcp:201-220, thehive-mcp:223-246, opencti-mcp:296-319. The safeId Zod regex is re-declared (with subtly different char classes) in each server. fetchWithRetry exists in shared but the per-server API wrappers and the manual AbortController connectivity check are not factored out.
confirmed.recommendation: Extract a startServer(name, healthCheck) helper and a single safeId factory into @matchbox/mcp-shared and import them in all three servers, eliminating the duplicated AbortController connectivity probe and StdioServerTransport startup. Critically, reconcile the three divergent safeId regexes to one shared definition — decide deliberately whether '.' and '~' are permitted (opencti currently rejects both while thehive/wazuh accept both) so IDs aren't silently rejected on one server and accepted on another.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading all three servers + shared. The main() connectivity-test + StdioServerTransport startup block is near-verbatim across wazuh-mcp:200-220, thehive-mcp:223-246, opencti-mcp:296-319 (each: manual AbortController with 5_000ms timeout, raw fetch — NOT fetchWithRetry — console.error connectivity log, then new StdioServerTransport()/server.connect()/running-on-stdio log, plus identical main().catch). The safeId Zod regex is re-declared in each server with genuinely divergent char classes: thehive-mcp:33 = /^[~a-zA-Z0-9_.-]+$/, wazuh-mcp:34 = /^[a-zA-Z0-9_.~-]+$/ (both allow '.' and '~'), opencti-mcp:31 = /^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$/ (allows NEITHER '.' nor '~'). That divergence is a latent functional inconsistency, not just cosmetic duplication. Per-server API wrappers (thehiveApi, wazuhApi, graphql) are also not factored out; only fetchWithRetry/formatResponse live in @matchbox/mcp-shared. The finding's evidence line ranges (wazuh 201-220, thehive 223-246, opencti 296-319) are accurate; the file:line in the header (224-241) under-covers the block, corrected to 223-246. Severity medium is justified because the regex divergence is a behavioral inconsistency, not pure style.

confirmed.title: MCP servers are committed with .env files but rely on plaintext credentials in .claude/settings.json env block
confirmed.evidence: .claude/settings.json registers all three servers with credential env keys (WAZUH_API_PASSWORD etc.) inline (currently empty). The intended workflow puts live secrets directly into a JSON settings file. There is no secret-injection indirection.
confirmed.recommendation: Rather than inlining secret values into .claude/settings.json, reference an external gitignored env file or a secret-manager retrieval command in the MCP launch config so live credentials are never written into the settings JSON. Drop the inaccurate framing that .env files are committed (only .env.example templates are, which is correct). Add a one-line doc note that .claude/ is gitignored but operators should still avoid pasting plaintext secrets at rest.
confirmed.verifyNote: Partially confirmed; the core design observation is real but the title overstates one claim. CONFIRMED: .claude/settings.json registers all three servers with credential env keys inline as plaintext values — WAZUH_API_PASSWORD (line 9), THEHIVE_API_KEY (line 17), OPENCTI_TOKEN (line 26) — all currently empty strings, with no secret-injection indirection (no ${VAR} expansion, no secret-manager command). Cited range 6-10 correctly covers the wazuh env block. REFUTED part of title: no live .env files are committed — git ls-files shows only .env.example templates are tracked (mcp-servers/{wazuh,thehive,opencti}-mcp/.env.example), and .gitignore lines 2-3 block .env/.env.* except !.env.example. Using .env.example templates is the documented best practice (CLAUDE.md: 'Use .env.example files to document required environment variables'), not a finding. CONFIRMED: .claude/ is gitignored (.gitignore line 33, verified via git check-ignore returning exit 0) and is not tracked. Severity downgraded from medium to low: there is no committed secret and no secret currently at rest (values empty); the residual issue is only that the launch pattern would have an operator paste plaintext into a JSON file, mitigated by the gitignore.

confirmed.title: Secrets managed as plain K8s Secrets / kubectl create with no GitOps-safe secret mechanism
confirmed.evidence: The whole stack relies on a hand-edited k8s/shared/secrets.yaml (gitignored) plus deploy-stack.sh:170-177 doing `kubectl create secret generic ... --from-literal` from shell env vars. There is no Sealed Secrets / SOPS / External Secrets Operator, so secrets cannot be safely version-controlled or rotated, and the opencti secret has two sources of truth (secrets.yaml.example AND the deploy script create).
confirmed.recommendation: Adopt SOPS-encrypted secrets (age key) or Sealed Secrets / External Secrets Operator so secret manifests can live in Git and be reconciled/rotated. As a prerequisite, pick a single owner for soc-opencti-secrets (resolve #2) and ensure all declarative secrets are actually applied (resolve #3). For a homelab, SOPS+age is the lightest-weight option.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed: the stack relies on a hand-edited, gitignored k8s/shared/secrets.yaml plus deploy-stack.sh:170-177 doing `kubectl create secret generic ... --from-literal` from shell env vars. No Sealed Secrets / SOPS / External Secrets Operator anywhere in the repo (grep finds none; only plain `kind: Secret` and existingSecret references). So secrets can't be version-controlled or rotated safely, and soc-opencti-secrets has two sources of truth (overlaps with #2). The factual claims hold. I am lowering severity from high to medium: this is a real design weakness, but for a single-node homelab k3s SOC (per CLAUDE.md) the absence of a GitOps secret operator is a defensible scope decision rather than a critical defect — and the concrete operational risks it produces are already captured as separate high findings (#1 missing soc-auth-secret, #3 secrets never applied). Listing the architectural recommendation at high would double-count those.

confirmed.title: Documentation drift: NetworkPolicy rule counts wrong (actual 31, docs say 23 and 18)
confirmed.evidence: grep -c 'kind: NetworkPolicy' network-policies.yaml = 31. CHANGELOG.md:92 claims '23 rules', CLAUDE.md:26 claims '18 explicit allow rules'. The file header comment (network-policies.yaml:1-8) also predates the egress rules it now contains.
confirmed.recommendation: Reconcile the NetworkPolicy counts: actual is 31 objects. Update CLAUDE.md:26 and CHANGELOG.md:92 to the real number, or stop hardcoding a count. Do NOT touch the network-policies.yaml header on account of egress wording — it already documents egress (lines 5-7).
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading the files. grep -c 'kind: NetworkPolicy' k8s/network-policies.yaml = 31 (each as a distinct object). CLAUDE.md:26 states 'default-deny ingress + 18 explicit allow rules across 5 namespaces'. CHANGELOG.md:92 states 'NetworkPolicies — 23 rules across 5 namespaces'. CHANGELOG.md:15 separately says '10 new policies'. None match the actual 31, and the two doc numbers (18, 23) disagree with each other. ONE part of the evidence is wrong, though: the claim that the file header (network-policies.yaml:1-8) 'predates the egress rules' is FALSE. I read lines 1-8: line 5 says 'Default-deny ingress AND egress per namespace', and lines 6-7 explicitly describe the egress DNS/HTTPS rules. The header has already been updated for egress (consistent with CHANGELOG.md:23). So the header-refresh part of the recommendation is moot.

confirmed.title: Compliance claims ('audited against ISO 27001 / NIST 800-53 / OWASP LLM Top 10') are unsubstantiated
confirmed.evidence: CHANGELOG.md:101 and CLAUDE.md:30 assert formal audits against ISO 27001:2022, NIST 800-53 Rev 5 and OWASP LLM Top 10, but there is no mapping doc, control matrix, or evidence artifact in docs/. Several controls cited (cert-manager TLS, NetworkPolicies) are not actually wired by the deploy path (see ingress finding).
confirmed.recommendation: Soften 'Audited against' to 'Designed with reference to' in CHANGELOG.md:101 and CLAUDE.md:30, OR add a real control-mapping doc under docs/ (e.g. docs/compliance-mapping.md) with per-control status and evidence pointers. The inline control tags can seed that matrix.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. CHANGELOG.md:101 states 'Audited against ISO 27001:2022, NIST 800-53 Rev 5, and OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025' and CLAUDE.md:30 says 'Compliance audited against ISO 27001, NIST 800-53, and OWASP Top 10 for LLMs'. I enumerated docs/ (architecture.md, network-diagram.md, resource-requirements.md, integrations.md, mcp-servers.md, runbooks/*) — there is no control-mapping document, control matrix, or audit evidence artifact. The only structured control references are inline tags like 'ISO 27001 A.8.3/A.8.20, NIST AC-4/SC-7' in CHANGELOG.md:92 and the network-policies.yaml:3 header comment; these are control citations, not an audit. 'Audited' overclaims for a self-described homelab. Note: the finding's parenthetical that 'cert-manager TLS, NetworkPolicies are not actually wired by the deploy path' is out of scope for the docs unit and unverified here. Severity stays low: this is a documentation-cleanliness/overclaim issue, not an exploitable defect.

confirmed.title: MCP servers emit only ad-hoc console.error; no structured logging, metrics, or tracing
confirmed.evidence: All three servers log via console.error strings (e.g. 'Retrying ... after 503'); there is no structured (JSON) logging, no request counters/latency metrics, and no correlation IDs. For a security tool that performs WRITE mutations (create-case, run-analyzer, enrich-observable), there is no audit log of who/what invoked them.
confirmed.recommendation: Add a small structured logger to the shared lib (or even a dependency-free JSON-to-stderr helper, to avoid adding deps to a stdio MCP). Log each tool invocation with tool name, an args summary scrubbed of secrets, and the outcome (success/error + status). Prioritize the WRITE tools (create-case, add-observable, run-analyzer, enrich-observable) so there is an audit record of mutations. Metrics/tracing are optional given the homelab scale; the audit log is the real win.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading all four source files. The only logging anywhere is console.error: shared/src/index.ts:29 and :36 (retries); each server's startup/connectivity/fatal handlers (e.g. thehive-mcp/src/index.ts:28,233,235,240,244; opencti-mcp 26,306,308,313,317; wazuh-mcp 28,207,209,214,218). No structured (JSON) logging, no counters/latency metrics, no correlation IDs, and no logger/metrics deps in any package.json (shared has empty dependencies; the three servers depend only on @modelcontextprotocol/sdk, zod, and the shared lib). Critically, the WRITE/mutation handlers run with zero logging: create-case (thehive index.ts:86-106), add-observable (:134-155), run-analyzer (:159-172), and opencti enrich-observable (index.ts:223) all POST to their APIs and return without recording who/what invoked them or the outcome — no audit trail. The cited line 29 is a real example but I re-pointed file:line to the create-case WRITE handler since the audit-trail gap on mutations is the load-bearing concern. Severity medium is appropriate for a security tool that performs state-changing mutations without an audit log on a single-operator homelab.

confirmed.title: No alerting rules shipped with the Prometheus stack
confirmed.evidence: The monitoring stack ships a Grafana dashboard JSON but the repo contains no PrometheusRule/alerting rules (e.g. pod restart/OOM, OpenSearch yellow/red, Wazuh manager down, connector CronJob failures). For a SOC running on a tight 16GB budget where OOM is a real risk, the absence of alerting is a notable gap.
confirmed.recommendation: Two concrete gaps to close: (1) Add an Alertmanager receiver+route in kube-prometheus-values.yaml (e.g. a webhook/Slack) so the already-enabled defaultRules actually notify; without this, alerting is effectively silent. (2) Add a custom PrometheusRule manifest for SOC-specific signals not covered by chart defaults: OpenSearch cluster status yellow/red, Wazuh manager pod down, and failures of the three connector CronJobs. Memory-pressure/OOM/eviction is largely covered by the chart's node/kubernetesSystem default rules, so verify those fire before adding duplicates.
confirmed.verifyNote: Partially confirmed; the finding's framing is overstated. There are NO custom PrometheusRule manifests in the repo (grep for 'kind: PrometheusRule' across k8s/ returns none), and no Alertmanager receiver/route config in the values file (grep for receivers/route/slack/webhook/email/pagerduty returns nothing), so even default alerts fire into the chart's null receiver and notify no one. HOWEVER the claim 'the repo contains no PrometheusRule/alerting rules' and 'absence of alerting' is inaccurate: defaultRules.create is set to true (kube-prometheus-values.yaml:139-151) which makes the kube-prometheus-stack chart deploy its bundled general/kubernetesSystem/node PrometheusRules — these DO include node memory-pressure/OOM and KubeJobFailed-style alerts — and Alertmanager IS deployed (alertmanager block at lines 101-117). So generic cluster/pod/node alerting exists; what is genuinely missing is (a) SOC-specific custom rules (OpenSearch cluster yellow/red, Wazuh manager down, and the 3 OpenCTI connector CronJobs at k8s/opencti/connectors/{mitre-attack,abuseipdb,alienvault-otx}.yaml have no failure alert) and (b) any Alertmanager routing/receiver so alerts actually reach someone. The w2thive.py 'alert' matches are Wazuh->TheHive security-alert forwarding, unrelated to Prometheus alerting. Severity low is correct.

confirmed.title: Inconsistent pod-level securityContext hardening across workloads
confirmed.evidence: OpenCTI deployment.yaml:39-50 has runAsNonRoot + drop ALL caps, but Cortex deployment.yaml has only allowPrivilegeEscalation:false (no runAsNonRoot, no cap drop, comment notes it runs su internally), and Wazuh Manager deployment.yaml:67-68 likewise only sets allowPrivilegeEscalation:false. No workload sets seccompProfile: RuntimeDefault. Hardening is applied unevenly.
confirmed.recommendation: Standardize a baseline securityContext (seccompProfile: RuntimeDefault everywhere; drop ALL capabilities and runAsNonRoot where the image allows). Document the deliberate exceptions inline (Cortex uses su internally; Wazuh agent needs privileged FIM). Note the same caveat as finding 1: for Helm-deployed components the hardening must be set in the helm-values files, not just the unused raw deployment.yaml.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. OpenCTI deployment.yaml has full hardening: pod-level runAsNonRoot/runAsUser/fsGroup (lines 40-42, 184-186) and container-level capabilities drop ALL (lines 49-50, 193-194). Cortex deployment.yaml container securityContext (lines 46-47) sets ONLY allowPrivilegeEscalation:false, with a comment (lines 40-42) explaining pod runAsUser was removed because the image manages user context via su.
