Wazuh manager deployment.yaml securityContext (lines 67-68) likewise sets ONLY allowPrivilegeEscalation:false. grep for seccompProfile/RuntimeDefault across k8s/ returns nothing -- no workload sets it. Only opencti, thehive, and shared/rabbitmq-deployment use runAsNonRoot. Hardening is genuinely uneven. Minor correction: the cited line range '38-47' for Cortex points at the pod spec start; the actual securityContext is at lines 46-47. Low severity appropriate.

confirmed.title: Orphaned duplicate manifests increase confusion (rabbitmq-deployment.yaml, raw redis/opensearch raw variants)
confirmed.evidence: shared/rabbitmq.yaml is a Helm values file ('Used by OpenCTI...helm install') while shared/rabbitmq-deployment.yaml is a raw 'replaces the Bitnami chart' Deployment; deploy-stack.sh:97 uses the Bitnami chart, so rabbitmq-deployment.yaml is dead. Same pattern for redis. These contradict each other.
confirmed.recommendation: Delete the orphaned k8s/shared/rabbitmq-deployment.yaml (it is never applied, references a nonexistent rabbitmq-config configmap, and contradicts the Bitnami-chart path used by deploy-stack.sh). Drop the 'redis raw variant' part of this finding -- no such file exists; only redis.yaml (Helm values) is present. Keep exactly one rabbitmq manifest matching the chosen deployment strategy.
confirmed.verifyNote: PARTIALLY CONFIRMED. rabbitmq.yaml header (line 1-5) says 'RabbitMQ Helm Values ... Chart: bitnami/rabbitmq' while rabbitmq-deployment.yaml:1 says 'Official image deployment (replaces Bitnami chart)' and is a raw StatefulSet using rabbitmq:4.1-management-alpine. deploy-stack.sh:97 installs bitnami/rabbitmq, and grep confirms rabbitmq-deployment.yaml is applied NOWHERE in scripts/ or k8s/ -> it is dead/orphaned. So the core rabbitmq duplication claim is REAL. However, the 'Same pattern for redis' claim is REFUTED: ls of k8s/shared/ shows only redis.yaml (a Helm values file); there is NO raw redis deployment variant. The title's parenthetical 'raw redis/opensearch raw variants' is inaccurate -- there is no raw redis or raw opensearch variant in shared/. Additional dead-reference note: rabbitmq-deployment.yaml mounts a configMap named rabbitmq-config (line 102-103) that does not exist anywhere in the repo, so even if applied it would be broken. Severity low is appropriate (cleanliness, no runtime impact since the file is never applied).

confirmed.title: Reliance on Bitnami charts (redis, rabbitmq) which became paywalled/legacy in 2025
confirmed.evidence: deploy_shared() installs redis and rabbitmq from bitnami/redis and bitnami/rabbitmq. As of Aug 2025 Bitnami moved maintained images to a subscription and froze the free catalog under 'bitnamilegacy' with only latest tags; unpinned Bitnami chart installs now pull images that may disappear or stop receiving security updates.
confirmed.recommendation: Migrate Redis and RabbitMQ off unmaintained Bitnami charts: either adopt official images via raw manifests (the repo already has rabbitmq-deployment.yaml with rabbitmq:4.1-management-alpine; add an equivalent redis using the official redis image) or switch to a maintained chart. In all cases pin image tags AND digests, and pin the Helm chart --version. If staying on Bitnami short-term, pin to a known-good chart version and an image from the bitnamilegacy path explicitly.
confirmed.verifyNote: CONFIRMED. deploy_shared() installs Redis from bitnami/redis (deploy-stack.sh:92-94) and RabbitMQ from bitnami/rabbitmq (lines 96-99). Neither helm command pins a chart version (no --version flag) and neither values file (redis.yaml / rabbitmq.yaml) pins image.tag, so installs float to whatever the chart pulls. The external premise is accurate per my knowledge: in Aug 2025 Bitnami moved maintained free images to a subscription (Bitnami Secure Images) and froze the legacy free catalog under the bitnamilegacy repo with limited tags, so unpinned bitnami chart installs are at risk of pulling images that disappear or stop receiving security updates. The recommendation's reference is correct: the repo's own k8s/shared/rabbitmq-deployment.yaml uses the official rabbitmq:4.1-management-alpine image (verified line 53). Severity medium is appropriate -- it is a supply-chain/maintainability risk, not an immediate functional break.

confirmed.title: Module-scoped JWT/token cache in SvelteKit endpoints is shared across all requests/users
confirmed.evidence: jwtToken/tokenExpiry are module-level mutable state in +server.ts. In adapter-node this is process-global, so the Wazuh JWT is cached and shared across every dashboard visitor; fine for a single-admin home lab but a latent multi-tenant/security smell, and there is no concurrency guard so a token refresh can be triggered by N parallel requests.
confirmed.recommendation: Acceptable for single-user; add a code comment documenting the single-shared-service-credential assumption. If multi-user is ever intended, move token handling to a per-credential cache and add an in-flight refresh lock (e.g. cache the in-progress getToken() promise) so concurrent cold requests don't trigger redundant authenticate calls.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed: src/routes/api/wazuh/+server.ts lines 9-11 declare `let jwtToken: string | null = null; let tokenExpiry = 0; const TOKEN_TTL_MS = 850_000;` at module scope. In adapter-node this is process-global, so the single Wazuh service-account JWT is cached and reused across all dashboard visitors. getToken() (lines 13-28) has no in-flight/concurrency lock, so N parallel requests on a cold/expired cache each fire their own authenticate call — accurate. Note the cache holds a single shared service credential (WAZUH_API_USER/wazuh-wui), not per-user tokens, so this is genuinely benign for the single-admin home-lab design and is a latent smell only if multi-user is ever added. Low severity is correct.

confirmed.title: setup-lima.sh rewrites kubeconfig 0.0.0.0->127.0.0.1 only on macOS sed, and provision writes 0.0.0.0 into kube config
confirmed.evidence: The provision script sed-replaces 127.0.0.1->0.0.0.0 in the guest kube config (line 106), and setup-lima.sh:63 reverses it with BSD `sed -i ''`. The manual-copy fallback at setup-lima.sh:69-71 does a no-op sed (127.0.0.1->127.0.0.1). This is brittle and confusing; binding kube API to 0.0.0.0 in-guest is also an unnecessary exposure on the VM network.
confirmed.recommendation: Drop the 0.0.0.0 rewrite at lima/k3s-soc.yaml:106 and keep the kubeconfig server as 127.0.0.1, relying on the Lima port-forward; remove the no-op fallback sed at setup-lima.sh:70. Optionally use a portable sed invocation (e.g. perl -i or a tmp-file rewrite) instead of BSD-only 'sed -i \'\''.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed exactly. lima/k3s-soc.yaml:106 runs 'sed -i s/127.0.0.1/0.0.0.0/g' on the guest /home/USER/.kube/config (with the comment 'so it works from macOS via port forward'). setup-lima.sh:63 reverses it with BSD-style 'sed -i \'\' s/0.0.0.0/127.0.0.1/g' (macOS-specific empty-arg form). The manual-copy fallback at setup-lima.sh:70 is a genuine no-op: 'sed -i \'\' s/127.0.0.1/127.0.0.1/g' replaces 127.0.0.1 with itself. Note the copyToHost block (lima/k3s-soc.yaml:120-122) copies /etc/rancher/k3s/k3s.yaml — which still contains 127.0.0.1, not the rewritten ~/.kube/config — so the primary KUBECONFIG path that deploy-stack/setup-lima consume actually never has 0.0.0.0 in it; the 0.0.0.0 rewrite only affects the in-guest ~/.kube/config and the setup-lima:63 sed is largely operating on an already-127.0.0.1 file. This makes the logic even more confused than described, but impact is low/cosmetic. Severity low confirmed.

confirmed.title: deploy script seeds threat-intel API keys with literal 'placeholder', silently producing broken connectors
confirmed.evidence: When OTX/AbuseIPDB/VT keys are unset, the secret is created with --from-literal=...='placeholder' and only a WARNING is printed. The CronJob connectors (abuseipdb.yaml etc.) will then run daily and fail auth against the feeds, generating recurring failed Jobs.
confirmed.recommendation: When a connector's API key is absent, skip deploying that connector (or apply it with the CronJob suspended / the Deployment scaled to 0) instead of seeding 'placeholder'. At minimum, gate the per-connector `kubectl apply` on the presence of its key so unconfigured feeds don't crash-loop or generate recurring failed Jobs. Note virustotal is a Deployment, so 'suspend the CronJob' applies only to abuseipdb and alienvault-otx; scale the VT Deployment to 0 instead.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed. deploy-stack.sh:174-176 create soc-opencti-secrets with alienvault-api-key/abuseipdb-api-key/virustotal-api-key defaulting to 'placeholder' when the env vars are unset, and lines 178-181 only print a WARNING (the script does not skip or suspend the connectors). The connectors are then applied unconditionally (line 189: kubectl apply -f connectors/). One correction to the evidence: abuseipdb.yaml and alienvault-otx.yaml are CronJobs (schedules '0 4 * * *' and '0 2 * * *'), so they will indeed produce recurring failed daily Jobs as claimed; however virustotal.yaml is a long-running Deployment (kind: Deployment), not a CronJob, so it would crash-loop / fail auth continuously rather than generate daily Jobs. mitre-attack is a weekly CronJob that needs no API key (control case). The 'etc.' in the finding overstates that all are CronJobs, but the core defect holds. Low severity is appropriate: this is a deploy-time convenience behind an explicit WARNING that names the exact env vars to set, and the connectors are on-demand/scheduled rather than always-on.

confirmed.title: fetchWithRetry loop can fall through to a misleading 'failed after retries' error
confirmed.evidence: The for-loop runs attempts 0 and 1; on attempt 1 it always returns or throws, so the trailing `throw new Error('failed after retries')` (line 45) is effectively unreachable, and a non-retryable error on attempt 0 returns immediately (correct) — but the dead final throw obscures intent and would hide the real status if the loop bounds ever change.
confirmed.recommendation: Keep line 45 (it satisfies TypeScript's return-type control-flow check) but add a comment marking it unreachable / TS-fallback, and add a unit test pinning the retry semantics: (1) 429 then 200 returns the 200, (2) two consecutive 503s returns the second 503 response (it does NOT throw 'failed after retries'), (3) a non-retryable 404 on attempt 0 returns immediately, (4) timeout then success retries once.
confirmed.verifyNote: Confirmed by reading shared/src/index.ts:18-46. The for-loop runs attempts 0 and 1. The retryable-status continue (line 28) and the AbortError-retry continue (line 35) are both guarded by `attempt === 0`, so on attempt 1 the code always reaches either `return resp` (line 33) or `throw err` (line 40). Therefore the trailing `throw new Error('failed after retries')` at line 45 is genuinely unreachable at runtime, and a non-retryable result on attempt 0 returns/throws immediately as the finding states. nit severity is correct — it is dead at runtime and harmless. Worth adding for accuracy: line 45 is NOT pure clutter; it is required to satisfy TypeScript's control-flow analysis for the Promise<Response> return type (removing it would cause a 'not all code paths return a value' error), so it should be kept-with-a-comment rather than deleted.
